syllabus_desc: State the International System (SI) unit used to express length.
concept: The SI system is built on a handful of base units that everything else derives from. For length it's the metre (m), for mass it's the kilogram (kg), for time it's the second (s), and for temperature you've got both kelvin (K) and degrees Celsius (C). Kelvin and Celsius use the same size degree -- the only difference is where zero sits. Zero kelvin is absolute zero (-273.15C), the coldest anything can theoretically get. Zero Celsius is the freezing point of water. In aviation, you'll mostly use Celsius for weather and performance calculations, but kelvin shows up in gas law problems and ISA calculations.
exam_tip: To convert Celsius to kelvin, add 273. To convert kelvin to Celsius, subtract 273. The exam loves testing this simple conversion.
key_facts: Length: metre (m)
Mass: kilogram (kg) -- note it's the kilogram, not the gram, that's the SI base unit
Time: second (s)
Temperature: kelvin (K) and degrees Celsius (C); K = C + 273
Kelvin and Celsius degrees are the same size; only the zero point differs

syllabus_desc: State the International System (SI) unit used to express time.
concept: Once you've got the base SI units, you can derive everything else. Force is measured in newtons (N) -- one newton is the force needed to accelerate one kilogram at one metre per second squared. Pressure is force per unit area, measured in pascals (Pa) -- one pascal is one newton per square metre. In aviation, we commonly use hectopascals (hPa), which are identical to millibars. Power is the rate of doing work, measured in watts (W) -- one watt is one joule of work per second. Non-SI units you'll encounter include pounds of force, pounds per square inch (psi), inches of mercury (inHg), and horsepower for engine output.
exam_tip: Remember the derivations: N = kg x m/s squared, Pa = N/m squared, W = J/s. Exam questions test whether you understand how these units relate to each other.
key_facts: Force: newton (N) = kg x m/s squared
Pressure: pascal (Pa) = N/m squared; 1 hPa = 1 millibar = 100 Pa
Power: watt (W) = joule/second; 1 horsepower = 746 watts approximately
Work/energy: joule (J) = N x m
Non-SI units include psi, inHg, pounds-force, and horsepower

syllabus_desc: State the International System (SI) unit used to express mass.
concept: Here's where aviation gets charmingly old-fashioned. Despite the SI system being the scientific standard, pilots worldwide measure altitude in feet, distance in nautical miles, speed in knots, and engine power in horsepower. A foot is 0.3048 metres. A nautical mile is 1,852 metres -- it's based on one minute of arc of latitude, which is why it's so useful for navigation. A knot is one nautical mile per hour. And horsepower, dating back to James Watt comparing steam engines to horses, equals 746 watts. You'll use these units every single flight, so they need to be second nature.
exam_tip: Know that 1 NM = 1,852 m, 1 ft = 0.3048 m, 1 kt = 1 NM/hr, and 1 HP = 746 W. These conversions appear regularly in exam calculations.
key_facts: 1 foot = 0.3048 metres (altitude measurement worldwide)
1 nautical mile (NM) = 1,852 metres = 1 minute of latitude
1 knot (kt) = 1 nautical mile per hour = 1.852 km/h
1 horsepower (HP) = 746 watts approximately
These non-SI units persist in aviation due to international convention and practical utility

syllabus_desc: State the International System (SI) unit used to express temperature (K and degC).
concept: Temperature is measured in degrees Celsius (°C) for everyday use and in Kelvin (K) in scientific calculations. The SI base unit for thermodynamic temperature is the Kelvin. The Kelvin scale starts at absolute zero (−273.15°C), where all molecular motion stops. Kelvin and Celsius are related by: K = °C + 273.15. In aviation performance calculations, temperatures are often used in Kelvin for gas law calculations (e.g., density calculations).
exam_tip: Temperature SI unit: Kelvin (K). Conversion: K = °C + 273.15. Kelvin starts at absolute zero. Know both units and how to convert.
key_facts: SI unit for temperature: Kelvin (K)
Everyday aviation: degrees Celsius (°C)
Conversion: K = °C + 273.15
Absolute zero: 0 K = −273.15°C (all molecular motion stops)
Kelvin used in gas law and density calculations

syllabus_desc: State the derivation of the SI unit for force.
concept: When you add vectors, you can't just add the numbers like you would with scalars. If two forces of 3 N and 4 N act on an object, the resultant could be anything from 1 N to 7 N depending on their directions. Vector addition uses the triangle method or parallelogram method: place the vectors head-to-tail (or side-by-side from a common point), and the resultant is the closing side. Vector subtraction is the same as adding a negative vector -- you reverse the direction of the vector being subtracted, then add normally. In the cockpit, you're doing vector addition every time you calculate a wind correction angle -- your heading vector plus the wind vector gives your track vector.
exam_tip: For vectors at right angles, use Pythagoras: resultant = square root of (A squared + B squared). For the angle, use trigonometry. Draw it out -- diagrams save time in the exam.
key_facts: Triangle method: place vectors head-to-tail; resultant closes the triangle
Parallelogram method: place vectors from common point; resultant is the diagonal
Same-direction vectors: resultant = sum of magnitudes
Opposite-direction vectors: resultant = difference of magnitudes
Right-angle vectors: use Pythagoras' theorem to find the resultant magnitude

syllabus_desc: State the derivation of the SI unit for pressure.
concept: Pressure is force spread over an area, so its SI unit comes straight from those two quantities. Force is measured in newtons (N) and area in square metres (m²). Divide one by the other and you get newtons per square metre (N/m²) -- which has its own name: the pascal (Pa). One pascal is one newton acting over one square metre. That's a tiny amount of pressure in everyday terms, so in aviation you'll mostly see hectopascals (hPa) -- one hPa equals 100 Pa. Standard sea-level atmospheric pressure is 1013.25 hPa. The derivation matters because it shows you that pressure changes whenever either the force or the area changes -- push harder on the same area and pressure goes up, spread the same force over a bigger area and pressure drops. That relationship underpins everything from altimeter settings to how a rotor blade generates lift.
exam_tip: Pressure = Force / Area. The SI unit is the pascal (Pa) = 1 N/m². Aviation uses hectopascals (hPa) where 1 hPa = 100 Pa. Standard atmosphere = 1013.25 hPa. Know the derivation cold.
key_facts: Pressure = Force / Area
SI unit is the pascal (Pa) = 1 N/m²
Named after Blaise Pascal
1 hectopascal (hPa) = 100 Pa
Standard sea-level pressure = 1013.25 hPa

syllabus_desc: State the derivation of the SI unit for power.
concept: Power is the rate of doing work: Power = Work ÷ Time. The SI unit of power is the Watt (W), which equals 1 Joule per second (J/s). Since Work = Force × Distance, and Distance/Time = Velocity, power can also be expressed as Power = Force × Velocity. This is why engine power in a helicopter directly determines the thrust available at a given airspeed. One kilowatt (kW) = 1,000 W.
exam_tip: Power = Work ÷ Time = Force × Velocity. SI unit: Watt (W) = 1 J/s. One kW = 1,000 W.
key_facts: Power SI unit: Watt (W)
1 W = 1 J/s = 1 N·m/s
Power = Work ÷ Time = Force × Velocity
Engine power determines available thrust at any given airspeed

syllabus_desc: State the derivation of the non-SI unit for length — foot and nautical mile.
concept: The foot is a non-SI unit of length still widely used in aviation. 1 foot = 0.3048 metres exactly. Altitudes, terrain heights, and cloud bases are reported in feet in NZ aviation. The nautical mile is derived from the Earth's geometry — 1 nautical mile (nm) = 1 minute of arc of latitude at the Earth's surface, or approximately 1,852 metres. Using nautical miles for navigation is convenient because 1 nm corresponds to 1 minute of latitude on a chart.
exam_tip: 1 foot = 0.3048 m. 1 nautical mile = 1,852 m = 1 minute of latitude arc. These are the aviation length units you'll use every day.
key_facts: Foot: 1 ft = 0.3048 m
Nautical mile: 1 nm = 1,852 m
1 nm = 1 minute of arc of latitude
Navigation is in nautical miles — charts use latitude/longitude
Altitude in feet; distance in nautical miles for NZ aviation

syllabus_desc: State the derivation of the non-SI unit for speed — knot.
concept: The knot is a non-SI unit of speed used in aviation and maritime navigation. 1 knot = 1 nautical mile per hour (nm/h) = 1.852 km/h = approximately 0.514 m/s. It is derived from the nautical mile — which itself is derived from the Earth's latitude scale. Using knots keeps airspeed, groundspeed, and wind consistent with navigation distances in nautical miles.
exam_tip: 1 knot = 1 nm per hour = 1.852 km/h. Aviation uses knots for airspeed, wind speed, and groundspeed — consistent with nautical mile navigation.
key_facts: 1 knot = 1 nautical mile per hour
1 knot = 1.852 km/h
1 knot ≈ 0.514 m/s
Derived from the nautical mile (1 minute of arc of latitude)
Airspeed, groundspeed, and wind all expressed in knots in NZ aviation

syllabus_desc: State the derivation of the non-SI unit for work — horsepower.
concept: Horsepower (hp) is a non-SI unit of power still commonly used for piston aircraft engine ratings. 1 horsepower = 745.7 watts (W) = 0.7457 kW. The unit was historically defined as the power needed to lift 550 pound-force one foot per second (550 ft·lbf/s). Metric horsepower (PS) is slightly different: 1 PS = 735.5 W. For helicopter performance calculations, engine power in horsepower is used in charts and tables throughout the AFM.
exam_tip: 1 hp = 745.7 W. Derived from 550 ft·lbf/s. Used for piston engine ratings. Metric hp (PS) = 735.5 W — slightly different. Know both conversions.
key_facts: 1 horsepower (hp) = 745.7 W = 0.7457 kW
Derived from: 550 ft·lbf/s
Non-SI unit still used for piston aircraft engine ratings
Metric PS = 735.5 W — slightly less than imperial hp
Performance charts often use horsepower — convert to kW if needed

syllabus_desc: Differentiate between scalar and vector quantities.
concept: A scalar quantity has magnitude only — no direction. Examples: temperature (25°C), mass (500 kg), speed (100 knots), energy (500 J). A vector quantity has both magnitude and direction. Examples: velocity (100 knots north), force (500 N upward), displacement (50 nm bearing 045°). In helicopter aerodynamics, most forces are vectors — lift acts perpendicular to relative airflow, drag acts parallel to it, weight acts vertically downward. Understanding vectors is essential for resolving forces in the rotor system.
exam_tip: Scalar: magnitude only (speed, temperature, mass). Vector: magnitude + direction (velocity, force, weight). Forces are always vectors.
key_facts: Scalar: magnitude only — no direction
Examples: speed, temperature, pressure, energy, mass
Vector: magnitude and direction
Examples: velocity, force, weight, displacement, thrust
In helicopter aerodynamics: lift, drag, thrust, and weight are all vectors

syllabus_desc: Explain vector addition and subtraction.
concept: Vector addition combines two or more vectors by considering both their magnitudes and directions. Graphically, this is done by placing vectors head-to-tail — the resultant is drawn from the tail of the first to the head of the last. The parallelogram law is an alternative: draw both vectors from the same point and construct a parallelogram — the resultant is the diagonal. Vector subtraction: to subtract vector B from A, reverse the direction of B and add it to A. In helicopter flight, vectors are routinely added (wind + airspeed = groundspeed velocity vector).
exam_tip: Vector addition: head-to-tail or parallelogram method — find the resultant. Vector subtraction: reverse direction of the subtracted vector, then add. Used for wind triangles and force diagrams.
key_facts: Head-to-tail method: place vectors end-to-end, resultant from start to finish
Parallelogram method: both vectors from same point, resultant is the diagonal
Vector subtraction: reverse one vector's direction then add
Resultant: single equivalent vector that replaces all component vectors
Wind triangle: TAS vector + wind vector = groundspeed vector

syllabus_desc: Resolve a single vector into two components at right angles.
concept: Trigonometry is your friend in aerodynamics, even if it didn't feel that way at school. For a right-angled triangle, the three trig functions relate the angles to the sides. Sine of an angle = opposite side divided by the hypotenuse. Cosine = adjacent side divided by the hypotenuse. Tangent = opposite divided by adjacent (or equivalently, sine divided by cosine). The mnemonic SOH-CAH-TOA makes this easy to remember. You'll use these constantly -- resolving weight into components in a climb, finding the horizontal component of lift in a turn, calculating bank angles, and working with forces along and perpendicular to the flight path.
exam_tip: SOH-CAH-TOA: Sine = Opposite/Hypotenuse, Cosine = Adjacent/Hypotenuse, Tangent = Opposite/Adjacent. Know that sin 30 = 0.5, cos 60 = 0.5, sin 45 = cos 45 = 0.707.
key_facts: sin(angle) = opposite / hypotenuse
cos(angle) = adjacent / hypotenuse
tan(angle) = opposite / adjacent = sin / cos
SOH-CAH-TOA is the standard mnemonic
Common values: sin 30 = 0.5, cos 30 = 0.866, sin 45 = cos 45 = 0.707, sin 60 = 0.866, cos 60 = 0.5

syllabus_desc: Describe Newton's three laws of motion.
concept: A moment is the turning effect of a force about a point. It's calculated as force multiplied by the perpendicular distance from the line of action of the force to the pivot point: Moment = Force x Distance. The units are newton-metres (Nm). A longer arm or a bigger force means a bigger moment -- that's why a long spanner makes it easier to undo a tight bolt, and why the tailplane, positioned well aft of the CG, can produce large pitching moments with relatively small forces. A couple is a special case: two equal and opposite parallel forces that aren't in line with each other. A couple produces pure rotation with no translation. The moment of a couple equals one force multiplied by the distance between them.
exam_tip: Moment = Force x perpendicular distance. A couple = two equal, opposite, parallel forces producing pure rotation. The tailplane-wing pitch interaction is a classic couple example.
key_facts: Moment = Force x perpendicular distance from the pivot (Nm)
A moment produces rotation about a point
A couple consists of two equal and opposite parallel forces separated by a distance
Moment of a couple = one force x distance between the forces
A couple produces pure rotation with no net translational force

syllabus_desc: Explain the meaning of speed.
concept: Speed is a scalar quantity — the rate of change of distance with time, regardless of direction. Speed = Distance ÷ Time. In aviation, speed is typically expressed in knots (nm/h). Airspeed is the speed of the aircraft relative to the surrounding air mass. Groundspeed is the speed of the aircraft relative to the ground. Wind speed is the speed of the air mass relative to the ground. Speed has no direction component.
exam_tip: Speed = distance ÷ time. Scalar (no direction). Airspeed = speed relative to air. Groundspeed = speed relative to ground. Both are speed, not velocity.
key_facts: Speed: scalar quantity — magnitude only
Speed = distance ÷ time
Aviation unit: knots (nm/hour)
Airspeed: speed relative to air mass
Groundspeed: speed relative to ground

syllabus_desc: Explain the meaning of velocity.
concept: Velocity is a vector quantity — it is the rate of change of displacement with time, combining both speed (magnitude) and direction. Velocity = Displacement ÷ Time. In aviation, groundspeed (100 knots north) is velocity; airspeed alone (100 knots) is speed. When the wind is added vectorially to true airspeed, the result is groundspeed velocity. Forces acting on an aircraft produce changes in velocity (acceleration), not just speed.
exam_tip: Velocity = speed + direction. It's a vector. Groundspeed heading is a velocity; airspeed alone is a speed. The wind triangle solves for velocity vectors.
key_facts: Velocity: vector quantity — magnitude and direction
Velocity = displacement ÷ time
Speed (scalar) + direction = velocity (vector)
Groundspeed vector = TAS vector + wind vector
Forces produce changes in velocity (acceleration)

syllabus_desc: Explain the meaning of acceleration.
concept: Acceleration is the rate of change of velocity with time — it can involve a change in speed, a change in direction, or both. Acceleration = Change in velocity ÷ Time. In Newton's second law: Force = Mass × Acceleration (F = ma). A helicopter in a turn is accelerating (changing direction) even if speed is constant — this is centripetal acceleration, directed toward the centre of the turn. Deceleration is negative acceleration.
exam_tip: Acceleration = rate of change of velocity. A turn is acceleration (direction change). F = ma. Centripetal acceleration in a turn acts toward the centre.
key_facts: Acceleration: rate of change of velocity (speed or direction)
Acceleration = ΔV ÷ time
F = ma: force causes acceleration proportional to mass
Turning at constant speed: centripetal acceleration (direction changes)
Deceleration: negative acceleration

syllabus_desc: Explain the meaning of equilibrium.
concept: Equilibrium is the condition where all forces acting on a body are balanced — the net force is zero and the body remains in a constant state of motion (or rest). Translational equilibrium: sum of all forces = zero (no change in linear motion). Rotational equilibrium: sum of all torques (moments) = zero (no change in rotation). A helicopter in straight and level flight is in both translational and rotational equilibrium — thrust = drag, lift = weight, and all moments are balanced.
exam_tip: Equilibrium: net force = zero. Translational = no change in linear motion; rotational = no change in rotation. Straight and level flight = both.
key_facts: Equilibrium: no net force, no net torque
Translational equilibrium: ΣF = 0 (constant velocity)
Rotational equilibrium: Στ = 0 (constant rotation rate)
Straight and level flight: lift = weight, thrust = drag, moments balanced
Disturbing one balance creates acceleration in that axis

syllabus_desc: Explain the meaning of momentum.
concept: Momentum is the product of mass and velocity: Momentum = Mass × Velocity (p = mv). It is a vector quantity with the same direction as velocity. Newton's second law can be stated as: Force = Rate of change of momentum. A heavier helicopter moving at the same speed has more momentum — it takes more force (and time) to change its velocity. The rotor system has significant angular momentum, which is why RRPM changes are slow and why gyroscopic precession occurs.
exam_tip: Momentum = mass × velocity (p = mv). Vector. More mass or more speed = more momentum. The rotor has angular momentum — this is why it gyroscopically precesses.
key_facts: Momentum = mass × velocity (p = mv)
Vector quantity — same direction as velocity
Greater mass or velocity = more momentum
Force = rate of change of momentum (Newton's 2nd law)
Rotor angular momentum causes gyroscopic precession of the disc

syllabus_desc: Explain the meaning of inertia.
concept: Inertia is the tendency of a body to resist any change to its state of motion — whether it's at rest or moving at constant velocity. It is a property of mass: more mass = more inertia. Newton's first law: a body remains at rest or in uniform motion unless acted upon by a net force. For a helicopter, the rotor has high rotational inertia — it takes time to spin up and time to slow down. This inertia is what makes autorotation possible: RRPM doesn't drop to zero instantly after engine failure.
exam_tip: Inertia: resistance to change in motion. More mass = more inertia. Rotor inertia is why autorotation works — RRPM doesn't immediately drop after engine failure.
key_facts: Inertia: resistance to change in state of motion (Newton's 1st law)
More mass = more inertia
A resting body resists being moved; a moving body resists being stopped
Rotor inertia: RRPM takes time to change — key to autorotation safety
High rotor inertia = more time to recover from engine failure

syllabus_desc: Differentiate between mass and weight.
concept: The centre of gravity (CG) -- also called the centre of mass -- is the single point at which the entire weight of the aeroplane can be considered to act. It's the balance point. If you could support the aeroplane at its CG, it would balance perfectly level. The CG position shifts as you load passengers, baggage, and fuel, and it changes during flight as fuel burns off. Its position relative to the wing's centre of pressure and the tailplane determines the aeroplane's pitch stability and controllability. A CG that's too far forward makes the aeroplane nose-heavy and hard to flare for landing. Too far aft and it becomes dangerously unstable in pitch. Every flight, the CG must be within the manufacturer's approved limits.
exam_tip: CG position affects stability, control authority, stall speed, and trim drag. Forward CG = more stable but higher stall speed and more trim drag. Aft CG = less stable, lighter control forces.
key_facts: CG is the point where total weight is considered to act
CG position moves with changes in loading and fuel burn
CG must be within manufacturer's approved limits for safe flight
Forward CG: more stable, heavier control forces, higher stall speed
Aft CG: less stable, lighter control forces, potential pitch-up divergence

syllabus_desc: State the value of the acceleration caused by the earth's gravity.
concept: The acceleration due to gravity -- usually written as 'g' -- is roughly 9.81 m/s² at the Earth's surface. That means any object in free fall accelerates downward by 9.81 metres per second every second, ignoring air resistance. For most aviation calculations, you'll round it to 9.8 m/s² or even 10 m/s² depending on the level of precision needed. This value isn't perfectly constant -- it varies slightly with latitude (stronger at the poles, weaker at the equator) and altitude (weaker as you climb away from the Earth's surface) -- but for helicopter performance work at the altitudes you'll be flying, 9.81 m/s² is the standard figure. You'll see g everywhere in performance calculations: weight = mass x g, load factor is measured in multiples of g, and manoeuvring limits are expressed in g. It's one of those numbers worth committing to memory permanently.
exam_tip: g = 9.81 m/s² (often rounded to 9.8 or 10 for quick calculations). Weight = mass x g. Load factor is expressed in multiples of g. Know the exact value for exam purposes.
key_facts: Standard acceleration due to gravity (g) = 9.81 m/s²
Commonly rounded to 9.8 m/s² or 10 m/s² for calculations
Slightly stronger at the poles, weaker at the equator
Decreases with altitude above Earth's surface
Weight = mass x g; load factor measured in multiples of g

syllabus_desc: Describe motion on a curved path, and differentiate between centripetal force and centrifugal reaction.
concept: When an object moves in a curved path -- like a helicopter in a banked turn -- it needs a force directed toward the centre of that curve to keep it turning. That inward force is centripetal force. Without it, the object would just continue in a straight line (Newton's first law in action). In a helicopter turn, the horizontal component of the rotor thrust provides the centripetal force, pulling the aircraft toward the centre of the turn. Centrifugal reaction is the equal and opposite 'force' that the occupants feel pushing them outward. Technically it's not a real force -- it's an inertial reaction, the body's tendency to keep going straight while the aircraft curves underneath it. But it feels very real to you and your passengers. The formula is centripetal force = mass x velocity² / radius. So faster speed or tighter radius means more centripetal force required, which means more bank angle, which means a higher load factor. That's why steep turns at speed load up the airframe more than gentle ones.
exam_tip: Centripetal force = mv²/r, directed toward the centre of the curve. Centrifugal reaction is the apparent outward force felt by occupants -- equal in magnitude, opposite in direction. Higher speed or tighter radius = more force needed.
key_facts: Centripetal force acts inward toward the centre of the curved path
Centrifugal reaction acts outward -- it's an inertial reaction, not a real force
Centripetal force = mass x velocity² / radius
In a turn, the horizontal component of rotor thrust provides centripetal force
Tighter turns or higher speeds require greater centripetal force and higher load factors

syllabus_desc: State the factors affecting centripetal force and rate of turn.
concept: Centripetal force is the force directed toward the centre of a circular path that keeps an object in curved flight. For a helicopter in a turn: Centripetal force = (Mass × Velocity²) ÷ Radius = Mass × Velocity × Rate of turn. Rate of turn depends on both bank angle and airspeed: Rate of turn = g × tan(bank) ÷ TAS. Radius of turn = TAS² ÷ (g × tan(bank)). Higher speed requires more centripetal force (or steeper bank) for the same rate of turn.
exam_tip: Centripetal force = mv²/r. Rate of turn = g × tan(bank)/TAS. Radius = TAS²/(g × tan(bank)). Know these relationships and how bank/speed affect them.
key_facts: Centripetal force = mv²/r = directed toward turn centre
Rate of turn = (g × tan bank) ÷ TAS — higher bank or lower speed = faster turn
Radius = TAS² ÷ (g × tan bank) — higher speed = larger radius
Centripetal force provided by horizontal component of rotor thrust in a turn
At any bank angle, lower speed = higher rate, smaller radius

syllabus_desc: Describe the trig functions for the sine, cosine and tangent of an angle.
concept: For a right-angled triangle, the three basic trigonometric ratios relate the angles to the side lengths. For an angle θ: Sine (sin θ) = opposite side ÷ hypotenuse; Cosine (cos θ) = adjacent side ÷ hypotenuse; Tangent (tan θ) = opposite side ÷ adjacent side. Mnemonic: SOH-CAH-TOA. In aviation: the load factor in a turn = 1/cos(bank) uses the cosine function; rate of turn = g × tan(bank)/TAS uses the tangent function.
exam_tip: SOH-CAH-TOA: Sin = Opposite/Hypotenuse; Cos = Adjacent/Hypotenuse; Tan = Opposite/Adjacent. Used for load factor (1/cos bank) and rate of turn (g×tan bank/TAS).
key_facts: Sine: opposite ÷ hypotenuse
Cosine: adjacent ÷ hypotenuse
Tangent: opposite ÷ adjacent
SOH-CAH-TOA mnemonic
Load factor = 1/cos(bank angle); Rate of turn uses tan(bank angle)

syllabus_desc: Describe the moment of a force, and the moment of a couple.
concept: The moment of a force (torque) is the tendency of a force to cause rotation about a pivot point. Moment = Force × Perpendicular Distance from pivot (lever arm). A couple is a pair of equal and opposite forces separated by a distance — it produces pure rotation with no translational effect. In a helicopter, the main rotor creates a torque reaction (couple) on the fuselage — the tail rotor provides an opposing moment to keep the helicopter from spinning.
exam_tip: Moment = Force × perpendicular distance. A couple = two equal/opposite forces creating pure rotation. Main rotor torque vs tail rotor moment = rotational equilibrium.
key_facts: Moment (torque) = Force × perpendicular lever arm distance
Moment causes rotation about a pivot point
Couple: two equal, opposite, parallel forces — creates pure rotation
Main rotor reaction torque is countered by tail rotor moment
Rotational equilibrium: sum of all moments = zero

syllabus_desc: Describe the conditions required for translational equilibrium, and for rotational equilibrium.
concept: Translational equilibrium: the condition where the sum of all forces in every direction equals zero — the body has no linear acceleration (it's either stationary or moving at constant velocity). Rotational equilibrium: the condition where the sum of all torques (moments) about any point equals zero — the body has no angular acceleration (it's not rotating, or rotating at constant rate). A helicopter in straight and level flight satisfies both: ΣF = 0 in all directions, and Στ = 0 about any axis.
exam_tip: Translational equilibrium: ΣF = 0 (no linear acceleration). Rotational equilibrium: Στ = 0 (no angular acceleration). Straight and level = both.
key_facts: Translational equilibrium: vector sum of all forces = zero
Body stationary or moving at constant velocity
Rotational equilibrium: sum of all torques = zero
Body not rotating, or rotating at constant angular velocity
Straight and level flight satisfies both equilibria simultaneously

syllabus_desc: Explain the meaning of Centre of Gravity (centre of mass).
concept: The centre of gravity (CG) — also called centre of mass — is the single point through which the total weight of the body can be considered to act. For a helicopter, the CG is the balance point: if you could support the helicopter at its CG, it would not tilt. The CG location affects stability and control — an aft CG reduces longitudinal stability and requires forward cyclic to maintain level flight, limiting forward cyclic authority for other inputs. CG must remain within limits specified in the AFM.
exam_tip: CG = the balance point — where total weight effectively acts. CG location affects stability and control authority. Must stay within AFM limits.
key_facts: Centre of gravity: point through which total weight acts
Balance point of the aircraft
Aft CG: reduces longitudinal stability, requires forward cyclic correction
Forward CG: requires aft cyclic, affects nose-heavy characteristics
CG must remain within fore-aft and lateral limits in the AFM

syllabus_desc: Explain the meaning of work, power and energy; and describe kinetic energy and potential energy.
concept: Work is done when a force moves through a distance: Work = Force × Distance (W = Fd). The SI unit is the Joule (J). Power is the rate of doing work: Power = Work ÷ Time = Force × Velocity (W/t = Fv). Energy is the capacity to do work. Kinetic energy (KE) is the energy of motion: KE = ½mv². Potential energy (PE) is stored energy due to position: PE = mgh (mass × gravitational acceleration × height). In autorotation, the helicopter converts potential energy (altitude) and kinetic energy (RRPM) into rotor thrust for a safe landing.
exam_tip: Work = F × d (Joules). Power = W/t = F × v (Watts). KE = ½mv². PE = mgh. Autorotation: converts altitude (PE) and RRPM (KE) into rotor thrust.
key_facts: Work = Force × distance (SI unit: Joule)
Power = Work ÷ time = Force × velocity (SI unit: Watt)
Kinetic energy = ½mv²
Potential energy = mgh
Autorotation: PE and KE converted to thrust for landing

syllabus_desc: Define air density.
concept: Air density is the mass of air per unit volume, and it's one of the most important variables in aviation because it directly affects lift, drag, and engine performance. Three factors change air density: temperature, pressure, and humidity. Higher pressure squeezes more molecules into a given volume, increasing density. Higher temperature makes molecules move faster and spread apart, decreasing density. Higher humidity means some of the heavier nitrogen and oxygen molecules are replaced by lighter water vapour molecules, so humid air is actually less dense than dry air at the same temperature and pressure. On a hot, humid, low-pressure day -- think Ardmore in February -- your aeroplane performs noticeably worse because the air is thinner than a cool, dry, high-pressure winter morning.
exam_tip: Remember: high pressure increases density, high temperature decreases density, high humidity decreases density. Humid air is LESS dense than dry air -- this catches people out.
key_facts: Air density = mass per unit volume (kg/m cubed)
Increased pressure increases density (direct relationship)
Increased temperature decreases density (inverse relationship)
Increased humidity decreases density (water vapour is lighter than N2 and O2)
Density directly affects lift production, drag, and engine power output

syllabus_desc: Describe the effect of temperature, pressure and humidity on air density.
concept: As you climb away from the surface, pressure, temperature, and density all decrease -- but not at the same rate. Pressure drops roughly exponentially: it halves approximately every 18,000 feet. Temperature drops at a fairly steady rate of about 2 degrees Celsius per 1,000 feet (the ISA lapse rate of 1.98C/1,000 ft) in the troposphere, which extends up to about 36,000 feet (11 km) at mid-latitudes. Above the tropopause, temperature stabilises at about -56.5C in the ISA model. Density, being a function of both pressure and temperature, also decreases with altitude. The practical upshot: as you climb, the air gets thinner, your engine produces less power, your wings need a higher TAS to generate the same lift, and eventually you reach a ceiling where you can't climb any more.
exam_tip: ISA lapse rate = approximately 2C per 1,000 ft. Pressure halves roughly every 18,000 ft. Temperature stops decreasing at the tropopause (36,090 ft ISA).
key_facts: Pressure decreases roughly exponentially with altitude (halves every ~18,000 ft)
Temperature decreases at ~2C per 1,000 ft (ISA lapse rate = 1.98C/1,000 ft) in the troposphere
Density decreases with altitude as both pressure and temperature decrease
The tropopause is at approximately 36,090 ft (11 km) in the ISA model
Above the tropopause, ISA temperature remains constant at -56.5C

syllabus_desc: Describe the normal changes in pressure, temperature and density with increased altitude.
concept: The International Standard Atmosphere (ISA) is a mathematical model that gives us a common baseline for comparing aircraft performance. At sea level, ISA conditions are: pressure 1013.25 hPa (29.92 inHg), temperature 15C (288.15 K), and density 1.225 kg/m cubed. The temperature lapse rate is approximately 1.98C per 1,000 feet up to the tropopause. These numbers aren't meant to represent any real day's weather -- they're a reference standard. When someone says an aeroplane's service ceiling is 14,000 feet, they mean in ISA conditions. Real performance will differ depending on how far the actual conditions deviate from ISA. Learning ISA sea-level values is like learning your times tables -- they're the foundation everything else builds on.
exam_tip: ISA sea level: 1013.25 hPa, 15C, 1.225 kg/m cubed. Lapse rate approximately 2C/1,000 ft. Know these numbers absolutely cold -- they appear in almost every performance question.
key_facts: ISA sea-level pressure: 1013.25 hPa (29.92 inHg)
ISA sea-level temperature: 15C (288.15 K)
ISA sea-level density: 1.225 kg/m cubed
ISA temperature lapse rate: approximately 1.98C per 1,000 ft
ISA is a mathematical model for standardising performance comparisons, not real weather

syllabus_desc: Describe the International Standard Atmosphere.
concept: The atmosphere isn't uniform -- it's layered, and the layer we care about most is the troposphere, where all weather occurs and where most general aviation takes place. The troposphere extends from the surface to about 36,000 feet at mid-latitudes (higher at the equator, lower at the poles). In this layer, pressure drops from 1013 hPa at sea level to roughly 226 hPa at the tropopause. Density follows a similar pattern, dropping from 1.225 kg/m cubed to about 0.365 kg/m cubed. The key takeaway is that by about 20,000 feet, you've lost roughly half the sea-level air density, which means your engine is producing roughly half its sea-level power and your wings need significantly higher TAS to generate the same lift.
exam_tip: By 18,000 ft, pressure is about half sea-level value. The troposphere contains about 75% of the atmosphere's total mass. Performance degrades progressively with altitude.
key_facts: Troposphere: surface to ~36,000 ft (most weather and GA operations occur here)
Pressure drops from 1013 hPa at sea level to ~226 hPa at the tropopause
Density drops from 1.225 kg/m cubed to ~0.365 kg/m cubed at the tropopause
By ~18,000 ft, pressure and density are approximately half their sea-level values
The troposphere contains roughly 75% of the atmosphere's total mass

syllabus_desc: State the ISA sea level pressure and temperature conditions, and the approximate lapse rates in the lower atmosphere.
concept: Density altitude is the altitude in the ISA that corresponds to the actual air density you're experiencing. It's the single best indicator of how your aeroplane will perform. If you're sitting on the runway at Queenstown (elevation 1,171 ft) on a hot day with low pressure, the air might be as thin as ISA air at 4,000 feet -- so your density altitude is 4,000 ft, and your aeroplane will perform as though it's at 4,000 ft regardless of what the altimeter reads. High density altitude means poor performance: longer take-off runs, reduced climb rates, and lower service ceilings. Density altitude increases with higher elevation, higher temperature, lower pressure, and higher humidity. On a hot Canterbury day with a nor'wester dropping the pressure, density altitude can be surprisingly high even at low-elevation aerodromes.
exam_tip: Density altitude = pressure altitude corrected for non-standard temperature. Hot, high, and humid = high density altitude = poor performance. This is a cornerstone of performance planning.
key_facts: Density altitude is the ISA altitude corresponding to the actual air density
Higher temperature, lower pressure, and higher humidity all increase density altitude
Density altitude is the best single measure of expected aircraft performance
High density altitude = reduced lift, reduced engine power, longer take-off distance
Calculated from pressure altitude corrected for temperature deviation from ISA

syllabus_desc: Describe the approximate altitude bands in which atmospheric pressure and density are reduced to 75, 50 and 25% of their normal sea level values.
concept: Viscosity is the internal friction or 'stickiness' of a fluid. Even though air seems thin and insubstantial, it does have viscosity -- and it matters for aerodynamics. Air viscosity is why we get a boundary layer on the wing surface: the air molecules immediately next to the skin are effectively stationary (the no-slip condition), and viscosity transmits this friction effect outward through the boundary layer. Higher viscosity means more internal friction. Here's the counterintuitive bit: unlike liquids, air viscosity increases with temperature. Hotter air is more viscous. However, the effect on aircraft performance is relatively small compared to density changes. The Reynolds number, which predicts whether airflow will be laminar or turbulent, is directly related to viscosity -- lower viscosity (or higher speed/larger chord) means higher Reynolds number and a greater tendency toward turbulent flow.
exam_tip: Air viscosity increases with temperature (opposite to liquids). Viscosity creates the boundary layer through the no-slip condition. Reynolds number relates viscosity to flow behaviour.
key_facts: Viscosity is the internal friction or resistance to flow within a fluid
Air viscosity increases with temperature (unlike liquids)
Viscosity causes the boundary layer through the no-slip condition at the surface
Reynolds number = (density x velocity x chord) / viscosity
Higher Reynolds number favours turbulent flow; lower Reynolds number favours laminar flow

syllabus_desc: Explain the meaning of density altitude (DA) and, in broad terms, the effect of pressure, temperature and humidity on DA and thus aerodynamic and engine performance.
concept: Density altitude (DA) is the altitude in the International Standard Atmosphere that corresponds to the actual air density at the location. It accounts for the combined effects of pressure altitude, temperature, and humidity. High pressure, low temperature, and low humidity all reduce DA. Low pressure, high temperature, and high humidity all increase DA. High DA reduces both aerodynamic performance (less dense air for rotor and wings) and engine performance (less air mass for combustion). DA is the most important factor in helicopter performance planning.
exam_tip: Density altitude = the altitude that corresponds to the actual air density. High DA (hot/high/humid) reduces everything: rotor efficiency, engine power, and all performance figures.
key_facts: High DA causes: reduced engine power, reduced rotor efficiency, degraded all performance
DA increases with: high temperature, low QNH, high humidity
DA decreases with: low temperature, high QNH, low humidity
DA formula: PA + 120 × (OAT − ISA temp at PA)
Most important performance factor — always compute DA before demanding operations

syllabus_desc: Explain the term viscosity, when related to air.
concept: Viscosity is a measure of a fluid's resistance to flow — its internal friction. A high-viscosity fluid (like honey) flows slowly; a low-viscosity fluid (like water or air) flows readily. Air viscosity affects the boundary layer and drag on rotor blades. As temperature increases, the viscosity of air increases (unlike liquids, where viscosity decreases with temperature). The Reynolds number — a dimensionless parameter relating velocity, density, and viscosity — determines whether airflow is laminar or turbulent over a surface.
exam_tip: Viscosity = resistance to flow (internal friction). Air viscosity increases with temperature (opposite of liquids). Affects boundary layer, skin friction drag, and Reynolds number.
key_facts: Viscosity: fluid's resistance to flow (internal friction)
High viscosity: thick, flows slowly (honey)
Low viscosity: thin, flows easily (water, air)
Air viscosity increases with temperature (unlike liquids)
Affects boundary layer and skin friction drag on rotor blades

syllabus_desc: Describe free stream static pressure.
concept: Free stream static pressure is the atmospheric pressure in the undisturbed airstream — away from any aerodynamic body. It is the pressure that would be measured by a static port that is not influenced by the velocity of the airflow. Static pressure decreases with altitude (as per the ISA). In Bernoulli's equation: where airspeed increases over a curved surface, static pressure decreases — this is the mechanism of lift generation on an aerofoil.
exam_tip: Static pressure: undisturbed atmospheric pressure away from the aircraft. It decreases with altitude. Where airflow speeds up (e.g., over a curved surface), static pressure drops — creating lift.
key_facts: Free stream static pressure: undisturbed atmospheric pressure
Not influenced by airflow velocity
Decreases with altitude (ISA lapse rate)
Bernoulli: increased velocity = decreased static pressure
Measured by static ports on the aircraft

syllabus_desc: Describe dynamic pressure (including the term 1/2 rho V squared).
concept: Dynamic pressure is the pressure associated with the kinetic energy of the moving airflow. It equals ½ρV² (half times air density times velocity squared). The term 'q' is used in aerodynamics to represent dynamic pressure. Dynamic pressure is the basis of the airspeed indicator — the ASI measures the difference between pitot (total) pressure and static pressure, which equals dynamic pressure. At higher altitudes, for the same TAS, dynamic pressure is lower (because density ρ decreases).
exam_tip: Dynamic pressure q = ½ρV². The 'push' of moving air — basis for the ASI reading. At altitude, same TAS = less dynamic pressure (less dense air).
key_facts: Dynamic pressure: q = ½ρV²
The kinetic energy component of pressure in moving air
Basis of the airspeed indicator (measures pitot minus static = dynamic pressure)
Increases with speed squared and with air density
At altitude: same TAS but less dynamic pressure (lower ρ)

syllabus_desc: Describe total (or pitot) pressure.
concept: Total pressure (pitot pressure) is the sum of static pressure and dynamic pressure: Pitot = Static + Dynamic (P_total = P_static + ½ρV²). This is Bernoulli's equation for incompressible flow. In a pitot tube, the airflow is brought to rest — all kinetic energy (dynamic pressure) converts to static pressure, giving total pressure. The difference between total and static pressure equals dynamic pressure, which the ASI uses to indicate airspeed.
exam_tip: Total pressure = static + dynamic (Bernoulli). Pitot tube measures total pressure. ASI reads pitot minus static = dynamic pressure = q = ½ρV².
key_facts: Total pressure = static pressure + dynamic pressure
P_total = P_static + ½ρV² (Bernoulli's equation)
Pitot tube: flow brought to rest — kinetic energy converts to static pressure
Measured by the pitot probe
ASI: pitot (total) − static = dynamic pressure → airspeed indication

syllabus_desc: Explain the principle of airspeed indication, and indicate the relationship between indicated, calibrated, equivalent and true airspeeds (IAS, CAS, EAS and TAS).
concept: The airspeed indicator (ASI) works by measuring the difference between total pressure and static pressure. A pitot tube faces into the airflow and captures total pressure (static + dynamic). A static port, flush with the fuselage, senses static pressure alone. The ASI capsule expands or contracts based on the pressure difference -- which is dynamic pressure -- and moves the needle. Since dynamic pressure depends on both density and TAS, the ASI effectively reads what the aeroplane 'feels' in terms of aerodynamic force, regardless of altitude. This is incredibly useful: indicated airspeed (IAS) directly relates to the aerodynamic forces acting on the aircraft. Your stall speed, for instance, occurs at the same IAS regardless of altitude. Calibrated airspeed (CAS) corrects IAS for instrument and position errors. Equivalent airspeed (EAS) corrects for compressibility. TAS corrects for density.
exam_tip: IAS relates to dynamic pressure and aerodynamic forces. Stall speed is the same IAS at any altitude. TAS increases with altitude for the same IAS because density decreases.
key_facts: ASI measures the difference between pitot (total) and static pressure
This pressure difference equals dynamic pressure (q = 0.5 x rho x V squared)
IAS relates directly to the aerodynamic forces on the aircraft
CAS = IAS corrected for instrument and position error
TAS = CAS corrected for density (altitude and temperature); TAS > CAS at altitude

syllabus_desc: With respect to aerofoils, explain the meaning of section.
concept: A section of an aerofoil is a two-dimensional cross-section of the blade taken perpendicular to the span. The shape of the section — its camber, thickness, chord — determines the aerodynamic characteristics of the blade at that point. Rotor blades may have different sections at different spanwise locations to optimise performance. The section determines the lift, drag, and stall characteristics of that blade element.
exam_tip: A section is a 2D cross-cut through the blade span — the shape that generates aerodynamic force. Different sections at different span locations optimise performance.
key_facts: Section: 2D cross-section of blade perpendicular to the span
Shape determines aerodynamic characteristics (lift, drag, stall)
Rotor blades may use different sections at different spanwise locations
Section is what we analyse in 2D aerofoil theory
NACA numbering system describes section shape

syllabus_desc: With respect to aerofoils, explain the meaning of leading edge.
concept: The leading edge is the forward-facing edge of the aerofoil section — the part that first encounters the oncoming airflow. For rotor blades, the leading edge is typically rounded to allow smooth airflow attachment across a range of angles of attack. A sharp leading edge (thin aerofoil) would separate flow at lower angles of attack. The leading edge is subject to erosion and must be maintained in good condition.
exam_tip: Leading edge: forward-facing edge of the aerofoil — first contact with airflow. Rounded for flow attachment across a range of AoA. Erosion must be checked regularly.
key_facts: Leading edge: forward-facing edge of aerofoil section
First part to contact the airflow
Typically rounded on rotor blades for smooth flow attachment
Allows stable flow attachment across a range of AoA
Subject to erosion — inspect regularly during pre-flight

syllabus_desc: With respect to aerofoils, explain the meaning of trailing edge.
concept: The trailing edge is the aft-facing edge of the aerofoil section — where the upper and lower surface airflows reunite. For an efficient aerofoil, the trailing edge should be sharp (or nearly so) to allow smooth flow departure and minimise separation drag. The Kutta condition states that flow at the trailing edge must leave smoothly. In rotor blades, the trailing edge is thinner than the leading edge.
exam_tip: Trailing edge: aft edge where upper and lower flows reunite. Should be sharp for efficient flow departure. Kutta condition: flow must leave the trailing edge smoothly.
key_facts: Trailing edge: aft-facing edge of aerofoil section
Where upper and lower surface airflows reunite
Should be sharp for efficient low-drag flow departure
Kutta condition: smooth flow departure at the trailing edge
Damage to trailing edge disrupts airflow and increases drag

syllabus_desc: With respect to aerofoils, explain the meaning of chord.
concept: The chord is the straight-line distance from the leading edge to the trailing edge of an aerofoil section. It is the reference dimension for expressing aerofoil characteristics (e.g., thickness as a percentage of chord). For rotor blades, chord is typically constant or tapered slightly along the span. A wider chord (greater chord length) increases blade area and lift capacity but also increases weight and drag.
exam_tip: Chord = straight-line distance from leading edge to trailing edge. Reference dimension for aerofoil geometry. Wider chord = more area but more weight and drag.
key_facts: Chord: straight-line leading edge to trailing edge distance
Reference length for aerofoil geometry
Thickness, camber expressed as % of chord
Wider chord: more blade area, more lift capacity, more weight
Tapered chord: narrower toward tip (common in rotor blade design)

syllabus_desc: With respect to aerofoils, explain the meaning of chord line.
concept: The chord line is the straight line connecting the leading edge and trailing edge of an aerofoil section. It is the reference line for measuring the angle of attack — the angle between the chord line and the relative airflow. For a symmetrical aerofoil, the chord line passes through the centre of the section. For a cambered aerofoil, the chord line is below the mean camber line at mid-chord.
exam_tip: Chord line: straight line from leading to trailing edge — the AoA reference line. Angle of attack = angle between chord line and relative airflow.
key_facts: Chord line: straight line from leading edge to trailing edge
Reference line for angle of attack measurement
Angle of attack = angle between chord line and relative airflow
For symmetrical aerofoil: chord line bisects the section
For cambered aerofoil: chord line below the mean camber line

syllabus_desc: With respect to aerofoils, explain the meaning of thickness.
concept: Thickness is the maximum perpendicular distance between the upper and lower surfaces of the aerofoil section, measured perpendicular to the chord line. It is usually expressed as a percentage of chord length — the thickness-to-chord ratio (t/c). A thick section (high t/c) generates more lift but more drag and may stall at a lower AoA than a thin section. Rotor blade sections typically have t/c ratios of 8–15%.
exam_tip: Thickness = maximum distance between upper and lower surfaces, expressed as % of chord (t/c ratio). Thicker = more lift potential but more drag.
key_facts: Thickness: maximum perpendicular distance between upper and lower surfaces
Measured perpendicular to chord line
Expressed as percentage of chord length (t/c ratio)
Higher t/c: more lift potential, higher drag
Rotor blades typically t/c ratio of 8–15%

syllabus_desc: With respect to aerofoils, explain the meaning of thickness/chord ratio.
concept: The thickness-to-chord ratio (t/c) is the maximum thickness divided by the chord length, expressed as a percentage. It is one of the most important parameters determining the aerodynamic characteristics of an aerofoil. A high t/c ratio (thick aerofoil) produces more lift at low AoA but stalls earlier and has higher profile drag. A low t/c (thin aerofoil) is efficient at high speed but stalls more abruptly. Rotor blades balance these requirements with a moderate t/c.
exam_tip: t/c ratio = max thickness ÷ chord × 100%. High t/c = thicker blade = more lift at low AoA but earlier stall. Low t/c = thinner = better at high speed.
key_facts: t/c ratio = maximum thickness ÷ chord length × 100%
High t/c (thick): more lift at low AoA, higher drag, earlier stall
Low t/c (thin): efficient at high speed, more abrupt stall
Most rotor blades: moderate t/c (8–15%) balancing both extremes
NACA 4-digit series includes t/c in the designation

syllabus_desc: With respect to aerofoils, explain the meaning of camber.
concept: Camber is the curvature of the mean camber line — the line equidistant between the upper and lower surfaces of the aerofoil. A cambered aerofoil generates lift even at zero angle of attack because the curvature accelerates the airflow over the upper surface more than the lower. Symmetrical aerofoils have zero camber — they generate no lift at zero AoA and produce equal lift/down-force for positive/negative AoA. Rotor blades typically use symmetrical or low-camber sections to allow operation at both positive and negative AoA without pitching moment complications.
exam_tip: Camber = curvature of the mean camber line. Cambered aerofoils generate lift at zero AoA. Symmetrical (zero camber) rotor blades work equally at positive and negative AoA.
key_facts: Camber: curvature of the mean line between upper and lower surfaces
Cambered aerofoil: generates lift at zero AoA
More camber: more lift but also more pitching moment
Symmetrical aerofoil: zero camber, zero lift at zero AoA
Rotor blades use symmetrical or low-camber sections for operational flexibility

syllabus_desc: Distinguish between symmetrical and non-symmetrical aerofoil section.
concept: Relative airflow (also called the freestream or relative wind) is the direction of the airflow relative to the aircraft, well ahead of any disturbance caused by the aircraft itself. In still air, it's exactly opposite to the aircraft's flight path direction. If you're flying straight and level, the relative airflow comes from directly ahead. If you're climbing, it comes from slightly below the nose. Angle of attack (alpha) is the angle between the chord line of the wing and the direction of the relative airflow. This is not the same as pitch attitude -- an aeroplane can have a nose-high attitude but a low angle of attack (think of a steep climb at high power), or a level attitude but a high angle of attack (slow flight). Angle of attack is the pilot's primary tool for controlling lift, and every aerofoil stalls at a specific critical angle of attack, regardless of speed, weight, or configuration.
exam_tip: Angle of attack is measured from the relative airflow to the chord line, NOT from the horizon. The stalling angle is constant regardless of weight, speed, or bank -- only the stalling speed changes.
key_facts: Relative airflow: airflow direction relative to aircraft, opposite to flight path in still air
Angle of attack (alpha): angle between chord line and relative airflow
Angle of attack is NOT the same as pitch attitude
Increasing angle of attack increases lift (up to the stalling angle)
The stalling angle of attack is essentially constant for a given aerofoil configuration

syllabus_desc: With the aid of a diagram, identify and explain the meaning of relative air flow.
concept: Relative airflow (RAF) — also called relative wind — is the direction from which the air appears to flow over the blade element, taking into account both the rotation of the blade and any translational or vertical motion of the helicopter. It is always opposed to the direction of the blade's motion through the air. In the hover, RAF is primarily from below (induced flow downward through the disc). In forward flight, RAF is a combination of rotational velocity and forward/vertical flight components.
exam_tip: Relative airflow = apparent direction of air over the blade element, from the blade's perspective. Always opposes the blade's motion. Changes with rotor position, speed, and flight regime.
key_facts: RAF: direction air appears to flow over the blade element
Always opposes the direction of blade motion
Hover: RAF mostly from below (downward induced flow through disc)
Forward flight: RAF = vector sum of rotational velocity + forward/vertical components
AoA is always measured from the RAF direction to the chord line

syllabus_desc: With the aid of a diagram, identify and explain the meaning of angle of attack.
concept: Angle of attack (AoA) is the angle between the chord line of the aerofoil section and the relative airflow. It is the primary aerodynamic variable that determines lift and drag on the blade element. Increasing AoA increases lift (up to the critical/stall angle). Exceeding the critical AoA causes the boundary layer to separate and the blade element to stall — lift decreases rapidly and drag increases dramatically. For rotor blades, collective pitch changes AoA across all elements; cyclic changes AoA periodically as the blade rotates.
exam_tip: AoA = angle between chord line and relative airflow. More AoA = more lift (up to stall). Past critical AoA: stall — lift drops, drag spikes.
key_facts: Angle of attack: angle between chord line and relative airflow
Increasing AoA increases lift (up to critical angle)
Critical AoA: typically 12–16° for most rotor blade sections
Exceeding critical AoA: stall — boundary layer separates, lift drops rapidly
Collective changes AoA uniformly; cyclic changes AoA periodically across rotation

syllabus_desc: State Bernoulli's Theorem in simple terms.
concept: A venturi is a tube that narrows in the middle and then expands again. When air flows through the narrow throat, it must speed up (by continuity -- the same mass of air has to pass through a smaller area). By Bernoulli's principle, this increase in speed causes a drop in static pressure at the throat. This is the venturi effect, and it's exactly what happens over the curved upper surface of a wing. The wing's camber acts like one side of a venturi, accelerating the air over the top surface and creating the low-pressure region that produces lift. Venturi tubes are also used in some aircraft instruments -- the attitude indicator and heading indicator in older aircraft are often driven by a venturi-powered vacuum system, where the pressure drop at the venturi throat creates suction to spin the gyroscope.
exam_tip: Venturi effect: constriction speeds up airflow and drops static pressure. This is the same principle that creates low pressure over the wing's upper surface.
key_facts: A venturi is a convergent-divergent tube (narrows then widens)
Continuity: air speeds up through the narrow throat (same mass flow, smaller area)
Bernoulli: increased speed at the throat means decreased static pressure
The wing's upper surface acts like one wall of a venturi
Venturi tubes are used in some aircraft vacuum systems for instrument power

syllabus_desc: Describe streamline flow, turbulent flow, and the application of Bernoulli's Theorem to the streamline flow around an aerofoil.
concept: As angle of attack increases from zero, the pressure distribution around an aerofoil changes dramatically. The low-pressure area on the upper surface expands and the suction peak near the leading edge intensifies, generating more lift. The stagnation point (where airflow splits between upper and lower surfaces) moves progressively aft and downward from the leading edge. On the lower surface, pressure increases, adding a small contribution to lift. At moderate angles of attack, the flow remains attached and orderly. But as you approach the stalling angle (typically 15-16 degrees for a GP aerofoil), the airflow can no longer follow the increasingly steep upper surface curvature. The boundary layer separates from the surface starting at the trailing edge, the low-pressure area collapses, and lift drops dramatically. That's the stall.
exam_tip: As alpha increases: suction peak grows, stagnation point moves aft, lift increases -- until the critical angle where flow separates and lift drops sharply.
key_facts: Increasing alpha intensifies the suction peak on the upper surface
The stagnation point moves progressively aft and below the leading edge
Upper surface contributes roughly two-thirds of total lift through suction
Lower surface contributes roughly one-third through increased pressure
Beyond the critical angle of attack, flow separation causes the stall

syllabus_desc: Describe a venturi and explain venturi effect.
concept: Upwash is the upward deflection of air ahead of the wing's leading edge. As the wing approaches, air is drawn upward to flow over the top surface. Downwash is the downward deflection of air behind the trailing edge. The wing turns the airflow downward as it generates lift -- Newton's third law in action: the wing pushes air down, and the air pushes the wing up. The amount of downwash increases with angle of attack and lift coefficient. Downwash has important practical effects: it reduces the effective angle of attack of the tailplane (which affects longitudinal stability), it creates induced drag (the rearward tilt of the lift vector), and the downwash field from a large aircraft's wings can be hazardous to smaller aircraft following behind -- that's wake turbulence.
exam_tip: Downwash increases with angle of attack and CL. It reduces the effective alpha at the tailplane and is a key factor in induced drag and wake turbulence.
key_facts: Upwash: airflow deflected upward ahead of the leading edge
Downwash: airflow deflected downward behind the trailing edge
Downwash increases with angle of attack and lift coefficient
Downwash reduces the effective angle of attack at the tailplane
Downwash is directly related to induced drag and wake turbulence

syllabus_desc: Explain the changes to the airflow and pressure distribution around a typical symmetrical aerofoil as the angle of attack is increased from the zero-lift angle of attack to the stalling angle.
concept: The centre of pressure (CP) is the single point on the chord line where the total aerodynamic force can be considered to act. Think of it as the 'balance point' of all the pressure forces on the aerofoil. For a cambered aerofoil, the CP moves forward as angle of attack increases and aft as angle of attack decreases. At the stall, the CP typically makes a sudden rearward shift as the pressure distribution collapses. This CP movement is significant for stability -- on a cambered wing without a tailplane, an increase in alpha would move the CP forward of the CG, creating a nose-up pitching moment that increases alpha further, which is unstable. That's one reason we need a tailplane. For a symmetrical aerofoil, the CP stays at approximately the quarter-chord point (25% of chord from the leading edge) regardless of angle of attack.
exam_tip: Cambered aerofoil: CP moves forward with increasing alpha, aft with decreasing alpha. Symmetrical aerofoil: CP stays at ~25% chord. CP movement affects pitch stability.
key_facts: CP is the point where total aerodynamic force is considered to act
Cambered aerofoil: CP moves forward as alpha increases, aft as alpha decreases
At the stall, CP moves sharply rearward
Symmetrical aerofoil: CP remains at approximately 25% chord (quarter-chord point)
CP movement relative to CG determines pitch stability contribution from the wing

syllabus_desc: Explain the terms upwash and downwash in an airflow.
concept: As air approaches a wing or rotor blade, it doesn't just split at the leading edge and flow straight back. Ahead of the aerofoil, the airflow actually curves upward to meet the leading edge -- this is upwash. It's caused by the pressure field around the aerofoil reaching forward and deflecting the oncoming air upward before it arrives. After the air passes over and under the aerofoil and flows off the trailing edge, it's been deflected downward compared to the original freestream direction -- this is downwash. Downwash is a direct consequence of the aerofoil generating lift; by Newton's third law, the wing pushes air downward and the air pushes the wing upward. The stronger the lift, the more pronounced the downwash. On a helicopter, the rotor system produces very strong downwash below the disc -- you'll feel it on the ground as rotor wash. Downwash also affects the tailplane and horizontal stabiliser, which sit in the wake of the main rotor or wing.
exam_tip: Upwash = air deflected upward ahead of the aerofoil. Downwash = air deflected downward behind it. Downwash is a direct result of lift production -- more lift means more downwash. Both are caused by the pressure field around the aerofoil.
key_facts: Upwash is the upward deflection of air ahead of the aerofoil
Downwash is the downward deflection of air behind the trailing edge
Downwash is a direct consequence of lift generation (Newton's third law)
Greater lift produces stronger downwash
Downwash affects tailplane effectiveness and, in helicopters, creates rotor wash

syllabus_desc: Explain the term centre of pressure (CP) and describe typical movement of the CP with increasing angle of attack with a symmetrical aerofoil section and with a non-symmetrical aerofoil section.
concept: As angle of attack increases from zero, the total reaction force grows in magnitude and changes direction. At small angles of attack, the TR is modest and acts roughly perpendicular to the relative airflow. As alpha increases toward the stalling angle, the TR continues to grow -- both the lift and drag components increase. However, the growth isn't proportional: lift increases roughly linearly at first, while drag increases more slowly at low alpha but accelerates as alpha gets higher. The TR tilts progressively more rearward (increasing the drag component relative to lift). At the critical angle of attack, the flow separates, the TR suddenly decreases in magnitude and changes direction significantly. Past the stall, the TR has a much larger drag component relative to lift -- the wing has gone from being a lifting surface to something closer to a flat plate being pushed through the air.
exam_tip: TR grows with alpha up to the stall, then drops sharply. The L/D ratio peaks at a moderate alpha and decreases as the TR tilts increasingly rearward approaching the stall.
key_facts: TR magnitude increases with alpha up to the critical (stalling) angle
TR direction tilts increasingly rearward (more drag relative to lift) as alpha grows
At the stall, TR drops sharply and changes direction significantly
The ratio of lift to drag component (L/D) peaks at a moderate alpha
Beyond the stall, the drag component dominates the diminished TR

syllabus_desc: Define aerodynamic centre.
concept: Lift and drag are the two components we get when we resolve the total reaction force into useful directions. Lift is defined as the component of TR that acts perpendicular to the relative airflow (not perpendicular to the horizon -- perpendicular to the relative airflow). Drag is the component that acts parallel to and opposing the relative airflow. In straight and level flight, lift acts vertically and drag acts horizontally rearward, which keeps things simple. But in a climb, the relative airflow comes from below, so lift tilts slightly backward from vertical and drag tilts slightly downward from horizontal. The distinction between 'perpendicular to relative airflow' and 'perpendicular to horizon' becomes critical when analysing climbing, descending, and turning flight.
exam_tip: Lift is always perpendicular to the relative airflow, not to the horizon. Drag is always parallel to and opposing the relative airflow. Get this definition right -- it's tested frequently.
key_facts: Lift: component of TR perpendicular to the relative airflow
Drag: component of TR parallel to and opposing the relative airflow
In level flight, lift is vertical and drag is horizontal
In a climb or descent, the lift and drag vectors are tilted relative to the horizon
These definitions are critical for resolving forces in non-level flight

syllabus_desc: Describe the total aerodynamic reaction force (TR) of an aerofoil.
concept: The total aerodynamic reaction (TR) — also called the resultant aerodynamic force — is the single force that represents the combined effect of all aerodynamic pressure and shear forces acting on the aerofoil section. It acts at the centre of pressure and is composed of two components: lift (perpendicular to the relative airflow) and drag (parallel to the relative airflow, opposing motion). As AoA increases from zero, the TR grows and its direction tilts further toward the leading edge.
exam_tip: Total aerodynamic reaction (TR): the single resultant force from all aerodynamic forces on the blade section. Components: lift (perpendicular to RAF) and drag (parallel to RAF).
key_facts: TR: single resultant of all aerodynamic forces on the section
Acts at the centre of pressure
Two components: lift (perpendicular to RAF) and drag (parallel to RAF)
TR direction and magnitude change with AoA
At high AoA: TR tilts toward the leading edge; at stall, TR collapses

syllabus_desc: Describe how TR varies with angle of attack.
concept: As angle of attack increases from zero, the total aerodynamic reaction (TR) grows in magnitude (both lift and drag increase). The direction of the TR changes — at low AoA it is nearly perpendicular to the chord (mostly lift, little drag); at higher AoA the TR tilts further toward the leading edge as the induced drag component increases. At the critical angle, lift peaks; beyond this, separation occurs and TR collapses rapidly as the stall condition develops.
exam_tip: As AoA increases: TR magnitude increases (more lift and drag), and TR tilts toward the leading edge (induced drag increases). At critical AoA: TR peaks then collapses at stall.
key_facts: TR magnitude increases with AoA (up to critical angle)
TR direction tilts: more drag at higher AoA
Near zero AoA: TR almost perpendicular to chord (mostly lift)
At critical AoA: TR is maximum then collapses on stall
TR collapse at stall: lift drops sharply, drag increases dramatically

syllabus_desc: Define the TR components of lift and drag.
concept: The TR is resolved into two components relative to the relative airflow: Lift (L) is the component of TR perpendicular to the relative airflow — it supports the aircraft's weight (or in a rotor blade, contributes to the rotor thrust). Drag (D) is the component of TR parallel to the relative airflow and opposing the motion of the blade — it requires power to overcome. The ratio L/D is a measure of aerodynamic efficiency — a higher ratio means more lift for a given drag penalty.
exam_tip: TR components: Lift = perpendicular to RAF (supports weight/generates thrust). Drag = parallel to RAF (opposes motion). L/D ratio = aerodynamic efficiency.
key_facts: Lift: component of TR perpendicular to RAF
Drag: component of TR parallel to RAF (opposing motion)
Lift supports weight; drag requires power to overcome
L/D ratio: measure of aerodynamic efficiency
Maximum L/D: the most efficient operating angle of attack

syllabus_desc: Define the TR components of rotor thrust and rotor drag.
concept: When TR is resolved for a rotor blade rather than a fixed-wing section, the components are named differently to reflect their function in the rotor system. Rotor thrust is the component of TR perpendicular to the rotor disc plane — it provides the lift to support the helicopter and the propulsive force when the disc is tilted. Rotor drag (profile drag + induced drag) is the component within the disc plane opposing blade rotation — it is what the engine must overcome to keep the rotor turning.
exam_tip: Rotor thrust: TR component perpendicular to disc plane (provides lift and propulsion when disc tilts). Rotor drag: TR component in disc plane (opposes rotation, must be overcome by engine).
key_facts: Rotor thrust: perpendicular to rotor disc — supports helicopter weight
When disc tilts: rotor thrust has horizontal component for propulsion
Rotor drag: acts in disc plane, opposing blade rotation
Engine overcomes rotor drag to maintain RRPM
Profile drag + induced drag = total rotor drag

syllabus_desc: Explain the factors that affect lift in subsonic and transonic flow.
concept: Lift depends on several factors, all captured in the lift formula. At low subsonic speeds (where compressibility effects are negligible), the factors are: air density, the square of the airspeed, the wing area, and the coefficient of lift (which itself depends on angle of attack, aerofoil shape, and surface condition). You can't change wing area in flight (ignoring Fowler flaps for the moment), and density is largely dictated by altitude and temperature. That leaves angle of attack and airspeed as the pilot's two main tools for controlling lift. Increase either one and lift goes up. The key insight is that airspeed is squared -- doubling your speed quadruples the available lift, while halving your speed cuts it to a quarter.
exam_tip: The four lift factors are: density (rho), velocity squared (V squared), wing area (S), and CL. Speed is squared, so it has the dominant effect on lift.
key_facts: Lift depends on air density (rho), velocity squared, wing area, and coefficient of lift
Airspeed is squared -- doubling speed gives four times the lift (at the same CL)
CL depends on angle of attack, aerofoil shape, and surface condition
Pilot controls lift primarily through angle of attack and airspeed
At low subsonic speeds, compressibility effects are negligible

syllabus_desc: State the lift formula.
concept: The lift formula is L = CL x 0.5 x rho x V squared x S. Let's break that down into its three basic functions. First, CL (coefficient of lift) captures the aerodynamic efficiency of the wing at a given angle of attack -- it's the 'shape and angle' factor. Second, 0.5 x rho x V squared is the dynamic pressure (q) -- it's the 'energy of the airflow' factor, combining air density and speed into a single pressure value. Third, S is the wing area -- the 'size' factor. So lift equals aerodynamic efficiency times airflow energy times wing size. In level flight, lift must equal weight, so if you increase altitude (reducing rho), you need either more speed or more CL (higher alpha) to compensate.
exam_tip: L = CL x q x S, where q = 0.5 x rho x V squared. The three functions are: CL (aerofoil efficiency), q (dynamic pressure), and S (wing area). Know this formula cold.
key_facts: Lift formula: L = CL x 0.5 x rho x V squared x S
CL = coefficient of lift (aerodynamic shape/angle factor)
q = 0.5 x rho x V squared = dynamic pressure (airflow energy factor)
S = wing area (size factor)
In level flight, L = W, so any change in one variable requires compensation from others

syllabus_desc: Explain the meaning of coefficient of lift (CL).
concept: The coefficient of lift (CL) is a dimensionless number that describes how effectively a wing is producing lift at a given angle of attack. It captures everything about the wing's aerodynamic efficiency that isn't already covered by dynamic pressure and wing area. CL varies primarily with angle of attack -- it increases approximately linearly as alpha increases, reaching a maximum value (CLmax) at the critical angle of attack, then drops sharply at the stall. CL also depends on aerofoil shape, camber, surface condition, Reynolds number, and the use of high-lift devices. A CL of 1.0 doesn't mean the wing is generating one newton of lift -- it means the lift equals the dynamic pressure times the wing area. Typical CLmax values range from about 1.2 to 1.6 for clean wings, and up to 3.0 or more with full flaps and slats.
exam_tip: CL is dimensionless. It increases linearly with alpha until CLmax at the stalling angle. Higher CLmax means a lower stalling speed for a given weight.
key_facts: CL is a dimensionless coefficient representing the wing's lift-producing efficiency
CL increases approximately linearly with angle of attack up to CLmax
CLmax occurs at the critical (stalling) angle of attack
Beyond the stalling angle, CL drops sharply
Typical CLmax: 1.2-1.6 clean, up to 3.0+ with high-lift devices deployed

syllabus_desc: Describe the variation of CL with angle of attack for a symmetrical and non-symmetrical aerofoil section.
concept: For a non-symmetrical (cambered) aerofoil, CL is non-zero at zero AoA — the camber generates lift even with no angle of incidence. As AoA increases, CL increases linearly up to the critical angle, then drops sharply at stall. For a symmetrical aerofoil, CL is zero at zero AoA and increases linearly with AoA in the same way up to the stall angle. The CL-alpha (lift slope) is approximately the same for both — about 0.1 per degree of AoA. The stall angle is similar for both types.
exam_tip: CL vs AoA: cambered aerofoil has non-zero CL at zero AoA. Symmetrical aerofoil: CL = 0 at zero AoA. Both increase linearly then stall similarly.
key_facts: Cambered aerofoil: CL is positive at zero AoA (camber provides lift)
Symmetrical aerofoil: CL = 0 at zero AoA (zero lift angle = 0°)
Both increase linearly with AoA at approximately 0.1 CL per degree
Stall AoA is similar for both types (typically 12–16°)
CL at stall (CLmax) may be slightly higher for cambered aerofoils

syllabus_desc: Given a CL graph of symmetrical and non-symmetrical aerofoil against angle of attack, show the zero lift angle.
concept: The zero lift angle is the angle of attack at which the aerofoil generates zero lift. For a symmetrical aerofoil, this is exactly 0° — the chord line aligned with the relative airflow produces no lift due to symmetry. For a cambered aerofoil, the zero lift angle is negative (below zero) — the chord line must be tilted slightly downward relative to the airflow before lift becomes zero. On a CL vs AoA graph, the zero lift angle is where the CL line crosses the AoA axis.
exam_tip: Zero lift angle: where CL = 0 on the graph. Symmetrical aerofoil: 0°. Cambered aerofoil: negative angle (below zero). Find it on the graph where the line crosses the AoA axis.
key_facts: Zero lift angle: AoA at which CL = 0
Symmetrical aerofoil: zero lift angle = 0°
Cambered aerofoil: zero lift angle is negative (below zero AoA)
On CL vs AoA graph: where the CL line crosses the x-axis
Rotor blades (symmetrical section): zero lift at zero AoA

syllabus_desc: Given a CL graph of symmetrical and non-symmetrical aerofoil against angle of attack, show the angle for maximum CL (CL max).
concept: The angle for maximum CL (CLmax) is the critical angle of attack — the point at which lift is greatest before the boundary layer separates and the aerofoil stalls. On a CL vs AoA graph, this is the peak of the curve. Beyond this angle, CL drops sharply (stall). The critical angle is typically 12–16° for most aerofoil sections. For a rotor blade, operating beyond CLmax on any section constitutes a local blade stall.
