As the disc moves through fresh air, induced velocity drops, inflow angle decreases, and the blade sections operate more efficiently. Most helicopters experience a noticeable 'lift into translational lift' at around 10–20 knots, where the rotor suddenly needs less power for the same thrust — or can produce more thrust for the same power.
exam_tip: Translational lift = efficiency gain from forward flight reducing induced velocity. It typically kicks in around 10–20 kt and is felt as a sudden lightening of controls or climb tendency. It's caused by reduced induced velocity, not by wing-like lift on the fuselage.
key_facts: Additional aerodynamic efficiency gained by transitioning into forward flight
Caused by reduced induced velocity as disc moves through undisturbed air
Typically felt at 10–20 kt (varies by helicopter type)
Reduces power required for same thrust, or allows more thrust for same power
Inflow angle decreases → more efficient blade operation

syllabus_desc: Explain how translational lift affects inflow angle; angle of attack; rotor thrust and the power required to overcome rotor drag in level flight.
concept: Translational lift affects multiple aspects of the rotor's operation simultaneously. As induced velocity drops: (1) the inflow angle decreases — this increases the effective angle of attack, giving more lift from the same blade pitch; (2) the angle of attack of the blade sections increases, putting the blade into a more efficient lift range; (3) rotor thrust increases for the same collective setting, or the same thrust can be maintained with less collective; and (4) the power required to overcome rotor drag decreases — because less induced power is needed. The pilot typically responds by slightly reducing collective to maintain level flight through translational lift.
exam_tip: All four effects occur simultaneously when translational lift kicks in: inflow angle down, AoA up, rotor thrust up, rotor drag power requirement down. Know the direction of each change for exam questions.
key_facts: Translational lift: induced velocity decreases as airspeed increases
Inflow angle decreases → effective AoA increases for same blade pitch
AoA increase → more lift (rotor thrust increases)
Rotor drag power requirement decreases (less induced power needed)
Pilot reduces collective to maintain level flight as helicopter transitions

syllabus_desc: Explain the principle of operation of delta-3 hinges and offset pitch horns in reducing blade flapping.
concept: Delta-3 hinges and offset pitch horns are design features that reduce blade flapping amplitude in response to control inputs and aerodynamic loads. A delta-3 hinge is an inclined flapping hinge — when a blade flaps up, the geometry automatically reduces its pitch angle (and vice versa), creating an inherent feedback that damps flapping. Offset pitch horns achieve a similar result mechanically: when the blade flaps, the pitch horn geometry causes a pitch-reducing input. Both designs reduce the tendency for blades to flap excessively when cyclic is applied, improving control response and reducing vibration.
exam_tip: Delta-3 hinges and offset pitch horns: flapping up → pitch decreases (feedback coupling), which damps further flapping. This reduces blade flapping amplitude without pilot input. They are passive, geometric feedback mechanisms — not active control systems.
key_facts: Delta-3 hinge: inclined flapping hinge creates flap-pitch coupling
When blade flaps up, pitch angle automatically reduces (negative feedback)
Offset pitch horn: geometric arrangement achieves similar pitch-flap coupling
Both features reduce flapping amplitude passively
Important in tail rotor design and semi-rigid main rotor systems

syllabus_desc: Explain, with the aid of a diagram, the function of the anti-torque tail rotor.
concept: The tail rotor is a small rotor mounted vertically at the tail of most single-rotor helicopters, producing thrust to counteract the torque reaction of the main rotor on the fuselage. Newton's third law: the engine applies torque to spin the main rotor, so the fuselage experiences an equal and opposite reaction torque. Without the tail rotor, the fuselage would spin in the opposite direction to the main rotor. The pilot controls tail rotor pitch with the anti-torque pedals (left/right), which change the tail rotor thrust to yaw the aircraft.
exam_tip: The tail rotor counteracts main rotor torque (reaction torque on fuselage). It also yaws the helicopter. Anti-torque pedals = tail rotor pitch control. In hover, increasing collective = more torque = more right pedal needed (in most US-built helicopters with counterclockwise main rotor when viewed from above).
key_facts: Produces sideways thrust to counteract main rotor torque reaction
Without tail rotor, fuselage rotates opposite to main rotor
Pilot controls via anti-torque pedals (left/right)
Increasing main rotor torque (more collective) = more tail rotor thrust needed
Also provides directional (yaw) control

syllabus_desc: Explain the effect of anti-torque on: power required.
concept: The tail rotor requires a measurable portion of the total engine power — typically 7–12% in hover, increasing with main rotor torque (collective pitch). As you raise the collective and need more anti-torque, the tail rotor demands more power, which comes directly at the expense of main rotor performance. In a tail rotor failure scenario at high collective settings, the sudden loss of anti-torque is compounded by the power available to the main rotor suddenly having no competing demand — but the fuselage will yaw rapidly.
exam_tip: Tail rotor power is a direct cost of single-rotor design — typically 7–12% of total power in hover. More collective = more torque = more tail rotor thrust required = more power consumed. This is one reason tandem rotor and NOTAR designs were developed.
key_facts: Tail rotor consumes engine power (typically 7–12% in hover)
More main rotor collective → more torque → more tail rotor power needed
Reduces power available to main rotor
Power demand increases with density altitude (torque increases)
Tandem/coaxial/NOTAR designs eliminate this power penalty

syllabus_desc: Explain the effect of anti-torque on: rotor rpm.
concept: The tail rotor operates at a fixed gear ratio to the main rotor, so main rotor RPM directly determines tail rotor RPM. In normal operation this isn't a concern, but if the main rotor RPM decays (due to engine failure or low collective during manoeuvre), tail rotor RPM also decreases. This reduces tail rotor thrust at exactly the moment when the pilot might need it most — such as during an autorotation entry when the fuselage may yaw. Conversely, overspeeding the main rotor overspeeds the tail rotor.
exam_tip: Tail rotor RPM is geared to main rotor RPM — they rise and fall together. Low main rotor RPM = reduced tail rotor effectiveness. This is critical during autorotation and engine failures. Know this relationship for anti-torque control in emergency procedures.
key_facts: Tail rotor RPM is geared to main rotor RPM (fixed ratio)
Main rotor RPM decrease → tail rotor RPM decrease → reduced anti-torque effectiveness
Critical during autorotation entry (main rotor RPM may initially decay)
Overspeed of main rotor: tail rotor also overspeeds
Cannot control tail rotor RPM independently of main rotor

syllabus_desc: Explain the effect of the wind on tail rotor thrust.
concept: Wind affects tail rotor thrust in several ways. A crosswind from the right (for a helicopter with a counterclockwise main rotor) can effectively 'assist' the tail rotor by pushing against the fuselage, requiring less tail rotor thrust and therefore less left pedal input. A crosswind from the left creates an 'adverse' weathervaning effect, requiring more right pedal. In a hover, wind direction determines how much pedal is needed, and critical situations can arise when the wind is from a direction that reduces effective tail rotor thrust — especially in a right quartering tailwind for many US-type helicopters.
exam_tip: Wind can assist or oppose tail rotor effectiveness depending on its direction relative to the tail. A right quartering tailwind is particularly hazardous for US-type helicopters (CCW main rotor) as it effectively reduces tail rotor thrust margin, increasing LTE risk. Always assess wind direction in relation to anti-torque requirements.
key_facts: Crosswind affects tail rotor effectiveness and required pedal input
Wind from right (CCW rotor heli): assists anti-torque, less pedal needed
Wind from left: opposes anti-torque, more pedal needed
Right quartering tailwind: reduces tail rotor effectiveness — loss of tail rotor effectiveness (LTE) risk
Hovering in crosswinds requires increased attention to anti-torque margins

syllabus_desc: Explain the meaning of: translating tendency (tail rotor drift).
concept: Translating tendency (also called tail rotor drift) is the sideways drift of the helicopter in a hover caused by tail rotor thrust. The tail rotor pushes the tail to the left (for a CCW main rotor helicopter), which means the nose yaws right — but the whole aircraft also drifts to the right (the direction of tail rotor thrust). This is a purely mechanical consequence of having a sideways-thrusting tail rotor. To counteract it, most helicopters are built with the rotor mast tilted slightly left, or the pilot applies a small left cyclic correction.
exam_tip: Translating tendency = helicopter drifts in the direction of tail rotor thrust in hover. For CCW main rotor: tail rotor pushes left, aircraft drifts right. Countered by mast tilt or left cyclic. Know the direction for your helicopter type.
key_facts: Sideways drift in hover due to tail rotor thrust
For CCW main rotor: tail rotor thrust is to the left → aircraft drifts right
A consequence of horizontal component of tail rotor thrust
Countered by: rotor mast tilt, left cyclic, or lateral CG offset
Must be corrected to maintain position in hover

syllabus_desc: Explain the meaning of: rolling tendency (tail rotor roll).
concept: Rolling tendency (tail rotor roll) occurs because the tail rotor is mounted higher than the main rotor mast and CG. The sideways tail rotor thrust creates a couple — a rolling moment — because the thrust line is not through the CG. For a CCW rotor helicopter, the tail rotor pushes left (and the aircraft drifts right) while also tending to roll the aircraft to the left (low left skid). Most helicopters have the main rotor mast tilted slightly to the right to counteract this rolling tendency, so in a hover you'll usually have slightly unequal skid heights.
exam_tip: Tail rotor roll: because tail rotor is above the CG, its thrust creates a rolling couple. For CCW rotor helicopter: tends to roll left (lower left skid). Countered by main rotor mast tilt. Know which skid tends to be lower and why.
key_facts: Sideways tail rotor thrust creates a rolling moment about the CG
Tail rotor is above the CG → force creates a couple
For CCW rotor: tail rotor pushes left → rolling tendency to the left
Results in left skid being lower in hover (CCW rotor types)
Countered by main rotor shaft tilt or collective offset

syllabus_desc: Explain design techniques that can compensate for translating and rolling tendencies.
concept: Designers compensate for translating and rolling tendencies through several techniques: (1) tilting the main rotor mast slightly to one side, so the thrust vector of the main rotor has a lateral component that counters tail rotor drift; (2) offsetting the CG laterally through component placement; (3) tilting the tail rotor thrust line slightly — some designs have a tail rotor that isn't perfectly vertical; and (4) some aircraft use a collective bias so the mast tilt doesn't cause unwanted lateral movement. The pilot may need to apply slight lateral cyclic to remain in position.
exam_tip: Design compensations for translating/rolling tendencies: mast tilt (most common), CG offset, and tail rotor thrust line inclination. In practice, expect to hold slight lateral cyclic to counter remaining effects. Know what 'mast tilt' corrects.
key_facts: Main rotor mast tilt: tilted laterally to provide countering force
CG offset: components placed to counteract lateral drift
Tail rotor thrust inclination: some designs tilt tail rotor thrust line
Pilot correction: slight lateral cyclic in hover
All designs are a compromise between hover position stability and translating tendency

syllabus_desc: Explain the influence of ground effect on the amount of tail rotor thrust required.
concept: In ground effect, the reduced induced flow (described earlier) means the main rotor is more efficient and requires less collective pitch for a given weight. This also means less torque is produced by the main rotor — which in turn requires less tail rotor thrust to maintain heading. The practical implication: when climbing out of ground effect, you need more power, which creates more torque, and your pedal input will need to change. Pilots transitioning from IGE hover to OGE hover often notice the need for more right pedal (CCW rotor) and more power simultaneously.
exam_tip: In ground effect: less collective needed → less torque → less tail rotor thrust required. Climbing through effective translational lift to OGE: more collective → more torque → more pedal. This transition is a critical management point, especially at max gross weight.
key_facts: IGE hover: rotor is more efficient, less collective needed
Less collective → less torque → less tail rotor thrust required
OGE: more collective needed → more torque → more tail rotor thrust required
Transition from IGE to OGE: increase pedal input simultaneously with collective
Critical at high weight/high altitude — power margin may be limited

syllabus_desc: Explain the effect of a tail rotor failure in flight.
concept: A tail rotor failure in flight can range from a fixed-pitch failure (tail rotor stuck at a given pitch setting) to a complete loss of tail rotor drive. The hazard depends on flight condition: in forward flight at cruise speed, the fuselage becomes directionally stable (like a weathervane) and the helicopter may be flyable without full anti-torque control. Reducing power (and thus torque) reduces the yaw tendency. In hover or low-speed flight, the loss of anti-torque causes rapid yaw — the most dangerous scenario. Altitude and airspeed management become the priority.
exam_tip: Tail rotor failure is most dangerous in hover/low speed — rapid uncontrolled yaw. In forward flight at speed, directional stability can help. Reduce power to reduce torque-induced yaw. Know the difference between a fixed-pitch and complete drive failure for your aircraft type.
key_facts: Most dangerous in hover/low-speed flight — rapid uncontrolled yaw
In forward flight at speed: fuselage weathervanes, may be controllable
Reduce power to reduce torque-induced yaw
Fixed pitch failure: yaw is constant (torque still exists)
Complete drive failure: progressive yaw without tail rotor authority

syllabus_desc: Describe pilot actions that may eliminate or reduce the effects of tail rotor failure in flight.
concept: Pilot actions for tail rotor failure depend on the type and flight regime. In forward flight: reduce power, autorotate or enter steep glide to reduce torque; land as soon as possible. In hover: immediately lower collective to reduce torque (and therefore yaw tendency), which causes a descent — but a controlled landing is preferable to an uncontrolled spin. For a running landing: maintain forward airspeed (above ETL) to use directional stability, then reduce power on touchdown. For an autorotative landing: as rotor RPM increases during descent, the torque from the engine is removed, so tail rotor authority is no longer needed — this is the 'safest' end state.
exam_tip: Tail rotor failure actions: reduce power, autorotate, land ASAP. In hover: lower collective immediately to reduce torque and yaw rate (accept descent). Running/rolling landing is preferred over a hover landing if tail rotor drive is lost. Each helicopter has its own specific emergency procedures.
key_facts: Forward flight: reduce power, maintain speed above ETL, land ASAP
Hover: lower collective immediately to reduce torque → accept descent rate
Running landing: maintain forward speed for directional stability, reduce power on touchdown
Autorotation: engine off → no torque → tail rotor not needed
Always follow the specific emergency procedures for your aircraft type

syllabus_desc: With the aid of a diagram, identify and explain the functions of: collective pitch/control.
concept: Collective pitch control changes the pitch angle of all rotor blades simultaneously and equally, increasing or decreasing the total rotor thrust. When you raise the collective lever, all blades feather to a higher pitch angle, increasing lift across the whole disc — the helicopter climbs or accelerates vertically. Simultaneously, the increased blade drag requires more power, which is why the throttle is usually correlated with the collective. In practice, the collective controls altitude (in hover) and airspeed/climb in forward flight.
exam_tip: Collective changes all blade pitch equally and simultaneously → changes total rotor thrust. Raising collective = more thrust = climb/more power needed. In hover: collective = altitude control. Correlated throttle compensates for changing power demand.
key_facts: Changes all blade pitch angles equally and simultaneously
Raises/lowers total rotor thrust
Used to control altitude (hover) and climb/descent rate (forward flight)
Increasing collective → more induced drag → more engine power required
Usually correlated with throttle to maintain RPM

syllabus_desc: With the aid of a diagram, identify and explain the functions of: cyclic pitch/control.
concept: Cyclic pitch control changes the pitch angle of each blade periodically as it rotates around the disc — one side gets more pitch, the other gets less. This causes differential lift across the disc, which makes the disc tilt. The disc tilts in the direction of the cyclic input, which tilts the total rotor thrust vector, creating a horizontal force component that moves the helicopter forward, backward, or sideways. In a hover, cyclic = attitude control; in forward flight, cyclic primarily controls airspeed and climb/descent attitude.
exam_tip: Cyclic = periodic pitch change → differential lift → disc tilts → horizontal force component. Forward cyclic: disc tilts forward, TRT tilts forward, helicopter accelerates forward. Cyclic controls attitude; the disc tilt drives movement.
key_facts: Changes blade pitch periodically as blades rotate
Creates differential lift across the disc
Causes disc to tilt in direction of cyclic input
Tilts total rotor thrust → horizontal force component
Controls attitude/direction in hover; airspeed/attitude in forward flight

syllabus_desc: With the aid of a diagram, identify and explain the functions of: swashplate (control orbit).
concept: The swashplate (or control orbit) is the mechanical assembly that translates pilot inputs from the stationary control system into the rotating blade pitch changes. It consists of two plates — a non-rotating (stationary) lower plate and a rotating upper plate — connected by bearings. The collective input tilts or raises/lowers the entire swashplate; cyclic input tilts it. The rotating plate connects via pitch links to each blade's pitch horn, transferring the swashplate motion into blade feathering.
exam_tip: Swashplate = the translation mechanism between fixed controls and rotating blades. Lower plate: non-rotating (connected to cockpit controls). Upper plate: rotates with rotor, connects to pitch links/horns. Collective raises/lowers the whole assembly; cyclic tilts it.
key_facts: Two-part assembly: non-rotating lower plate and rotating upper plate
Connected by bearings allowing relative rotation
Collective input: raises/lowers entire swashplate
Cyclic input: tilts the swashplate
Rotating upper plate connects to pitch links → blade pitch horns

syllabus_desc: With the aid of a diagram, identify and explain the functions of: stationary star (non-rotating plate).
concept: The stationary star (non-rotating plate) is the lower half of the swashplate assembly. It's connected to the helicopter's fixed structure and does not rotate with the rotor. Pilot inputs through the collective and cyclic are fed directly to this plate via pushrods and mixing levers. Its movement (tilt and vertical position) is replicated by the rotating upper plate via the bearing between them.
exam_tip: The stationary star is connected to the fixed airframe — it doesn't rotate. Cockpit inputs go here first. The bearing between stationary and rotating plates allows the rotating plate to follow swashplate position while spinning with the rotor.
key_facts: Lower half of swashplate assembly
Does not rotate with rotor
Receives collective and cyclic inputs from cockpit via pushrods
Connected to rotating plate via bearing
Sets the plane and position that the rotating plate replicates

syllabus_desc: With the aid of a diagram, identify and explain the functions of: rotating star (rotating plate).
concept: The rotating star (rotating plate) is the upper half of the swashplate. It rotates with the main rotor at the same speed. It replicates the position and tilt of the stationary plate via the bearing between them, but because it rotates, it translates that position into periodic pitch changes at each blade as they pass through different disc positions. The pitch links connect the rotating plate to each blade's pitch horn.
exam_tip: The rotating star spins with the rotor and replicates the swashplate tilt/position. Because it rotates, blades get more pitch when their pitch link is higher (where the plate is tilted up) and less when it's lower. This is how cyclic creates periodic pitch changes.
key_facts: Upper half of swashplate, rotates with main rotor
Replicates stationary plate position via bearing between them
As it rotates, each blade pitch link rises/falls with disc tilt
Creates periodic pitch change corresponding to cyclic input direction
Connected to pitch horns on each blade via pitch links

syllabus_desc: With the aid of a diagram, identify and explain the functions of: pitch link.
concept: Pitch links are rigid rods connecting the rotating plate of the swashplate to the pitch horns on each rotor blade. They translate the vertical movement of the rotating plate into a rotation of each blade about its feathering axis. When the rotating plate is tilted by cyclic input, pitch links on one side rise and those on the other side descend as they rotate, causing periodic blade pitch changes. The geometry and length of the pitch links affect the overall control system sensitivity and swashplate travel required.
exam_tip: Pitch links = the mechanical connection between the rotating swashplate plate and each blade's pitch horn. They convert swashplate vertical position into blade feathering (pitch change). Length and attachment point affect control geometry and response.
key_facts: Rigid rods connecting rotating swashplate plate to blade pitch horns
Convert swashplate vertical movement into blade feathering rotation
One pitch link per blade
As swashplate tilts, links on one side rise while others descend → periodic pitch change
Link geometry affects control sensitivity and swashplate travel

syllabus_desc: With the aid of a diagram, identify and explain the functions of: pitch horn.
concept: The pitch horn is a lever arm attached to each rotor blade at the feathering axis, extending roughly perpendicular to the blade span. The pitch link attaches to the end of the pitch horn. When the pitch link pushes up or pulls down on the pitch horn, the blade rotates about its feathering axis, changing its pitch angle. The length of the pitch horn determines the mechanical advantage — a longer pitch horn requires less pitch link travel for the same blade pitch change, while a shorter one gives more direct response.
exam_tip: The pitch horn is the lever arm attached to each blade at the feathering axis. Pitch link pushes/pulls on it → blade feathers. Horn length determines mechanical advantage and sensitivity. Know this as the final link in the control chain from swashplate to blade pitch.
key_facts: Lever arm attached to each blade at the feathering axis
Pitch link attaches to outer end of pitch horn
Push/pull by pitch link → rotates blade about feathering axis
Horn length determines mechanical advantage
Last mechanical link in the chain: cockpit → mixing unit → swashplate → pitch link → pitch horn → blade

syllabus_desc: Explain the use of a control orbit in applying cyclic and collective pitch.
concept: The control orbit (swashplate) applies both cyclic and collective simultaneously. For collective: raising the orbit raises all pitch links equally around the disc, increasing all blade pitch by the same amount. For cyclic: tilting the orbit means pitch links on one side of the disc are raised and on the other side are lowered. As each blade passes through a complete revolution, its pitch link rises and falls, creating the periodic blade pitch variation that tilts the disc. The two inputs can be applied simultaneously — the swashplate both tilts (for cyclic) and rises (for collective) at the same time.
exam_tip: The control orbit (swashplate) applies collective by vertical movement and cyclic by tilting. The two effects are independent and additive — raising and tilting simultaneously gives a combined collective + cyclic input. Mixing units in the control system may combine these before reaching the swashplate.
key_facts: Collective: swashplate rises/falls → all pitch links move equally → all blades pitch same amount
Cyclic: swashplate tilts → pitch links on one side higher, other side lower → periodic pitch change
Both inputs can be applied simultaneously (additive effect)
Phase of disc tilt corresponds to direction of cyclic input
Mixing levers below swashplate may combine collective and cyclic inputs

syllabus_desc: Explain the effect of collective and cyclic control movement on swashplate movement, total rotor thrust and disc orientation.
concept: Collective movement raises or lowers the entire swashplate, increasing or decreasing all blade pitch equally — this increases or decreases total rotor thrust without changing disc orientation. Cyclic movement tilts the swashplate, causing a periodic pitch variation that tilts the disc toward the direction of applied cyclic. Disc tilt changes the direction of total rotor thrust, giving the horizontal force component for directional movement. So: collective changes magnitude of TRT; cyclic changes direction of TRT.
exam_tip: Collective changes TRT magnitude (how much thrust). Cyclic changes TRT direction (which way the disc tilts). Both together give complete control of where the helicopter goes and how fast. Know this split clearly for exam questions.
key_facts: Collective: changes all blade pitch equally → changes TRT magnitude
Cyclic: tilts swashplate → periodic pitch change → disc tilt → TRT direction change
Collective = vertical control; Cyclic = directional/attitude control
TRT always acts perpendicular to tip path plane
Tilting disc forward = forward TRT component = forward flight

syllabus_desc: Explain the effect of control input on blade lead/lag behaviour in fully articulated rotor systems.
concept: In a fully articulated rotor system (with lead-lag hinges), applying a control input — particularly cyclic — causes a change in blade pitch. As blade pitch increases, blade lift and drag change, which causes the blade to lead or lag slightly about its drag hinge due to the change in torque and angular momentum. The drag dampers absorb this energy. This coupling between blade pitch and lead-lag behaviour is important for understanding vibration, blade tracking, and why damper serviceability is so critical — failed dampers can lead to ground resonance.
exam_tip: Control input → pitch change → changed aerodynamic forces → lead/lag blade movement. Drag dampers absorb these movements. Damper failure allows excessive lead-lag oscillation — the cause of ground resonance. Know this chain for hazardous condition questions.
key_facts: Control inputs change blade pitch → change aerodynamic load on blade
Changed load alters blade's angular momentum → causes lead or lag
Drag dampers on each blade absorb lead-lag energy
In flight: dampers control oscillation and prevent resonance
Failed or degraded dampers → uncontrolled lead-lag → potential ground resonance

syllabus_desc: Explain the following causes of movement about the lead/lag hinge: conservation of angular momentum (Coriolis effect).
concept: As a blade flaps up, it effectively moves closer to the axis of rotation (its mass distribution shifts inward), and as it flaps down, it moves further out. By conservation of angular momentum (the ice-skater effect), when the blade moves inward (flaps up), it tends to speed up rotationally — it leads. When it flaps down and moves outward, it slows down — it lags. This Coriolis effect is the primary driver of lead-lag motion in articulated rotor blades and is why drag dampers are essential.
exam_tip: Coriolis effect on rotor blades: flapping changes the effective radius of the blade mass, causing acceleration or deceleration in the plane of rotation. Flap up = move inward = lead; flap down = move outward = lag. This is identical to the ice-skater pulling arms in to spin faster.
key_facts: Flapping changes the effective radius of blade mass from axis of rotation
Flap up → mass moves inward → angular momentum conserved → blade speeds up (leads)
Flap down → mass moves outward → angular momentum conserved → blade slows (lags)
Primary driver of lead-lag motion on articulated rotors
Drag dampers absorb the resulting oscillatory force

syllabus_desc: Explain the following causes of movement about the lead/lag hinge: Hookes joint effect.
concept: The Hooke's joint effect (also called 'two per rev' effect) occurs because the rotor shaft drives the blade via a universal joint geometry. When the disc is tilted relative to the shaft, the coupling between the shaft rotation and the blade rotation is not perfectly uniform — it creates a twice-per-revolution (2P) speed variation at the blade, causing periodic lead-lag. This is a second-order effect compared to Coriolis but becomes more significant as disc tilt increases.
exam_tip: Hooke's joint effect: disc tilt relative to shaft creates 2P lead-lag oscillations. Less significant than Coriolis at normal disc tilts but can contribute to vibration. Examiners may ask you to list causes of lead-lag motion.
key_facts: Caused by universal joint geometry when disc is tilted relative to shaft
Creates twice-per-revolution (2P) periodic velocity variation
Causes 2P lead-lag oscillations
More significant with greater disc tilt
A secondary cause of lead-lag motion after Coriolis

syllabus_desc: Explain the following causes of movement about the lead/lag hinge: periodic drag changes.
concept: Periodic drag changes occur as blades complete each rotation — in forward flight, the advancing blade experiences higher airspeed (more aerodynamic drag) while the retreating blade is slower (less drag). This cyclic variation in drag loads causes the blades to lead when drag is low (higher airspeed sections) and lag when drag is high. These periodic drag variations feed into the lead-lag motion and must be absorbed by the drag dampers.
exam_tip: In forward flight, blades experience varying drag as they advance and retreat. Higher drag on the advancing blade side causes a lag tendency; lower drag on retreating side allows leading. This is an in-flight, periodic source of lead-lag loading.
key_facts: In forward flight: advancing blade has higher TAS → more aerodynamic drag
Retreating blade: lower TAS → less drag
Periodic drag change causes lead-lag oscillation at 1P frequency
Absorbed by drag dampers
Increases with forward airspeed as dissymmetry of drag increases

syllabus_desc: Explain the following causes of movement about the lead/lag hinge: random changes.
concept: Random changes in lead-lag occur due to turbulence, gusts, and other transient aerodynamic loads that don't have a regular periodic pattern. These random inputs can excite the natural lead-lag frequency of the blade/damper system. If the dampers are worn or not functioning correctly, random excitation can grow rather than damp out — particularly during ground running where the rotor/airframe system is most vulnerable to resonance.
exam_tip: Random lead-lag changes from turbulence and gusts are unpredictable and can excite rotor resonance if dampers are ineffective. Most significant during start-up, shutdown, and ground running when rotor RPM passes through resonance speeds.
key_facts: Caused by turbulence, gusts, and non-periodic aerodynamic loads
Random in frequency and amplitude
Can excite natural frequency of lead-lag/damper system
Most hazardous during ground operation (ground resonance risk)
Underscores importance of functional drag dampers at all times

syllabus_desc: Explain phase lag and advance angle.
concept: Phase lag is the delay between when a cyclic input is applied and when the disc responds — due to the gyroscopic behaviour of the spinning rotor. A gyroscope precesses 90° from the applied force: if you push down on the front of the disc (by increasing pitch at the front), the disc actually tilts to the right (for a CCW rotor). To account for this, the swashplate is rigged so that cyclic inputs are applied 90° ahead of the desired disc tilt. The advance angle is the angle by which the pitch change is applied before the blade reaches the desired tilt point, to compensate for phase lag and other geometric effects.
exam_tip: Phase lag = gyroscopic precession of the rotor disc (90° for a pure gyroscope). Advance angle = the amount by which the swashplate rigging pre-compensates for phase lag, so that forward cyclic produces forward disc tilt. This is why the pitch horn doesn't point straight forward.
key_facts: Phase lag: gyroscopic precession — disc responds 90° from applied force
Applied cyclic force → rotor precesses 90° later in rotation
Advance angle: swashplate rigged to compensate for phase lag
For a CCW rotor: pitch control rigged to apply pitch ~90° before desired disc tilt
Net result: pushing forward cyclic tilts the disc forward (not sideways)

syllabus_desc: State the two main categories of vibration in helicopters.
concept: The two main categories of helicopter vibration are: (1) low-frequency (LF) vibrations, which are caused by rotor imbalance, blade tracking errors, and aerodynamic forces at 1P (once per revolution) and 2P frequencies; and (2) medium/high-frequency (MF/HF) vibrations, which originate from the tail rotor, engine, and gearbox at higher frequencies. Understanding which category a vibration falls into helps identify its source — 1P is always at main rotor frequency; 4P is common for 4-bladed rotors; engine/gearbox frequencies are much higher.
exam_tip: Two main categories: low-frequency (rotor-sourced, 1P-4P) and high-frequency (engine/gearbox/tail rotor). Vibration frequency identifies the source: count vibrations per second and compare to rotor RPM. LF vibrations are felt more physically; HF vibrations may show more in instruments.
key_facts: Category 1: Low-frequency (rotor-sourced) — 1P, 2P, and nP (n = number of blades)
Category 2: High-frequency — engine, gearbox, tail rotor, accessories
1P vibration: main rotor frequency, usually due to imbalance or tracking error
nP vibration: number of blades × RPM frequency, common with all blades interacting
Engine/tail rotor vibrations: much higher frequency, felt as buzz/tremor

syllabus_desc: Explain the causes and characteristics of vertical vibrations and lateral vibrations.
concept: Vertical vibrations occur in the vertical plane — an up-and-down oscillation felt through the seat and cyclic. They are usually caused by 1P vertical forces from blade imbalance or out-of-track blades. Lateral vibrations occur side-to-side and are most commonly caused by tail rotor imbalance or by lateral forces at 2P. Both types have characteristic felt signatures: vertical vibrations are obvious to the occupants as a bouncing sensation; lateral vibrations feel like a shimmy or shake side-to-side.
exam_tip: Vertical vibrations = up/down, usually 1P from main rotor imbalance or out-of-track blades. Lateral vibrations = side-to-side, often from tail rotor or 2P forces. Distinguishing these by feel is a key flight skill. Report any unusual vibrations to maintenance.
key_facts: Vertical vibrations: up/down oscillation, 1P, from blade imbalance or tracking error
Lateral vibrations: side-to-side, often from tail rotor imbalance or 2P forces
Vertical: felt as bouncing/rhythmic up-down sensation
Lateral: felt as shimmy or shake
Both indicate a maintenance issue requiring investigation

syllabus_desc: Describe the causes and characteristics of high frequency vibrations and engine vibrations.
concept: High-frequency vibrations come from the engine (at engine RPM frequency), gearbox meshing frequencies, and tail rotor (tail rotor RPM × number of blades). These are characterised by a buzz or tremor rather than a physical bounce, and may show up more clearly in instrument vibration than as a felt sensation. Engine vibrations can indicate engine roughness, misfiring, or imbalance. Gearbox vibrations may indicate gear wear, chip contamination, or bearing failure.
exam_tip: High-frequency vibrations: engine, gearbox, tail rotor. Felt as buzz/tremor, not physical bounce. Engine vibration = engine problem; gearbox vibration = gearbox problem. High-frequency vibes should trigger an immediate precautionary landing assessment.
key_facts: High-frequency vibrations sourced from engine, gearbox, and tail rotor
Engine vibration: misfire, imbalance, bearing wear
Gearbox vibration: gear wear, chip contamination, bearing issues
Tail rotor vibration: imbalance, blade damage, bearing failure
Felt as buzz or tremor; may appear in instruments rather than physically

syllabus_desc: Describe recommended pilot actions when confronted with vibrations.
concept: When confronted with abnormal vibrations, the recommended pilot actions are: (1) identify the vibration category (low or high frequency) and probable source; (2) reduce collective (and thus rotor loads) if safe to do so — lower RPM/power often reduces vibration amplitude; (3) perform a precautionary landing as soon as practical — vibrations that change in character or increase should be treated as an emergency; (4) do not attempt to fly until the cause is identified and rectified. Vibrations that develop suddenly or worsen rapidly may indicate imminent structural or mechanical failure.
exam_tip: Vibration actions: identify, reduce load if possible, land ASAP. Vibration that suddenly develops or worsens = emergency. Never wait to see if it gets better — mechanical deterioration can be rapid. Know this as a standard abnormal procedure.
key_facts: Identify vibration frequency and probable source
Reduce power/collective if safe — reduces aerodynamic loads
Perform precautionary landing as soon as practical
Sudden onset or worsening vibration = treat as emergency, land immediately
Do not continue flight — vibrations may indicate imminent mechanical failure

syllabus_desc: Define hover.
concept: A hover is a flight condition in which the helicopter maintains a stationary position relative to the ground (or a fixed reference), with no translational velocity and minimal vertical movement. In a hover, all forces are in equilibrium: total rotor thrust equals weight (vertical equilibrium), tail rotor thrust equals main rotor torque (directional equilibrium), and there is no net horizontal force. In practice, perfect hovering requires constant small control inputs to counteract wind, turbulence, and the inherent instability of the helicopter.
exam_tip: Hover = stationary position relative to ground, forces in equilibrium. Three equilibria in hover: vertical (TRT = weight), directional (tail rotor thrust = main rotor torque), horizontal (no net lateral/longitudinal force). Helicopters are inherently unstable in hover and require continuous correction.
key_facts: Stationary position relative to ground (or reference point)
Vertical equilibrium: Total Rotor Thrust = Weight
Directional equilibrium: Tail rotor thrust = Main rotor torque
Horizontal equilibrium: no net forward/backward/sideways force
Inherently unstable — requires continuous small control inputs

syllabus_desc: Describe the control inputs required during hovering flight.
concept: In a hover, the primary control inputs and their effects are: collective — controls altitude; anti-torque pedals — control heading (yaw); cyclic — controls position (fore-aft and lateral). Because of the gyroscopic and lag characteristics of the rotor, there is a slight lag between input and response. Additionally, the controls interact — raising the collective increases torque (requiring more pedal) and can change the attitude if not compensated. Effective hovering requires anticipating these interactions and making small, coordinated corrections.
exam_tip: Hover controls: collective = altitude, pedals = heading, cyclic = position. Controls interact — collective change requires pedal adjustment. Small, anticipatory inputs are better than large corrections. Developed through practice, not just knowledge.
key_facts: Collective: controls altitude (raise = climb, lower = descend)
Anti-torque pedals: control heading/yaw
Cyclic: controls position (fore-aft and lateral translation)
Control interactions: collective change → torque change → pedal required
Small anticipatory inputs better than large reactive corrections

syllabus_desc: Explain the meaning of in ground effect (IGE).
concept: In Ground Effect (IGE) means hovering within the aerodynamic influence of the ground surface — typically defined as hovering at a height of less than one rotor diameter AGL. In this range, the ground reduces the downward velocity of induced flow, increasing pressure below the disc and reducing the power required to hover. IGE operations are more efficient than OGE, and all helicopter performance charts distinguish between the two. Standard hover height for an IGE hover is usually around 5–10 feet AGL (skid height), though the effect begins to tail off beyond about 0.5 rotor diameters.
exam_tip: IGE = hovering within one rotor diameter AGL. Ground reduces induced velocity → less power required. Performance charts for IGE hover assume a specific height (often 5 ft skid height). Ground effect is most pronounced at lower heights and diminishes with altitude.
key_facts: IGE: hovering within approximately one rotor diameter AGL
Ground surface restricts downwash → reduces induced velocity
Lower induced velocity → less rotor drag → less power required
IGE hover charts assume a specific standard height (typically 5 ft skid height)
Effect diminishes progressively as hover height increases toward one rotor diameter

syllabus_desc: Explain the meaning of out of ground effect (OGE).
concept: Out of Ground Effect (OGE) means hovering at a height greater than one rotor diameter AGL, where the ground has no measurable influence on rotor performance. In OGE conditions, the rotor must work against its full induced flow with no 'cushion' from the ground — requiring significantly more power than an IGE hover at the same weight and conditions. The OGE hover is the limiting case for performance planning: if a helicopter can hover OGE, it can certainly hover IGE. At high density altitudes, the ability to hover OGE may be lost while IGE hover remains possible.
exam_tip: OGE = hovering more than ~1 rotor diameter AGL. Full induced velocity, no ground assistance. More power required than IGE. OGE ceiling is lower than IGE ceiling. At high DA, OGE hover may not be achievable but IGE might still be possible.
key_facts: OGE: hovering beyond one rotor diameter AGL (no ground influence)
Full induced velocity — no reduction from ground proximity
Significantly more power required than IGE hover at same conditions
OGE hover ceiling is lower than IGE hover ceiling
Performance-limiting scenario for high-altitude or high-weight operations

syllabus_desc: Explain the relationship between inflow angle and collective pitch.
concept: In hover, the inflow angle is determined by the ratio of induced flow to rotational airflow at each blade section. To maintain a given rotor thrust (to support the aircraft weight), a specific induced velocity is required. If you increase collective (higher blade pitch angle), induced flow initially increases, which increases the inflow angle — reducing the effective angle of attack unless pitch is raised further. This feedback loop means there's an equilibrium pitch angle for every weight/density altitude combination. In a high-density altitude hover, you need much more collective pitch to develop the same thrust, which significantly increases the inflow angle.
exam_tip: Inflow angle and collective pitch are interdependent in hover. More collective → more induced flow → larger inflow angle → need even more pitch to maintain AoA → higher collective again. At high DA, this cycle demands a large collective angle for the required thrust — close to the stall angle.
key_facts: Inflow angle = arctan(induced velocity / rotational airflow velocity)
Higher collective → more rotor thrust → more induced velocity
More induced velocity → larger inflow angle → reduces effective AoA
Must increase collective further to compensate → positive feedback loop
At high DA: large collective angles required → close to blade stall margin

syllabus_desc: Explain the following factors affecting ground effect: skid height AGL.
concept: Skid height AGL is the primary determinant of ground effect strength. The closer the helicopter is to the ground, the more the ground restricts downward airflow and the stronger the ground effect cushion. Ground effect is typically negligible above one rotor diameter AGL, most significant below half a rotor diameter, and maximum at very low skid heights. This is why helicopters are slightly easier to hover at low skid heights and become progressively harder to hold in position as they climb through the ground effect region.
exam_tip: Ground effect is strongest at lowest skid heights. The relationship is non-linear — most effect at very low AGL, diminishing rapidly as height increases. Standard IGE hover is at about 5 ft skid height; beyond about 0.5 rotor diameters the effect is much reduced.
key_facts: Strongest ground effect at lowest skid heights (few feet AGL)
Diminishes with height — approximately inversely related to height²
Typically negligible above one rotor diameter AGL
Most power saving at very low hover heights
Hovering at different heights within the IGE region noticeably changes power required

syllabus_desc: Explain the following factors affecting ground effect: density altitude.
concept: Higher density altitude means thinner air, which reduces the mass of air the rotor can work with per unit time. In thinner air, the rotor requires a higher induced velocity to generate the same thrust — this means the 'cushion' of compressed air below the disc in ground effect is weaker. The percentage power saving from ground effect remains roughly constant regardless of density altitude, but the absolute power required is higher at altitude, so the actual power saving in kilowatts or brake horsepower may be similar. Performance charts account for density altitude in both IGE and OGE figures.
exam_tip: High density altitude reduces the magnitude of ground effect in absolute terms (less air density, weaker pressure cushion), but the percentage power saving is approximately constant. Always use density altitude (not just elevation) when reading IGE/OGE performance charts.
key_facts: Higher density altitude = thinner air = weaker ground effect cushion
Absolute power saving from ground effect is lower at high DA
Percentage power saving remains roughly constant with DA
Contributes to reduced hover ceiling at high DA/high temperature
Always use density altitude for performance planning

syllabus_desc: Explain the following factors affecting ground effect: aircraft weight.
concept: Heavier aircraft need more rotor thrust to hover, which means more induced velocity and a stronger downwash below the disc. The ground must work harder to 'bounce' this stronger induced flow back upward to create the pressure cushion. As weight increases, the required collective pitch is higher, which increases the inflow angle and reduces the benefit of ground effect relative to the total power required. Heavy aircraft also have less margin between IGE and OGE hover — the 'extra' power from ground effect becomes proportionally less compared to total power needs.
exam_tip: Heavier aircraft: more induced velocity → stronger downwash → ground effect still works but you're closer to your power limit. Ground effect power saving is roughly proportional, but the margin between hovering and not hovering is smaller at higher weights.
key_facts: Heavier aircraft require more rotor thrust → more induced velocity
Stronger downwash: ground still restricts it but power required is higher overall
Power saving from ground effect is roughly proportional to weight
Heavier = smaller margin between IGE hover capability and OGE hover capability
At max gross weight + high DA: may only be able to hover IGE, not OGE

syllabus_desc: Explain the following factors affecting ground effect: nature of the surface.
concept: The surface below the helicopter significantly affects ground effect. Hard, smooth surfaces (sealed runway, concrete apron) reflect the rotor downwash most efficiently, providing maximum ground effect cushion. Water creates a smooth but wave-generating surface — ground effect exists but can be complicated by spray and wave patterns. Tall grass, crops, or uneven terrain allow the downwash to dissipate through the vegetation rather than being reflected cleanly — reducing ground effect. Snow can be similar to grass or may compact. When operating over uneven terrain or vegetation, expect less ground effect than over hard surfaces.
exam_tip: Smooth hard surfaces give maximum ground effect. Vegetation (grass, crops) allows downwash to dissipate rather than reflect — reducing ground effect. When hovering over grass or rough terrain, expect to need more power than over a sealed pad.
key_facts: Hard smooth surface (concrete, tarmac): maximum ground effect
Grass/crops: downwash penetrates vegetation → reduced reflection → less ground effect
Water: effective but can generate spray complicating operations
Snow: variable — can be like hard surface or like deep grass depending on depth
Over uneven/sloped terrain: one side may be closer to ground → uneven cushion

syllabus_desc: Explain the following factors affecting ground effect: slope of the surface.
concept: A sloped surface beneath the hovering helicopter creates an asymmetric ground effect — the downhill side of the disc has more distance to the ground than the uphill side. This creates uneven ground effect across the disc, which the pilot may notice as a tendency to drift toward the uphill side. More significantly, landing and taking off from sloped ground creates hazard through dynamic rollover as one skid touches or leaves the ground before the other.
exam_tip: Sloped surface: uneven ground effect — uphill side has stronger cushion. Pilots may feel a drift tendency. The main hazard of sloped ground is dynamic rollover during landing/take-off, not just uneven ground effect.
key_facts: Sloped surface creates asymmetric ground effect across the disc
Uphill side: closer to ground → stronger ground effect → more cushion on that side
Downhill side: further from ground → weaker effect
Can create a drift tendency toward the uphill side
Main hazard: dynamic rollover risk during landing/take-off on slopes

syllabus_desc: Explain the following factors affecting ground effect: the wind.
concept: Wind velocity and direction significantly affect ground effect. A headwind or tailwind improves translational lift (pushing the rotor disc into undisturbed air), but a strong wind can also disrupt the ground effect cushion by blowing the reflected downwash laterally rather than allowing it to build pressure. In crosswinds, one side of the disc may have stronger ground effect than the other, creating a rolling tendency. Wind also affects the risk of settling with power (vortex ring state) when hovering downwind with power on.
exam_tip: Wind affects ground effect by potentially disrupting the pressure cushion. A strong crosswind can blow the reflected downwash sideways, reducing the cushion. Wind direction affects anti-torque margin and LTE risk in hover. Strong tailwinds downwind hover: VRS risk.
key_facts: Wind can disrupt the ground effect pressure cushion if strong
Crosswind: reflected downwash blown sideways → asymmetric ground effect
Headwind: assists with translational lift, generally improves efficiency
Tailwind: less translational lift benefit, VRS risk if power on in descent
Wind direction relative to tail rotor affects anti-torque pedal requirements

syllabus_desc: Describe overpitching.
concept: Overpitching occurs when the helicopter is held too close to the ground with excessive collective pitch applied, trapping the helicopter in ground effect while preventing it from climbing away. The pilot applies high collective to hover or climb, but the rotor is so highly loaded that it's operating at or near the blade stall angle. The helicopter can't climb out of its own downwash recirculation and may even settle as the rotor approaches stall. This is most likely at high weight, high density altitude, or when trying to force a hover from low skid height with insufficient power.
exam_tip: Overpitching = applying too much collective for conditions, approaching blade stall and losing the ability to climb. The rotor is at or near stall angle, power is at or beyond maximum available. The helicopter won't climb and may sink. Recognise it early — once the rotor stalls, recovery is very difficult.
key_facts: Excessive collective pitch → blade AoA approaching stall
Rotor thrust cannot further increase despite more collective
Helicopter unable to climb and may begin to sink
Most likely at high weight, high DA, or insufficient power margin
Prevention: check performance data before flight; do not attempt if below OGE hover capability

syllabus_desc: Describe: the conditions likely to lead to over-pitching.
concept: The conditions likely to lead to over-pitching include: operating at or near maximum gross weight; high density altitude (high temperature, low QNH, high elevation); insufficient power margin (engine not producing full rated power); attempting to hover OGE when only IGE hover capability exists; trying to force a climb from ground effect with inadequate power; and high humidity (reduces engine power output). The common thread is a mismatch between power available and power required — usually at high weight and/or high density altitude.
exam_tip: Conditions for overpitching: high weight, high DA, insufficient engine power, attempting OGE hover when only IGE is achievable, or low-RPM excursion. Performance planning should identify the risk before engine start.
key_facts: High gross weight (near MAUW)
High density altitude (high temperature, high elevation, low QNH)
Engine not delivering full rated power (hot engine, degraded performance)
Attempting OGE hover when only IGE hover is achievable
Excessive collective application without checking power margins first

syllabus_desc: Describe: the symptoms of over-pitching.
concept: The symptoms of over-pitching are: increasing collective with no corresponding increase in climb rate (or the helicopter is sinking despite maximum collective); RPM decaying as the rotor is increasingly loaded; throttle already at maximum and still insufficient power; the helicopter settles toward the ground despite full collective; the pedal and cyclic controls feel sluggish or less responsive as rotor RPM drops. In extreme cases, the rotor can be driven to a stall from which recovery is nearly impossible.
exam_tip: Symptoms: collective going up but no climb (or settling), RPM decay, throttle already at max, sluggish controls. Distinguish from vortex ring state — overpitching is about too much blade pitch (near stall), not descending into own downwash.
key_facts: Collective increasing but no climb rate response (or helicopter settling)
Rotor RPM decaying despite engine at maximum power
Throttle at maximum — no further power available
Controls feel sluggish or unresponsive
Helicopter sinking toward ground despite full collective — rotor approaching stall

syllabus_desc: Describe: the recovery technique for over-pitching.
concept: The recovery technique for over-pitching is to simultaneously reduce collective (to reduce blade AoA and recover rotor RPM), accept the descent rate that results, and attempt to make a controlled landing. If height permits, reducing collective allows RPM to recover, after which a climb may be possible. If close to the ground, this means accepting a firm landing. Do NOT raise the collective further — this will only deepen the stall. Autorotation entry may be required if RPM decays significantly. Prevention through pre-flight performance planning is far better than any recovery.
exam_tip: Recovery: LOWER collective to unstall blades and recover RPM. Accept descent/landing. Do NOT raise collective — it makes it worse. If RPM has decayed badly, consider autorotation entry. This is a 'reduce and accept' recovery, not a 'power through it' recovery.
key_facts: Lower collective immediately to reduce blade AoA and recover RPM
Accept the resulting descent — do not fight it
Make a controlled landing from the subsequent descent
Do NOT raise collective further — worsens the stall
If RPM decay is severe, prepare for autorotation entry

syllabus_desc: Describe recirculation.
concept: Recirculation occurs when the helicopter hovers in conditions where the rotor downwash is fed back up through the rotor disc rather than flowing away cleanly. This typically happens when hovering in a confined area (the downwash hits the ground or obstacles and bounces back up), or when hovering close to a wall or hangar. The recirculated air is already moving downward — as it passes back through the disc it reduces the effective pressure differential, reducing rotor efficiency, increasing power required, and causing the helicopter to sink.
exam_tip: Recirculation = rotor downwash recirculates back up through the disc (not the same as vortex ring state, which is about descent into own downwash). Caused by hovering near walls, in confined areas, or over surfaces that redirect airflow back up. Reduces rotor efficiency.
key_facts: Rotor downwash recirculates back up through the disc
Caused by hovering near walls, buildings, or in confined areas
Downwash bounces off surfaces and re-enters rotor from below
Reduces effective pressure differential across disc — less efficient
Result: more power required, helicopter may sink unexpectedly

syllabus_desc: Describe: the conditions likely to lead to recirculation.
concept: Conditions likely to lead to recirculation: hovering in confined areas surrounded by buildings, walls, or vegetation; hovering close to a vertical surface such as a wall or hangar; hovering over an uneven surface where the terrain directs downwash back up; hovering downwind of a large structure that redirects airflow; and hovering in a 'bowl' or depression in terrain. The key factor is any obstacle or surface geometry that redirects the rotor downwash back toward the disc.
exam_tip: Recirculation conditions: confined areas, near walls/buildings, terrain that channels downwash back up. Know these for operations in off-airfield sites — a common CPL scenario. Identify and avoid before committing to a hover.
key_facts: Hovering in confined areas enclosed by walls or buildings
Hovering close to a vertical wall or large structure
Terrain that channels/redirects downwash back up toward disc
Hovering downwind of obstacles that redirect airflow
Operating in bowls, gullies, or terrain depressions

syllabus_desc: Describe: the symptoms of recirculation.
concept: The symptoms of recirculation are: unexplained high power requirement to maintain hover height; the helicopter sinking despite collective being at or near maximum; unusual turbulence or buffeting through the controls or airframe; a 'bogging' sensation with increased power demand; and difficulty maintaining a stable hover position due to the disturbed airflow. These symptoms can develop gradually as the helicopter approaches a recirculating airflow zone.
exam_tip: Recirculation symptoms: unexplained power increase needed, sinking despite high collective, turbulence, control buffeting. Distinguish from overpitching (no external cause) and vortex ring (descending into own downwash). Recirculation has an obvious environmental cause.
key_facts: High power requirement with no obvious reason
Helicopter sinks despite collective near maximum
Control turbulence or buffeting from disturbed airflow
Difficulty maintaining stable hover position
Gradually worsening power demand as approach to confined area progresses

syllabus_desc: Describe: the recovery technique for recirculation.
concept: The recovery technique for recirculation is to exit the zone of recirculating airflow — either by climbing above the obstacle height (if power permits), moving laterally away from the wall or enclosed area, or transitioning to forward flight to escape the downwash recirculation zone. Do not lower collective if the helicopter is already sinking — this will accelerate the descent. Moving into a headwind or out of the confined area is usually the most effective recovery. If near the ground, a firm landing may be the safest option.
exam_tip: Recirculation recovery: exit the zone — climb above obstacles (if power available), move laterally away from walls, or transition to forward flight. Don't lower collective while sinking. Moving out of the confined area is the primary fix.
key_facts: Move laterally away from walls or obstacles causing recirculation
Climb above obstacle height if sufficient power available
Transition to forward flight to move out of downwash recirculation zone
Do not lower collective while already sinking
If near the ground, accept a firm landing rather than risking uncontrolled descent

syllabus_desc: Explain the arrangement of forces on the rotor and fuselage when: transitioning into forward flight.
concept: When transitioning from hover into forward flight, the pilot applies forward cyclic. This tilts the rotor disc forward, which tilts the total rotor thrust vector forward — creating a horizontal component. Initially, the horizontal component of TRT is greater than the drag of the fuselage and rotor system, so the helicopter accelerates forward. During this transition, the rotor disc is tilted forward, so the vertical component of TRT is slightly less than the full TRT — meaning the helicopter may initially sink slightly unless collective is increased to compensate. The tail boom and fuselage generate drag as airspeed increases.
exam_tip: Transitioning to forward flight: forward cyclic → disc tilt → horizontal TRT component → acceleration. The vertical component of TRT decreases as disc tilts (same thrust vector, but now partly horizontal) — compensate with slight collective increase. This transition also brings translational lift benefits.
key_facts: Forward cyclic → disc tilts forward → TRT tilts forward
Horizontal component of TRT → net forward accelerating force
Vertical component of TRT decreases as disc tilts → risk of slight sink
Increase collective slightly to compensate during transition
As airspeed increases, translational lift improves rotor efficiency

syllabus_desc: Explain the arrangement of forces on the rotor and fuselage when: in equilibrium in forward flight.
concept: In equilibrium in forward flight, all forces are balanced. Total rotor thrust (TRT) is tilted forward to provide both the vertical component to support weight and the horizontal component to overcome drag (fuselage + rotor drag). The fuselage is in a nose-down attitude relative to the disc, so weight acts downward through the CG while TRT acts upward and forward perpendicular to the tip path plane. For steady cruise, TRT magnitude and direction are adjusted (via collective and cyclic) so that its components exactly match weight (vertical) and total drag (horizontal). No net acceleration in any direction.
exam_tip: In equilibrium forward flight: TRT tilted forward, vertical component = weight, horizontal component = total drag. The disc is tilted forward of the fuselage attitude. Four forces: weight, TRT, and they're the only real forces — drag is included in TRT horizontal component because TRT opposes motion.
key_facts: All forces in equilibrium — no acceleration
TRT tilted forward: vertical component = Weight
Horizontal component of TRT = Total drag (fuselage + rotor drag)
Disc tilted forward relative to shaft/fuselage
Maintained by cyclic (disc tilt) + collective (TRT magnitude)

syllabus_desc: Describe the changes to blade angle and tip path as the disc is tilted with cyclic.
concept: When the pilot applies forward cyclic, the swashplate tilts, increasing blade pitch in the aft sector of the disc and decreasing it in the forward sector (with phase lag compensation). This differential pitch change creates more lift in the aft sector and less in the forward sector. Due to gyroscopic precession, the disc response is 90° later — so the disc tilts forward. As the disc tilts, the blade tip path tilts forward. Simultaneously, the blade pitch angles change periodically as they rotate — blades have maximum pitch roughly over the rear of the fuselage and minimum pitch over the front.
exam_tip: Disc tilt from cyclic: swashplate tilts → periodic blade pitch change → differential lift → gyroscopic precession → disc tilts 90° later. Forward cyclic: blades get more pitch at the rear, less at the front → disc tilts forward. Know the 90° precession rule.
key_facts: Forward cyclic: swashplate tilts (rear higher, front lower after phase lag compensation)
More pitch as blade passes over rear of fuselage → more lift there
Gyroscopic precession: disc responds 90° later → disc tilts forward
Blade pitch angle is maximum roughly over the rear/side of the disc
Tip path tilts in the direction of applied cyclic after precession compensation

syllabus_desc: Explain: flapping to equality.
concept: Flapping to equality is the process by which rotor blades automatically compensate for the dissymmetry of lift in forward flight, equalising the lift across the advancing and retreating sides. The advancing blade (higher airspeed = more lift) flaps upward, which reduces its angle of attack and thus its lift. The retreating blade (lower airspeed = less lift) flaps downward, increasing its angle of attack and lift. The result is that both blades produce roughly equal lift at the same disc position, despite the speed difference.
exam_tip: Flapping to equality: the natural self-correcting mechanism of an articulated or semi-rigid rotor. No pilot input required — it's automatic. Advancing blade flaps up (AoA down), retreating blade flaps down (AoA up). Result: equalised lift across the disc.
key_facts: Automatic process — no pilot input required
Advancing blade: higher TAS → more lift → flaps up → AoA reduces → lift equalises
Retreating blade: lower TAS → less lift → flaps down → AoA increases → lift equalises
Net result: lift distribution across disc is approximately equalised
Mechanism that allows helicopters to fly in forward flight without rolling

syllabus_desc: Explain: dissymmetry of lift.
concept: Dissymmetry of lift is the unequal lift distribution between the advancing and retreating blades in forward flight. The advancing blade has a velocity equal to rotor tip speed plus aircraft forward speed (Vr + V), while the retreating blade has a velocity equal to rotor tip speed minus forward speed (Vr - V). Since lift is proportional to V², the advancing blade generates far more lift than the retreating blade. Without compensation (via flapping), this would cause the helicopter to roll toward the retreating blade side.
exam_tip: Dissymmetry of lift: advancing blade velocity = Vr + V (more lift), retreating blade = Vr - V (less lift). Lift ∝ V², so the difference grows rapidly with airspeed. Without flapping, the helicopter would roll. Know the velocities: advancing blade TAS = Vr + V.
key_facts: Advancing blade velocity: Vr + V (rotational + forward speed)
Retreating blade velocity: Vr - V (rotational − forward speed)
Lift ∝ V², so advancing blade lift much greater than retreating
Without compensation: helicopter would roll toward retreating blade side
Grows with airspeed — high-speed flight pushes blades toward stall limits

syllabus_desc: Explain: means of overcoming dissymmetry of lift.
concept: Dissymmetry of lift is overcome primarily by flapping — blades flap up on the advancing side (reducing AoA and lift) and down on the retreating side (increasing AoA and lift), automatically equalising lift. Blade feathering (cyclic pitch) can also contribute — some designs apply more pitch to the retreating blade to boost its lift. In practice, the combination of flapping and cyclic feathering (applied through the swashplate geometry) manages dissymmetry effectively up to the speeds limited by retreating blade stall.
exam_tip: Dissymmetry overcome by: (1) flapping — primary mechanism, automatic; (2) blade feathering — pitch applied differentially to advancing/retreating sides via swashplate. Both mechanisms work together. The limit is retreating blade stall — beyond that, dissymmetry cannot be overcome.
key_facts: Primary mechanism: flapping to equality (automatic)
Secondary mechanism: cyclic feathering applies differential pitch
Swashplate geometry applies more pitch to retreating blade side
Both mechanisms work together to equalise lift
Limit: retreating blade stall — Vne is set to prevent this

syllabus_desc: Explain: flap-back (blow-back).
concept: Flap-back (blow-back) is the tendency of the rotor disc to tilt rearward as forward airspeed increases. As the helicopter accelerates, the advancing blade flaps up more and the retreating blade flaps down less (relative to hover), causing the tip path plane to tilt backward. The pilot must apply progressive forward cyclic as airspeed increases to maintain the desired disc attitude and airspeed. Flap-back is most pronounced during acceleration and reduces once a steady forward speed is established. It explains why more forward cyclic is needed at higher speeds than in hover.
exam_tip: Flap-back: as airspeed increases, the disc tilts rearward automatically due to increased flapping. Pilot must apply more forward cyclic to maintain acceleration and cruise speed. It's a natural gyroscopic/flapping effect — not a problem, just something to compensate for.
key_facts: As forward airspeed increases, advancing blade flaps up more
Disc tip path tilts rearward — reduces forward tilt
Pilot must apply progressive forward cyclic to compensate
More noticeable during acceleration phases
Explains why forward cyclic displacement increases with airspeed

syllabus_desc: Explain: flap-forward.
concept: Flap-forward is the tendency of the disc to tilt forward during deceleration. As the helicopter slows from cruise, the airspeed decreases and the advancing blade's extra lift reduces — so it flaps less. The retreating blade's deficit reduces too. The net effect is a disc tilt forward as the asymmetric flapping decreases. During a flare, the pilot applies aft cyclic, which also contributes to the disc tilting rearward — but if the cyclic is released, the natural flap-forward tendency becomes apparent. Flap-forward is relevant when managing autorotation flares and decelerations.
exam_tip: Flap-forward: as airspeed decreases, the disc tilts forward (less asymmetric flapping). During flare, pilot applies aft cyclic to control deceleration. Releasing aft cyclic prematurely during flare allows flap-forward tendency to reduce deceleration effectiveness.
key_facts: As airspeed decreases, asymmetric flapping reduces
Disc tilts forward (flap-forward tendency)
Relevant during deceleration and flare
Aft cyclic during flare must be held to prevent premature flap-forward
Related to translational lift dissipating as speed decreases

syllabus_desc: Explain: reverse flow.
concept: Reverse flow is the region near the root of the retreating blade where, at high forward airspeeds, the forward speed of the aircraft exceeds the rotational velocity of that blade section. In this region, the airflow over the blade is reversed — from trailing edge to leading edge. The reverse flow region grows as airspeed increases. Blades are designed with enough rotational velocity at the tip that the tips never experience reverse flow, but the root sections do at high speed. Reverse flow causes turbulence and reduces the effective contributing area of the retreating blade.
exam_tip: Reverse flow region: at high airspeed, retreating blade root has aircraft forward speed > blade rotational speed → airflow goes backward over blade. Grows with airspeed. Contributes to retreating blade stall limitation. This is why rotor blades are symmetric aerofoil sections — they can generate some lift even in reverse flow.
key_facts: Retreating blade root: aircraft forward speed can exceed blade rotational speed
Results in airflow from trailing edge to leading edge (reversed)
Reverse flow region grows with increased forward airspeed
Root sections generate less (or negative) lift in reverse flow
Symmetric blade sections generate less-negative lift in reverse flow than cambered sections

syllabus_desc: State the flight profile in which translational lift is experienced.
concept: Translational lift is experienced when the helicopter transitions from hover into forward flight — typically from approximately 10 to 20 knots, depending on the helicopter type. During this speed range, the rotor disc exits the hover downwash recirculation zone and starts to operate in relatively undisturbed air. The induced velocity decreases, rotor efficiency increases, and the helicopter noticeably 'lifts' into translational lift. The flight profile is: hover → acceleration through ETL speed → establishing cruise.
exam_tip: Translational lift is experienced during the transition from hover to forward flight (ETL = Effective Translational Lift speed, typically 10–20 kt). It's felt as the helicopter wanting to climb or as a reduction in collective needed to maintain height. Know this is a 'profile' event — happens during acceleration through a specific speed range.
key_facts: Experienced during transition from hover into forward flight
Typically occurs between approximately 10–20 knots (helicopter type dependent)
Marks the point where rotor exits hover downwash recirculation
Pilot notices: tendency to climb or reduced collective needed
After ETL, rotor operates in undisturbed air — improved efficiency

syllabus_desc: State the typical speed range through which translational lift is noticeable.
concept: Translational lift becomes noticeable (and is commonly defined as 'effective translational lift') across a speed range of approximately 10 to 20 knots, though exact figures vary by helicopter type, rotor diameter, and disc loading. On some larger helicopters with lower disc loading, ETL may be felt at lower speeds; on smaller, high-disc-loading machines, it may be felt closer to 20 kt. The flight manual for each aircraft type specifies the ETL speed range. The sensation begins as a gentle vibration (inflow roll) followed by the lift 'bump' of ETL.
exam_tip: ETL speed range: typically 10–20 kt, but helicopter-specific. Check flight manual for exact speeds. Low disc loading = ETL at lower speed; high disc loading = ETL later. The speed range, not a single speed, is where translational lift progressively improves.
key_facts: Translational lift typically noticeable between 10 and 20 knots
Exact speed range is helicopter-type specific (check flight manual)
Low disc loading helicopters: ETL may begin sooner
High disc loading helicopters: ETL begins at higher speeds
ETL is a progressive improvement across a speed range, not an instantaneous event

syllabus_desc: Explain the meaning of inflow roll (transverse flow effect).
concept: Inflow roll (transverse flow effect) is a rolling tendency experienced as the helicopter transitions through translational lift. As forward speed increases, the induced flow through the disc is not uniform — the front of the disc (which moves into undisturbed air first) experiences reduced induced flow before the rear. This asymmetry creates an uneven lift distribution that tends to roll the helicopter. For most helicopters with a CCW main rotor viewed from above, inflow roll produces a tendency to roll to the right (low right skid). The pilot counteracts with lateral cyclic.
exam_tip: Inflow roll: asymmetric induced velocity across the disc as the front enters fresh air before the rear. Creates a rolling tendency — typically right for CCW rotor helicopters. Felt in the speed range just before ETL. Counteracted with lateral cyclic.
key_facts: Inflow asymmetry: front of disc gets reduced induced flow before rear during acceleration
Creates unequal lift — rear disc sector has more induced flow → less efficient
Net roll tendency (typically to the right for CCW main rotor helicopters)
Felt as a vibration/roll tendency just below ETL speed
Counteracted by applying lateral cyclic in the appropriate direction

syllabus_desc: State the speed range in which inflow roll is most pronounced.
concept: Inflow roll is most pronounced at the speed range just before effective translational lift is achieved — typically between 5 and 15 knots, where the transition is progressing but not yet complete. At very slow speeds, the induced flow is relatively uniform (hover). At ETL speed and beyond, the transition is complete and flow becomes more uniform again. The 5–15 kt range is where the front/rear disc asymmetry is greatest. This is also the speed range associated with the most vibration during departure and approach.
exam_tip: Inflow roll most pronounced: 5–15 kt (approximately), just before ETL. The sweet spot where induced flow asymmetry is greatest. Also the speed range with characteristic vibration from transverse flow. Know this speed range for exam questions.
key_facts: Most pronounced between approximately 5 and 15 knots
Speed range where front/rear disc induced flow asymmetry is greatest
Below this range: hover — flow more uniform
Above this range: ETL — flow more uniform again
Also the speed range with most noticeable vibration from transverse flow effect

syllabus_desc: Describe how inflow roll is compensated for by the pilot.
concept: The pilot compensates for inflow roll by applying lateral cyclic in the opposite direction to the roll tendency. For a CCW rotor helicopter with right roll tendency from inflow, this means applying left cyclic during the 5–15 kt speed range. Helicopter manufacturers design the swashplate rigging and cyclic neutral position with this tendency in mind, so the pilot naturally holds a slight left cyclic in that speed range without large correction. As ETL is achieved and inflow roll diminishes, the pilot releases the cyclic toward neutral.
