exam_tip: Compensation for inflow roll: apply lateral cyclic opposite to the roll direction. For CCW rotor (right roll tendency): apply left cyclic. The correction is small and brief — as ETL is established, the tendency diminishes and cyclic returns toward neutral.
key_facts: Apply lateral cyclic opposite to roll direction
For CCW rotor: right roll tendency → apply left lateral cyclic
Correction is typically small (cyclic near-neutral position)
As ETL is achieved and inflow roll diminishes, cyclic returns toward neutral
Factory rigging and cyclic lateral trim may pre-compensate for this

syllabus_desc: With the aid of the power available/power required curves: identify the TAS for minimum and maximum straight and level flight.
concept: On the power available/power required curves for forward flight, the minimum power speed corresponds to the lowest point on the total power required curve — this is the speed for maximum endurance (flying at this speed minimises fuel consumption per unit time). The maximum straight and level airspeed is where the power available curve intersects the power required curve on the high-speed side — at this point, all available power is consumed maintaining cruise, with nothing left for acceleration. Speeds between minimum power and maximum level flight cover the practical operating envelope.
exam_tip: Minimum SL flight speed: speed where power required = power available on the slow side of the curve (not the same as power-off stall — it's where the rotor demands so much induced power at low speed that engine max is reached). Max SL flight speed: power required = power available on the high-speed side. Identify both on the curve.
key_facts: Minimum SL flight speed: slow end where power required curve meets power available curve
Maximum SL flight speed: fast end where power required curve meets power available curve
Between these speeds: helicopter can maintain level flight
Speed for minimum power: lowest point on power required curve (max endurance speed)
Speed for best range: slightly higher than minimum power speed (tangent to power required curve from origin)

syllabus_desc: With the aid of the power available/power required curves: describe the factors that affect this TAS.
concept: The TAS for minimum and maximum level flight is affected by several factors: (1) weight — heavier aircraft have higher minimum and maximum level speeds (higher power required at all speeds); (2) density altitude — at high DA, power available decreases (engine delivers less power in thin air), narrowing the speed range between minimum and maximum; (3) configuration — external loads or increased parasite drag shift the power required curve upward, narrowing the envelope; (4) rotor RPM — lower RPM reduces both maximum and minimum level flight speeds.
exam_tip: Factors affecting min/max SL flight speeds: weight (shifts power required up), density altitude (reduces power available), configuration (extra drag), and rotor RPM. High weight + high DA + external loads can combine to leave no excess power for level flight — aircraft cannot maintain altitude.
key_facts: Weight: higher weight → power required increases at all speeds → reduces envelope
Density altitude: reduces power available → narrower speed range
Configuration: external loads/parasite drag → power required increases → speeds reduced
Rotor RPM: lower RPM → less tip speed → less thrust → reduced speed envelope
All factors combine: extreme conditions may leave no positive power margin

syllabus_desc: Describe the forces acting on a rotor blade established in a vertical climb.
concept: In a vertical climb, the rotor disc is horizontal and generating thrust vertically upward. The forces acting are: total rotor thrust (upward, opposing weight and drag), aircraft weight (downward), and rotor drag (opposing the direction of motion — so downward in a vertical climb). There is no horizontal component of thrust in a pure vertical climb, but tail rotor anti-torque force still acts. The power required is substantial — vertical climb eliminates any translational lift benefit.
exam_tip: Vertical climb forces: thrust up, weight down, rotor drag down (opposing motion). No translational lift — power demand is high.
key_facts: Total rotor thrust acts vertically upward in a vertical climb
Weight acts vertically downward
Rotor (climbing) drag acts downward opposing the climb
Tail rotor thrust acts horizontally to counter torque
No translational lift — less aerodynamic efficiency than forward flight

syllabus_desc: Define: rate of climb.
concept: Rate of climb (ROC) is the rate at which a helicopter gains altitude, expressed in feet per minute (fpm). It is determined by the excess power available after level flight requirements are met: ROC = excess power ÷ AUW. At maximum continuous power, the best rate of climb gives the greatest altitude gain per minute — used when you want to reach a given altitude in minimum time.
exam_tip: ROC = excess power ÷ weight. It's a vertical speed — fpm. Best ROC = Vy speed.
key_facts: Rate of climb expressed in feet per minute (fpm)
ROC = excess power ÷ AUW
Maximum ROC occurs at Vy (best rate of climb speed)
Decreases as weight increases or altitude increases
Zero ROC = level flight; negative ROC = descent

syllabus_desc: Define: angle of climb.
concept: Angle of climb is the angle between the flight path and the horizontal — how steeply you're climbing relative to the ground. It is determined by the ratio of ROC to groundspeed (or TAS in still air). Best angle of climb (Vx) gives the greatest height gain per unit of horizontal distance — used for obstacle clearance. Vx is lower than Vy for most helicopters.
exam_tip: Angle of climb = arctan(ROC ÷ groundspeed). Best angle (Vx) for obstacle clearance; best rate (Vy) for reaching altitude quickly.
key_facts: Angle of climb = height gained per horizontal distance
Best angle at Vx (lower speed than Vy)
Vx used for obstacle clearance
Headwind improves ground angle of climb
Angle increases with excess power and decreases with airspeed

syllabus_desc: On the power available/power required curves, identify TAS for: maximum rate of climb.
concept: The power required curve for a helicopter in climbing flight shifts upward compared to level flight — more total power is needed to sustain the same airspeed while also gaining altitude. The minimum power required speed (Vy) is where excess power is greatest, making it the speed for best rate of climb. Climbing at speeds below or above Vy increases power required and reduces excess power for climbing.
exam_tip: Vy (best rate of climb speed) occurs at the lowest point of the power required curve — that's where excess power is greatest.
key_facts: Power required increases for any airspeed when climbing
Vy is the speed with maximum excess power
Below Vy: induced power losses dominate
Above Vy: parasite drag and profile drag dominate
Power required curve shifts up in a climb but its minimum speed (Vy) stays similar

syllabus_desc: On the power available/power required curves, identify TAS for: best angle of climb.
concept: The power available curve is essentially flat (or slightly declining at high airspeeds) and represents the maximum shaft power the engine can deliver to the rotor. The gap between the power available curve and the power required curve is the excess power — and this excess power determines climb performance. At altitude, the power available curve drops as the engine loses output, reducing the excess power gap.
exam_tip: The gap between power available and power required equals excess power. A bigger gap = better climb performance.
key_facts: Power available is relatively constant across airspeeds (slightly decreasing at high speed)
Excess power = power available minus power required
Excess power diminishes as altitude increases
At service ceiling, excess power is just 100 fpm worth of climb
The intersection of PA and PR curves marks the absolute ceiling

syllabus_desc: With the aid of power available/power required curves, explain the effects on rate of climb, angle of climb, and required TAS as applicable, of: collective setting changes.
concept: Increasing collective raises blade pitch, generating more thrust and consuming more power. If collective is raised while in level flight, the excess power becomes a climb — rate and angle of climb increase until a new equilibrium is reached. Lowering collective reduces power required, potentially below the level-flight value, resulting in descent. The TAS for a given power condition shifts as climb or descent changes the drag balance.
exam_tip: More collective = more thrust = climb (if power available allows). The power curve shifts upward with increased collective in a climb — ROC depends on how much excess power is available.
key_facts: Increasing collective increases blade pitch, thrust, and power required
If power available > power required: aircraft climbs
Rate of climb increases until excess power is zero
Lowering collective below level flight setting initiates descent
TAS for minimum power shifts slightly with collective changes

syllabus_desc: With the aid of power available/power required curves, explain the effects on rate of climb, angle of climb, and required TAS as applicable, of: altitude.
concept: As altitude increases, air density decreases. For a normally aspirated piston engine, this reduces engine power output (power available decreases). At the same time, the rotor must work harder in thinner air (power required increases at altitude). The combined effect is a reduction in excess power, meaning lower rate of climb, shallower angle, and eventually reaching the service ceiling (ROC = 100 fpm) and absolute ceiling (ROC = 0 fpm).
exam_tip: Altitude reduces excess power from both sides: less engine power available AND more rotor power required. This limits ROC and ceiling.
key_facts: Higher altitude: engine power available decreases
Higher altitude: power required increases (thinner air)
Excess power margin shrinks — less ROC
Best rate and angle of climb speeds may shift slightly
Service ceiling: ROC = 100 fpm; absolute ceiling: ROC = 0

syllabus_desc: With the aid of power available/power required curves, explain the effects on rate of climb, angle of climb, and required TAS as applicable, of: aircraft weight.
concept: Increased aircraft weight raises the power required curve at all airspeeds — more lift is needed, so more induced power is consumed. With the same power available, excess power is reduced, directly reducing rate and angle of climb. The speeds for best ROC and best angle change slightly. A heavy helicopter may be unable to clear obstacles that a lighter one could manage easily — weight planning is critical for departure performance.
exam_tip: More weight → higher power required → less excess power → worse climb performance. Weight is one of the most controllable factors.
key_facts: Higher weight raises power required at all airspeeds
Excess power gap reduces — less ROC and shallower climb angle
Ceiling lowers with increased weight
Best ROC speed (Vy) shifts slightly higher with weight
Weight planning critical for obstacle clearance on departure

syllabus_desc: With the aid of power available/power required curves, explain the effects on rate of climb, angle of climb, and required TAS as applicable, of: density altitude.
concept: Density altitude combines the effects of pressure altitude, temperature, and humidity on air density. High DA (hot, high, humid) degrades both the engine power available and rotor efficiency. The effect on the power curves is similar to increased altitude: power available drops, power required increases, and the excess power gap shrinks. In extreme conditions, a helicopter may not be able to maintain level flight, let alone climb.
exam_tip: High density altitude degrades climb performance from both sides of the power equation. Always compute DA before departure, especially in summer or at elevated aerodromes.
key_facts: Density altitude = pressure altitude corrected for temperature and humidity
High DA reduces engine power output
High DA increases rotor power required
Both effects reduce excess power and climb performance
NZAA aerodromes like Queenstown at 1,171 ft AMSL — check DA in summer

syllabus_desc: With the aid of power available/power required curves, explain the effects on rate of climb, angle of climb, and required TAS as applicable, of: angle of bank.
concept: Banking the helicopter tilts the rotor disc, redirecting some rotor thrust horizontally for the turn. This reduces the vertical component of thrust available to support weight and climb. To maintain altitude in a turn, collective must be increased. The additional power required for the turn reduces excess power available for climbing — the steeper the bank, the more power goes to supporting the load factor, leaving less for climb.
exam_tip: In a banked turn, some thrust goes horizontal (centripetal force) and collective must increase to maintain altitude — this leaves less excess power for climbing.
key_facts: Turning requires collective increase to maintain altitude
Increased collective in a turn increases power required
Less excess power available for climbing in a turn
Rate of climb decreases in proportion to the bank angle and increased power
Steep turns in a climbing helicopter may cause ROC to approach zero

syllabus_desc: With the aid of power available/power required curves, explain the effects on rate of climb, angle of climb, and required TAS as applicable, of: external loads.
concept: External loads (slingloads, floats, cameras, wire cutters, etc.) increase the effective AUW and add parasite drag. Both effects raise the power required curve. The additional weight increases induced power required; the increased parasite drag raises the drag component at all airspeeds. The combined effect reduces excess power and degrades climb performance — ROC, angle of climb, and ceiling all worsen with external loads.
exam_tip: External loads increase both weight and drag — both raise power required and reduce excess power for climbing. Always check performance with external load fitted.
key_facts: External loads add weight → higher induced power required
External loads add drag → higher parasite power required
ROC and ceiling both decrease with external loads
Aerodynamic shape of load affects drag penalty
Performance must be recalculated with specific external load attached

syllabus_desc: With the aid of power available/power required curves, explain the effects on rate of climb, angle of climb, and required TAS as applicable, of: the wind.
concept: Wind affects angle of climb over the ground significantly but not rate of climb (which is a vertical speed). A headwind improves the angle of climb over terrain — the helicopter covers less horizontal ground distance per foot of altitude gained. A tailwind reduces the ground angle of climb. TAS remains unchanged (assuming no density effect), but groundspeed is lower in a headwind, improving the altitude-per-distance ratio.
exam_tip: Wind affects ground angle of climb but not ROC. Headwind = steeper ground angle = better obstacle clearance per unit of ground distance.
key_facts: Headwind improves angle of climb over the ground
Tailwind worsens angle of climb over the ground
ROC (vertical speed) is unaffected by wind
For obstacle clearance planning, use groundspeed not TAS
TAS remains constant — groundspeed changes with wind component

syllabus_desc: Describe the forces on the rotor disc in a vertical descent.
concept: In a vertical descent (powered), the rotor disc remains horizontal and thrust acts vertically upward. The forces include: rotor thrust (upward, now less than weight — allowing descent), weight (downward, now greater than thrust), and rotor drag (upward, opposing the downward motion). At low descent rates, the flow through the disc is still downward — the rotor is operating in a powered state. At high descent rates, the flow can reverse and VRS becomes possible.
exam_tip: Vertical descent forces: weight down, thrust up (less than weight), rotor drag up (opposing motion). Key risk: if descent rate exceeds rotor downwash, VRS can develop.
key_facts: Weight exceeds rotor thrust in a powered vertical descent
Rotor drag now acts upward (opposing downward motion)
Tail rotor thrust still acts horizontally to counter torque
Very slow descent: rotor in powered state, flow still downward through disc
High descent rate: risk of vortex ring state (VRS)

syllabus_desc: Define rate of descent and angle of descent.
concept: Rate of descent (ROD) is the vertical speed of descent, expressed in feet per minute. It represents how quickly altitude is being lost. Angle of descent is the angle between the descent flight path and the horizontal — how steep the descent path is relative to the ground. Both are affected by power setting, airspeed, weight, and wind. Rate of descent is the vertical component; angle of descent depends on both vertical and horizontal speed.
exam_tip: ROD is vertical speed downward (fpm). Angle of descent depends on both ROD and horizontal speed. Know both definitions and how they differ.
key_facts: Rate of descent: vertical speed of descent in fpm
Angle of descent: angle between flight path and horizontal
Angle of descent = arctan(ROD ÷ groundspeed)
Lower power = higher ROD; more collective = lower ROD
Headwind steepens the ground angle of descent

syllabus_desc: With the aid of power available/power required curves, explain the effects on rate of descent, angle of descent, and required TAS as applicable, of: collective setting changes.
concept: In a descent, collective controls the rate of descent. Lowering collective reduces pitch and rotor thrust, allowing the aircraft to descend — ROD increases. Raising collective increases thrust, slowing the descent or converting it to level flight. The angle of descent also changes with collective: more collective reduces angle (shallower), less collective steepens it. In autorotation (zero collective), descent rate is maximised aerodynamically.
exam_tip: In descent, collective controls ROD: lower collective = faster descent, more collective = slower descent. Cyclic controls airspeed.
key_facts: Lowering collective reduces thrust → ROD increases
Raising collective increases thrust → ROD decreases
Angle of descent decreases with more collective (shallower descent)
In autorotation: collective at minimum; RPM controlled by collective in flare
Coordinated descent: cyclic for airspeed, collective for vertical rate

syllabus_desc: With the aid of power available/power required curves, explain the effects on rate of descent, angle of descent, and required TAS as applicable, of: altitude.
concept: At higher altitude, the power required curve shifts (induced power increases, parasite decreases). For a given collective position, a higher altitude setting may result in a slightly different ROD because the rotor operates less efficiently. Engine power available also decreases, but in a descent this is less critical. The main effect of altitude on descent is through changes in TAS for a given IAS — at high altitude, TAS is higher for the same IAS, affecting the angle of descent.
exam_tip: At higher altitude, TAS increases for the same IAS — groundspeed increases — which reduces the angle of descent for the same ROD. Use TAS in angle calculations at altitude.
key_facts: Higher altitude: TAS greater than IAS
Same ROD at higher altitude = shallower angle of descent
Rotor efficiency decreases at high altitude
Power required shifts slightly at altitude
Airspeed indicator reads IAS — TAS must be calculated for performance planning

syllabus_desc: With the aid of power available/power required curves, explain the effects on rate of descent, angle of descent, and required TAS as applicable, of: aircraft weight.
concept: Heavier aircraft require more collective (thrust) to maintain the same rate of descent. For a given collective setting, a heavier aircraft will descend faster than a lighter one because weight exceeds the available thrust by a greater amount. The power required in descent is also higher at greater AUW, consuming more of the limited power margin.
exam_tip: Heavier aircraft descend faster for a given collective setting. More weight = more collective needed to achieve the same descent rate.
key_facts: Higher weight increases descent rate for any given collective
More collective required to achieve same ROD as lighter aircraft
Angle of descent for the same airspeed increases with weight
Weight has large effect on autorotative descent rate
Always compute performance using current AUW

syllabus_desc: With the aid of power available/power required curves, explain the effects on rate of descent, angle of descent, and required TAS as applicable, of: density altitude.
concept: High density altitude reduces engine power output and rotor efficiency. In a powered descent, this matters less than in a climb — you're reducing power, not demanding maximum power. However, if a go-around is required during descent, the reduced power available at high DA is critical. Power margins for go-around must be assessed before commencing the approach at high-altitude aerodromes.
exam_tip: In descent, high DA is less critical for the descent itself but critical for a go-around — always have enough power for a missed approach from the planned descent profile.
key_facts: High DA reduces power available for go-around
Descent itself is less affected by DA than climb
TAS higher at altitude — accounts for some apparent anomaly
Plan go-around capability before descending at high-altitude aerodromes
Autorotative descent rate is slightly lower at high DA (thinner air)

syllabus_desc: With the aid of power available/power required curves, explain the effects on rate of descent, angle of descent, and required TAS as applicable, of: angle of bank.
concept: Banking during a descent (turning approach) redirects rotor thrust horizontally, requiring increased collective to prevent the descent rate from increasing more than intended. The load factor in a bank also increases power required. A descending turn therefore uses more power than a straight descent at the same airspeed, which must be factored into the approach power budget.
exam_tip: Banked descents require more collective to maintain the same descent rate — the bank increases load factor and reduces the vertical component of thrust.
key_facts: Bank angle increases load factor in descent as in level flight
Vertical component of thrust reduces with bank angle
To maintain ROD in a banked descent, collective may need to increase
Power required in a banked descent is higher than straight descent
Avoid excessive bank at low airspeeds in descent — VRS and tail rotor authority risk

syllabus_desc: With the aid of power available/power required curves, explain the effects on rate of descent, angle of descent, and required TAS as applicable, of: external loads.
concept: External loads increase AUW and add parasite drag. In a descent, a heavier aircraft descends faster for the same collective setting, and more collective is required to control ROD. External loads may also affect directional stability and tail rotor authority during the approach. Performance assessment with the specific external load must be completed before the descent.
exam_tip: External loads increase descent rate for a given collective — they add weight and drag, requiring more careful collective management during the approach.
key_facts: External loads add weight → higher descent rate at same collective
External loads add parasite drag → affects approach angle and TAS
More collective required to achieve desired ROD with external load
Check that tail rotor authority is adequate with load attached
Performance charts for descent must use AUW including external load

syllabus_desc: With the aid of power available/power required curves, explain the effects on rate of descent, angle of descent, and required TAS as applicable, of: the wind.
concept: Wind affects the angle of descent over the ground. A headwind reduces groundspeed, steepening the approach angle for the same ROD and TAS. A tailwind increases groundspeed, shallowing the approach angle — requiring a lower approach path or increased ROD to arrive at the same point. For approach planning, always account for the surface wind and its effect on groundspeed and approach angle.
exam_tip: Headwind steepens the ground angle of descent; tailwind shallows it. Plan the approach considering the wind component and its effect on groundspeed.
key_facts: Headwind reduces groundspeed → steeper angle of descent for same ROD
Tailwind increases groundspeed → shallower angle of descent
ROD is unaffected by wind (it's a vertical speed)
Wind affects where you touch down — plan the approach path accordingly
Surface wind direction affects VRS risk in powered approaches

syllabus_desc: Define power check.
concept: A power check is a pre-flight or pre-hover check performed to determine whether the engine is producing adequate power for the planned operation. It involves setting a known manifold pressure (or torque) and RPM and comparing actual fuel flow or torque against the reference value for the conditions (temperature, pressure altitude, and weight). The power check confirms the engine is healthy and reveals whether there is sufficient power for the intended task (especially hover OGE, confined area, or slingload).
exam_tip: A power check tells you whether the engine is performing normally and whether sufficient power exists for the planned operation — especially hover OGE or confined area work.
key_facts: Power check: compare actual power output to reference for conditions
Confirms engine is healthy before committing to demanding operation
Parameters checked: torque or MAP, RPM, fuel flow, temperatures
Performed at or near the operating area before committing
Essential before confined area, high-load, or high-DA operations

syllabus_desc: Describe: situations where a power check is necessary.
concept: A power check is necessary whenever there is doubt about power availability — particularly before operations at high density altitude, maximum AUW, confined areas, or when carrying slingloads. It should be done before any hover OGE requirement, before entering confined areas, and before departures from high-altitude or hot-weather aerodromes. Repeated or unexpected power shortfalls indicate a maintenance issue.
exam_tip: Always do a power check before: confined area operations, OGE hover, max AUW operations, high DA operations, slingload work.
key_facts: High density altitude operations
Maximum or near-maximum AUW
Confined area entry requiring OGE hover
Slingload or external load operations
Any operation where power margins are narrow
Suspected engine performance issue

syllabus_desc: Describe: the sequence of a power check.
concept: The standard sequence of a power check: (1) set takeoff or hover power (torque/MAP and RPM as per POH); (2) note actual torque, fuel flow, engine temperatures, and RPM; (3) compare to the reference values in the performance chart for the actual OAT, pressure altitude, and AUW; (4) if actual power matches or exceeds reference, engine is performing normally and power is available; (5) if actual power is less than reference, investigate before continuing. The check should be completed before any committed descent into a confined area.
exam_tip: Power check sequence: set power → read instruments → compare to performance chart for conditions → go or no-go decision. Do it before committing, not after.
key_facts: Set the reference power (torque/MAP and RPM) for conditions
Note: torque, fuel flow, OAT, RPM, engine temps
Compare to POH/AFM performance table for pressure altitude and OAT
Pass: actual power ≥ reference for conditions
Fail: investigate before continuing — not an acceptable risk to proceed

syllabus_desc: Describe the forces acting on a helicopter in a level turn.
concept: In a banked turn, the rotor disc tilts in the direction of the turn, and the total rotor thrust vector is divided into a vertical component (supporting weight) and a horizontal component (providing centripetal force for the turn). Because the vertical component is now less than total thrust, you need to increase collective (and thus total thrust) to maintain altitude in the turn. This increases power required — turns are more expensive in power than straight-and-level flight.
exam_tip: Turns require more power to maintain altitude — the banked disc splits thrust into vertical and horizontal components, so you need more total thrust to maintain the same vertical component.
key_facts: Bank angle causes thrust to split into lift and centripetal force components
Vertical component of thrust must still equal weight to maintain altitude
Total thrust must increase with bank angle to maintain altitude
Load factor (G) increases with bank angle: 30° = 1.15G, 45° = 1.41G, 60° = 2G
Power required increases with bank angle

syllabus_desc: State the effect of angle of bank on rate of turn and power required.
concept: Increasing bank angle increases load factor and therefore increases power required. The rotor must develop more total thrust (requiring more collective) to maintain the same vertical component while the horizontal component increases for the turn. At the same time, rate of turn increases (more centripetal force for a given airspeed) and radius of turn decreases. Power required increases with the square of the load factor approximately — steep turns are expensive.
exam_tip: Steeper bank → higher load factor → more power required. Also: steeper bank → higher rate of turn, smaller radius for same airspeed.
key_facts: Load factor = 1/cos(bank angle)
Power required increases with bank angle
Rate of turn increases with bank angle (for constant airspeed)
Radius of turn decreases with bank angle
Collective must be increased to maintain altitude as bank increases

syllabus_desc: Explain the factors involved in forward flight during a: steep turn.
concept: A steep turn (typically >45° bank) in a helicopter involves significant load factor (>1.41G at 45°), requiring substantially more power than level flight. The pilot must apply coordinated cyclic, collective, and pedal inputs to maintain height, heading rate, and balance. At steep bank angles, rotor thrust may approach the power limit before structural limits — particularly at high AUW or density altitude. Steep turns demand precise technique and awareness of power margins.
exam_tip: Steep turns demand coordinated control inputs, high collective, and close monitoring of power — power limits are typically reached before structural limits in a helicopter.
key_facts: Steep turn: typically bank angle >45°
Load factor exceeds 1.41G at 45° bank
Collective increase required to maintain altitude
Power limit may be reached before structural limit in most light helicopters
Coordinated pedal input essential to prevent sideslip

syllabus_desc: Explain the factors involved in forward flight during a: minimum radius/maximum rate turn.
concept: Maximum rate / minimum radius turn: achieved by using the maximum bank angle that power allows at the lowest safe airspeed. The rate of turn formula (rate = g × tan(bank) / TAS) shows that lower TAS and higher bank both increase turn rate. Minimum radius also favours lower speed and higher bank. The constraints are: power available (limits bank angle), tail rotor authority (limits yaw coordination at high bank and slow speed), and structural G limits.
exam_tip: Max rate / min radius = steep bank + slow speed. Limited by: power available, tail rotor authority, and structural limits.
key_facts: Max rate of turn: steep bank + low airspeed
Minimum radius: steep bank + low airspeed
Limited by power available before structural G limits in most helicopters
Tail rotor authority can limit coordination at slow speed and steep bank
Rate = (g × tan bank) / TAS — so lower TAS and higher bank both help

syllabus_desc: Explain the effect of the following factors on the rate and radius of turn: altitude.
concept: Altitude affects turn performance primarily through density altitude effects on power available. At higher altitude, the engine produces less power, which limits the maximum bank angle that can be sustained while maintaining altitude. For a given airspeed and bank angle, the rate and radius of turn do not change with altitude (they depend on TAS and bank angle). However, TAS is higher at altitude for the same IAS, so using IAS figures can be misleading — the actual radius is larger at altitude.
exam_tip: Altitude doesn't change rate/radius for a given TAS and bank, but reduces the power available to sustain steep banks. Also, same IAS = higher TAS at altitude = larger radius.
key_facts: Rate of turn depends on TAS and bank angle — not directly on altitude
Radius of turn increases at altitude (same IAS = higher TAS)
Maximum bank angle limited by power available — reduces at high altitude
High DA reduces power available for steep turns
Plan turns at altitude using TAS, not IAS

syllabus_desc: Explain the effect of the following factors on the rate and radius of turn: gross weight.
concept: Increased gross weight increases power required to maintain altitude in any turn (due to higher load factor and higher weight). The maximum sustainable bank angle decreases because more power is required. Rate and radius of turn (for a given TAS and bank) are not directly affected by weight, but the power penalty for any given bank angle is higher at higher AUW.
exam_tip: Higher gross weight reduces the maximum bank angle achievable (more power needed). Rate and radius for a given TAS and bank are unchanged — but the power cost is higher.
key_facts: Higher gross weight increases power required in turns
Maximum bank angle (power-limited) decreases with weight
Rate and radius at given TAS and bank unchanged by weight directly
But power cost of any given bank is higher — less margin
Weight critical consideration in confined area turning flight

syllabus_desc: Explain the effect of the following factors on the rate and radius of turn: external loads.
concept: External loads increase effective AUW and add parasite drag. Both effects increase power required, particularly in turns where load factor is applied. A heavy external load reduces the maximum bank angle sustainable. The load may also swing during turns, creating dynamic loads on the helicopter structure and affecting stability. External load turning must be done at reduced bank angles and with gradual, smooth inputs.
exam_tip: External loads reduce maximum bank angle in turns (more power required, possible swing loads). Use gradual inputs and reduced bank angles.
key_facts: External loads increase effective AUW → higher power required in turns
Parasite drag of load increases power required further
Slingload may swing in a turn — creates dynamic structural loads
Use shallower bank angles with external loads
Gradual smooth inputs reduce load pendulum swing

syllabus_desc: Explain the effect of the following factors on the rate and radius of turn: the wind.
concept: Wind affects turn radius over the ground. In a headwind turn, the inbound leg covers more ground distance and the outbound less — the ground track becomes egg-shaped. For the helicopter's structure and aerodynamics, rate and radius in the air remain the same; but for terrain clearance and confined area planning, the ground track is what matters. Always plan turns in confined areas accounting for the wind and the resulting asymmetric ground track.
exam_tip: Wind distorts the ground-track circle. Turning downwind: larger radius over ground. Turning upwind: smaller radius over ground. Plan confined area turns accounting for this.
key_facts: Wind has no direct effect on airspeed-referenced rate or radius of turn
Wind causes ground track to be asymmetric (egg-shaped)
Downwind portion of turn: larger radius over ground
Upwind portion: smaller radius over ground
Plan confined area orbit accounting for wind effect on ground track

syllabus_desc: State the effect of angle of bank on altitude, at a constant power and IAS.
concept: Factors affecting turning performance include: airspeed (lower speed → smaller radius but slower rate unless also steeper bank), bank angle (steeper → faster rate, smaller radius, more G), power available (limits maximum bank angle that can be sustained), density altitude (reduces power available, limiting turn performance), and wind (affects turn radius over the ground). Understanding these factors is essential for operational planning in confined areas.
exam_tip: Turning performance is limited by power, bank angle, and airspeed — all of which interact. High DA significantly degrades the maximum sustainable bank angle.
key_facts: Low airspeed + steep bank = tight fast turn but high power demand
High DA reduces power available, limiting maximum bank
Wind alters ground track radius — account for it in confined area turns
Load factor increases rapidly at steep bank angles
Coordinated turns essential for efficiency and passenger comfort

syllabus_desc: Explain the effect of wind on indicated airspeed and translational lift during a turn.
concept: The effect of speed on turning in a helicopter: at low speeds, rate of turn is high and radius is small even at moderate bank angles — this is the helicopter's manoeuvring advantage. As speed increases, radius grows and you need more bank to achieve the same rate. Very low speed turns (near hover) use primarily tail rotor pedal rather than banked disc, making them unique to rotary-wing aircraft.
exam_tip: Hovering turns use pedal (tail rotor). Low-speed forward flight uses disc tilt and pedal coordination. High-speed turns are similar to fixed-wing — steeper bank needed for same rate.
key_facts: Hover: turns initiated entirely by pedal (tail rotor torque)
Low speed: combination of disc tilt and pedal input
High speed: primarily disc tilt, same as fixed-wing bank-and-pull
Rate of turn at hover is limited by tail rotor power
Understanding the transition from pedal to disc-tilt turns is key for CPL

syllabus_desc: Explain the effect of slipping and skidding on the rate and radius of turn.
concept: Slipping and skidding turns: A slip occurs when the ball moves toward the low wing (inside the turn) — insufficient top rudder/inside pedal. A skid occurs when the ball moves toward the high wing (outside the turn) — too much inside pedal. In a helicopter, these are corrected with tail rotor pedals. Both conditions create drag and reduce performance; skids can be aerodynamically destabilising at high bank angles.
exam_tip: Ball to the low side (toward the inside of the turn) = slip — add inside pedal. Ball to the high side (outside) = skid — reduce inside pedal.
key_facts: Slip: ball inside the turn — too little inside pedal
Skid: ball outside the turn — too much inside pedal
Correction: step on the ball with opposite pedal to correct slip; reduce inside pedal to correct skid
Uncoordinated turns are less efficient and create additional drag
Skidding turns at high bank angles can be structurally hazardous

syllabus_desc: Explain the meaning and purpose of the flare.
concept: Transitioning from forward flight to a hover involves decelerating, losing translational lift, and simultaneously increasing collective to compensate — this is where 'the hover requires more power than cruise flight' becomes very real. As airspeed reduces below ETL (~16–24 knots), the rotor loses the efficiency gained from translational lift and induced flow patterns change, requiring a significant power increase to maintain altitude.
exam_tip: Losing translational lift on approach requires a power increase — this is why power demand spikes when you slow below ETL. Anticipate it with collective before you need it.
key_facts: ETL (effective translational lift) lost below approximately 16–24 knots
Power demand increases significantly below ETL
Deceleration requires aft cyclic, which also tends to climb — co-ordinate collective
Pilot must increase collective as speed reduces to maintain height
High AUW or high DA makes the transition to hover more demanding

syllabus_desc: Explain the effects of the flare on: rotor rpm.
concept: During the landing flare (aft cyclic), the aft tilt of the rotor disc increases the blade angle of attack. The airflow angle of attack on the rotor increases and rotor RPM initially rises as the blades experience increased lift and torque is momentarily reduced (engine no longer spinning the rotor at the same rate). If collective is not adjusted, RRPM will temporarily increase above normal limits before stabilising. The pilot monitors RRPM carefully through the flare.
exam_tip: The flare momentarily increases RRPM as the aft tilt changes blade aerodynamics. Monitor RRPM and avoid over-revving the rotor during flare.
key_facts: Aft cyclic during flare tilts disc rearward
Increased angle of attack on rotor blades
RRPM tends to rise during the flare
Monitor rotor tachometer — avoid exceeding high RPM limit
Collective management during flare controls RRPM

syllabus_desc: Explain the effects of the flare on: total rotor thrust.
concept: Total rotor thrust increases during the flare because the increase in angle of attack (from the aft disc tilt) generates more lift from each blade. The flare converts the helicopter's kinetic energy (airspeed and RRPM) into additional thrust, decelerating the aircraft and arresting the descent rate simultaneously. This is the aerodynamic mechanism that allows autorotative landings — flare energy replaces engine power at the critical final moments.
exam_tip: Total rotor thrust increases during the flare — this is the energy exchange that arrests descent and decelerates the aircraft. It's the core of the autorotative landing technique.
key_facts: Aft disc tilt increases angle of attack on all blades
Total rotor thrust increases during flare
Both descent rate and airspeed decrease simultaneously
Kinetic energy (airspeed + RRPM) converted to increased thrust
Flare is the key energy management moment in autorotation

syllabus_desc: Explain the effects of the flare on: rotor drag.
concept: Rotor drag increases during the flare as the blades operate at higher angles of attack and produce more lift — induced drag increases with lift. Additionally, the deceleration of airspeed during the flare reduces the rotor's rotational efficiency, and the increased lift production extracts energy from the rotor. If the flare is held too long or collective is raised too early, RRPM will decay rapidly as rotor drag dissipates the stored energy.
exam_tip: Flare increases rotor drag — the energy being extracted from the rotor (to arrest descent) shows up as drag. Too long a flare depletes RRPM faster than it's recovered.
key_facts: Rotor drag increases during the flare (more lift = more induced drag)
Rotor drag is how the flare converts RRPM energy into thrust
RRPM decreases if flare energy is exhausted before touchdown
Raising collective too early reduces RRPM through blade pitch drag
Timing of collective raise at touchdown is critical to avoid RRPM decay

syllabus_desc: Describe the causes of rotor rpm changes during the flare.
concept: The factors affecting the transition to the hover include: AUW (heavier requires more power at the bottom of the approach), density altitude (high DA reduces power margins), wind (headwind component helps maintain translational lift longer and reduces the power demand during transition), and approach angle (steeper approaches compress the transition — less distance to manage the deceleration).
exam_tip: Wind helps, weight and altitude hurt. Plan the approach angle and speed profile to match the power available — don't arrive over the landing spot below ETL with insufficient collective margin.
key_facts: High AUW increases power demand during transition
High density altitude reduces available power margin
Headwind extends translational lift, smoothing the transition
Steep approaches require early anticipation of power increase
Transition is most demanding at high AUW, high DA, and calm wind

syllabus_desc: Describe a zero-speed landing.
concept: Flying the approach: the standard technique is to fly at a stabilised airspeed and approach angle, then manage the deceleration so that the helicopter arrives over the landing point at approximately zero groundspeed at zero height. The aim is to avoid either decelerating too quickly (sudden power demand) or arriving too fast (overshoot). Good technique involves continuous adjustment of cyclic and collective together through the transition.
exam_tip: Aim to arrive at the landing point at zero groundspeed and zero AGL simultaneously — any mismatch requires go-around or repositioning.
key_facts: Stabilised approach: constant airspeed and approach angle to a set point
Flare to decelerate: aft cyclic raises nose, reduces speed and descent rate
Co-ordinate collective increase during flare to maintain height
Aim to cross ETL just before the landing point
Go-around is always an option if the approach is unstabilised

syllabus_desc: Describe the power requirements during a zero-speed landing.
concept: Running landing: when power is marginal (heavy load, high DA), a running landing allows the helicopter to land while still moving forward — maintaining translational lift until the skids or wheels touch. This is safer than trying to come to a zero-groundspeed hover with insufficient power. Requires a suitably long, firm, and clear landing area.
exam_tip: A running landing trades hover capability for safety when power is marginal — land while still moving to keep translational lift helping you.
key_facts: Used when power margin is insufficient for hover-in hover-out landing
Running landing uses a longer, clear, firm surface
Maintains translational lift during touchdown — reduces power required
Groundspeed at touchdown should be minimal but not zero
Requires a suitable landing surface — not suitable for rough terrain

syllabus_desc: Define autorotation.
concept: Autorotation is the aerodynamic condition in which a helicopter's rotor continues to produce lift and control without any engine power input. When the engine fails (or is disconnected), the pilot immediately lowers collective to reduce blade pitch — this allows the rotor to continue spinning freely, driven by the upward airflow through the disc as the helicopter descends. The rotor acts like a windmill, with the airflow providing energy to sustain rotation.
exam_tip: Autorotation: rotor driven by upward airflow through descending disc — not by the engine. Lower collective immediately on engine failure to enter autorotation and prevent rotor RPM decay.
key_facts: Autorotation: engine disconnected, rotor driven by aerodynamic forces
Upward airflow through descending disc sustains rotor RPM
Entry: immediately lower collective to reduce drag on rotor
Freewheeling unit disconnects engine from rotor during autorotation
Aircraft still fully controllable — cyclic, collective, and pedals all work

syllabus_desc: Describe the factors involved when transitioning into an autorotation from: the hover at altitude.
concept: Autorotative RPM is a function of blade pitch, descent rate, and airspeed. Lower collective reduces blade pitch, increasing RPM (the rotor spins faster in autorotation when collective is low). The pilot controls RRPM in autorotation with collective — raising collective increases drag and reduces RRPM; lowering collective reduces drag and increases RRPM. Maintaining RRPM within the green arc is essential for rotor efficiency and for the energy available for the flare.
exam_tip: In autorotation, collective controls RRPM: raising collective reduces RRPM; lowering collective increases RRPM. Keep RRPM in the green arc.
key_facts: RRPM in autorotation controlled by collective pitch
Low collective = low blade drag = higher RRPM
High collective = more drag = lower RRPM
RRPM must remain within approved limits (green arc)
High RRPM at beginning of flare provides more energy for landing flare

syllabus_desc: Describe the factors involved when transitioning into an autorotation from: forward flight.
concept: The forces on the rotor blade in autorotation include: drag (opposing rotation), centrifugal force (pulling the blade outward along the span), and lift from the relative airflow striking the blade at a positive angle of attack. In autorotation, the resultant of drag and lift (the total aerodynamic reaction) is tilted forward of the blade's axis of rotation — this forward component is what drives the blade to keep rotating, converting potential energy (height) into kinetic energy (rotor RPM).
exam_tip: In autorotation, the aerodynamic resultant force on the blade has a forward-tilting component that drives the rotation — the disc is extracting energy from the descending airflow.
key_facts: Total aerodynamic reaction tilted forward of rotation axis in driving region
Forward-tilted TAR drives continued blade rotation
Outer blade tips are in the driving region
Inner blade sections (near hub) are in the stalled region
Middle annular region: in equilibrium — neither driving nor braking

syllabus_desc: Identify, on a diagram, the stalled, driven and driving regions (sections) of a rotor disc in autorotation.
concept: The autorotating rotor disc is divided into three distinct regions based on the direction of the aerodynamic resultant force on each blade element. The driven (stalled) region occupies the inner portion near the hub — blades here are at high angle of attack, stalled, and producing drag that retards rotation. The driving region occupies the middle annular portion — blades generate a forward-tilted lift resultant that drives continued rotation. The propeller (windmilling) region occupies the outer tips — blades are at low angle of attack and create a retarding drag.
exam_tip: Three regions of the autorotating disc: inner driven/stalled region (retards), middle driving region (sustains rotation), outer propeller region (slightly retards). Know them all.
key_facts: Inner region (~0–30% radius): driven/stalled — retards rotation
Middle region (~30–70% radius): driving — sustains RRPM
Outer tip region: propeller region — slightly retards rotation
Driving region must dominate for RRPM stability
Changing collective shifts the boundary between regions

syllabus_desc: Describe the forces acting on the stalled, driven and driving regions of a rotor in autorotation.
concept: Entry into autorotation: the key is immediate action — lower collective to the floor simultaneously as the engine failure is recognised. This prevents RRPM decay below safe limits. Delay in lowering collective allows RRPM to droop; at very low RRPM, the rotor cannot generate enough lift for a controlled landing even if collective is then lowered. The freewheeling unit disconnects the engine automatically once the rotor is turning faster than the engine.
exam_tip: The most critical point in autorotation is the first second after engine failure — lower collective immediately to prevent RRPM decay. Hesitation means RRPM droops and recovery becomes impossible.
key_facts: Lower collective immediately on engine failure
Delay = RRPM decay = potentially unrecoverable rotor
Freewheeling unit disconnects engine automatically
Maintain RRPM in green arc throughout descent
Entry time from power loss to stable autorotation is typically 1–3 seconds

syllabus_desc: Describe the combined effect of all regions of a rotor in autorotation.
concept: The combined effect of all three regions determines whether the rotor speeds up, slows down, or maintains stable RRPM in autorotation. At equilibrium, the driving moment from the middle region exactly balances the retarding moments from the inner and outer regions. If collective is reduced, the driving region expands and RRPM increases until a new equilibrium is found. If collective is raised, the driving region shrinks, drag increases, and RRPM decreases.
exam_tip: Equilibrium in autorotation: driving region torque = driven + propeller region drag. Collective shifts the balance. This is how the pilot controls RRPM in autorotation.
key_facts: Equilibrium: driving moment = retarding moments from other regions
Lower collective → larger driving region → RRPM increases to new equilibrium
Higher collective → smaller driving region → RRPM decreases
Stable autorotation RRPM is the self-regulating balance point
The pilot can adjust RRPM using collective throughout the descent

syllabus_desc: Explain the effect of airspeed on the angle of attack of a rotor blade during autorotation.
concept: In autorotation, the angle of attack of each rotor blade element is determined by the vector sum of the rotational velocity and the inflow velocity (upward through the disc due to the descent). At low forward airspeeds, the inflow is nearly vertical and uniform. At higher forward airspeeds, the advancing blade experiences higher rotational relative velocity (increasing AoA slightly) while the retreating blade experiences lower relative velocity. Forward speed therefore changes the AoA distribution across the disc, shifting the driving and driven regions.
exam_tip: AoA in autorotation is determined by combining rotational velocity and upward inflow. Higher airspeed shifts the AoA distribution and the driving region boundaries.
key_facts: AoA = resultant of rotational velocity and upward inflow velocity vectors
Higher inflow (faster descent) increases AoA and extends the driving region
Forward flight complicates the AoA distribution — retreating/advancing asymmetry
Higher collective pitch reduces the driving AoA region
Lower collective pitch keeps AoA in the driving range for more of the disc

syllabus_desc: Describe the effect of forward flight on the distribution of autorotative forces.
concept: In forward flight autorotation, the advancing blade has a higher relative airspeed (rotation speed + forward TAS component), while the retreating blade has a lower relative airspeed. This asymmetry changes the angle of attack distribution across the disc. On the advancing side, the blade element AoA is lower (it flaps up); on the retreating side, AoA is higher. The driving region also shifts — it is more effective on the retreating side at higher forward speeds.
exam_tip: Forward flight creates advancing/retreating blade AoA asymmetry in autorotation, just as in powered flight. The driving region shifts with the asymmetric inflow.
key_facts: Advancing blade: higher relative velocity → lower AoA (flaps up)
Retreating blade: lower relative velocity → higher AoA
Driving region shifts to one side at higher forward speeds
Blade flapping corrects lift asymmetry
Forward speed improves autorotative glide ratio despite disc asymmetry

syllabus_desc: Explain the effect of increased collective pitch on autorotation.
concept: Increasing collective pitch in autorotation raises blade pitch angle, which increases the drag on the rotor blades (blade profile drag and induced drag increase). This retarding effect slows the rotor — RRPM decreases. The driving region shrinks as collective increases, because the blade pitch is now higher than the optimum for self-sustaining rotation. If collective is raised too much, the rotor may slow below safe limits. Collective must be managed carefully to maintain RRPM in the green arc.
exam_tip: Higher collective in autorotation = more blade drag = lower RRPM. Don't raise collective until the flare — you'll deplete RRPM before landing.
key_facts: Increased collective raises blade pitch and drag
Rotor braking effect: RRPM decreases with increased collective
Driving region shrinks as collective increases
At some collective setting, the rotor cannot sustain rotation — stall of autorotation
Collective must be held low during descent to maintain green-arc RRPM

syllabus_desc: Explain the effect of rotor RPM and airspeed on autorotational rate of descent.
concept: RRPM affects the autorotative rate of descent: higher RRPM means the rotor disc generates more thrust for any given collective setting, which can reduce descent rate or allow a more effective flare. Lower RRPM means less thrust available and higher descent rate. Airspeed also affects descent rate — best glide airspeed gives the lowest descent rate for the horizontal distance covered; below this speed, descent rate increases; above it, extra parasite drag worsens glide performance.
exam_tip: Higher RRPM = more rotor energy = more effective flare and lower descent rate. Best glide airspeed minimises descent rate per unit of horizontal distance.
key_facts: Higher RRPM increases thrust and reduces descent rate (for same collective)
Below best glide speed: descent rate increases rapidly
Above best glide speed: increased parasite drag worsens glide
Best glide airspeed minimises ROD per unit of horizontal distance
RRPM and airspeed management together determine autorotative performance

syllabus_desc: Explain the effect of increased altitude and increased weight on rotor RPM in autorotation.
concept: Increased altitude: at higher altitude, the air is thinner. The rotor must spin at higher RRPM to generate the same thrust from thinner air. As a result, autorotative RRPM is slightly higher at altitude, but the rotor energy (½Iω²) may be similar or even less due to changes in inertia and rotor efficiency. Increased weight: a heavier helicopter requires more thrust to sustain descent at the same rate, which requires higher RRPM. In practice, heavier helicopters have higher autorotative RRPM for the same collective setting, but also have a higher descent rate.
exam_tip: Higher altitude → higher autorotative RRPM (thinner air needs faster spinning). Higher weight → higher RRPM needed to generate required thrust.
key_facts: Altitude: thinner air requires higher RRPM for same thrust
Weight: heavier aircraft requires more thrust → higher RRPM for same descent rate
Both effects interact — high altitude and heavy weight compound RRPM demands
Rotor RPM limits must not be exceeded even in autorotation
Monitor RRPM at all times — limits apply in autorotation too

syllabus_desc: Identify on a graph, range and endurance speeds for autorotation.
concept: On an autorotative performance graph (altitude versus airspeed or rate of descent versus airspeed), the best endurance speed in autorotation corresponds to the minimum rate of descent — the bottom of the ROD curve. The best range speed (maximum glide distance) is found at the point where the tangent from the origin touches the ROD curve — this is the speed with the lowest ROD per knot of airspeed, giving the greatest horizontal distance.
exam_tip: Best endurance autorotation: minimum ROD (bottom of curve). Best range autorotation: tangent from origin to ROD curve. Know how to identify both on a graph.
key_facts: Best endurance: minimum ROD airspeed = bottom of the curve
Best range: tangent point from origin to ROD curve
Best range speed is slightly faster than best endurance speed
On the graph: find the tangent from origin touching the ROD curve
Both speeds specified in the AFM for the specific aircraft

syllabus_desc: Explain the effect of the following factors on the range and endurance in autorotation: altitude.
concept: At higher altitude in autorotation, the air density is lower. The rotor requires higher RRPM to develop the same thrust, which marginally changes the descent rate. In terms of range, TAS is higher at altitude for the same IAS — so the same glide angle covers more ground distance. Endurance may be slightly shorter at altitude (higher descent rate in fpm), but range can improve because of higher TAS. Use the AFM charts for the actual altitude being flown.
exam_tip: At altitude in autorotation: TAS is higher → more ground covered for same glide angle → better range. Endurance may decrease slightly.
key_facts: Higher altitude: TAS greater than IAS — covers more ground
Autorotative range generally increases at altitude
Endurance (time airborne) may decrease slightly at altitude
Rotor RRPM slightly higher at altitude
Use AFM performance charts adjusted for actual pressure altitude

syllabus_desc: Explain the effect of the following factors on the range and endurance in autorotation: gross weight.
concept: Higher gross weight increases the autorotative descent rate — the rotor must generate more thrust and the potential energy (weight × height) is lost faster. Range performance in autorotation is less sensitive to weight (glide ratio depends mainly on aerodynamic efficiency, similar to fixed-wing L/D); but endurance decreases with weight because the descent rate is higher. A heavier helicopter also has more energy for the flare — the landing flare must be managed carefully to avoid over-RRPM.
exam_tip: Higher weight → higher descent rate → less endurance. But glide ratio (range) is less affected since L/D depends on aerodynamics, not weight.
key_facts: Higher weight increases autorotative descent rate
Endurance (time airborne) decreases with higher weight
Range is less sensitive to weight (glide ratio similar)
Heavier aircraft requires more precise flare technique at landing
AUW affects the power of the landing cushion — more energy available but also more to arrest

syllabus_desc: Explain the effect of the following factors on the range and endurance in autorotation: parasite drag.
concept: Parasite drag in autorotation (from airframe, hub, or external loads) increases the total drag on the helicopter at any given airspeed. This worsens the glide ratio — more altitude is lost per unit of horizontal distance. Best glide airspeed may shift slightly. Range decreases with increased parasite drag; endurance is less affected (minimum ROD speed shifts but minimum ROD value changes less). Streamlining the helicopter improves autorotative range.
exam_tip: Parasite drag hurts autorotative range — more drag = worse glide ratio. Keep the aircraft clean; no unnecessary external drag items.
key_facts: Parasite drag increases total drag → worse glide ratio
Range decreases with increased parasite drag
Best glide airspeed shifts with increased parasite drag
Endurance less affected than range
External loads or non-streamlined items worsen range in autorotation

syllabus_desc: Explain the effect of the following factors on the range and endurance in autorotation: external loads.
concept: External loads (slingloads, floats, camera mounts) add both weight and parasite drag. The weight component increases descent rate (reducing endurance); the drag component worsens glide ratio (reducing range). Together, these effects significantly degrade autorotative performance. Autorotation with an external load requires very careful management — the load may swing, affecting stability, and the increased weight and drag demand precise technique in the flare.
exam_tip: External loads degrade both range and endurance in autorotation — they add weight and drag. Autorotation with slingloads requires extra precision.
key_facts: External loads add weight → higher descent rate → less endurance
External loads add drag → worse glide ratio → less range
Load swing in autorotation can affect directional stability
Flare with external load requires more anticipation
Check autorotative performance charts for the specific load configuration

syllabus_desc: Explain the effect of the following factors on the range and endurance in autorotation: the wind.
concept: Wind has a significant effect on autorotative range over the ground. A headwind reduces groundspeed, improving the ground glide ratio — more altitude is converted to horizontal distance in the face of the headwind. A tailwind increases groundspeed and worsens the ground glide ratio. For selecting a landing area from a given height in autorotation, a headwind component is strongly preferred — it maximises the options available.
exam_tip: Headwind dramatically improves autorotative range over the ground. Into-wind autorotations give more landing area options.
key_facts: Headwind reduces groundspeed → improves ground glide ratio
Tailwind increases groundspeed → reduces ground glide ratio
Wind has no effect on airspeed-referenced ROD
Select landing sites upwind if possible in forced landing
Strong headwind may make the autorotation appear very steep to an observer — actual glide ratio is fine

syllabus_desc: Explain the transition from autorotation to landing.
concept: The transition from autorotation to landing involves the flare and cushion phases. At flare height (typically 40–100 ft AGL for most types), the pilot applies aft cyclic, which increases disc AoA, arrests descent, and decelerates the aircraft while RRPM rises. As speed and height reduce, collective is progressively raised (the cushion phase), converting stored RRPM energy into a final burst of thrust to cushion the touchdown. The aircraft must be approximately level laterally and longitudinally at touchdown.
exam_tip: Autorotation landing: flare (aft cyclic at 40–100 ft) → cushion (raise collective as speed reduces) → touchdown. Timing the collective is the critical skill.
key_facts: Flare height: approximately 40–100 ft AGL (aircraft-specific — check AFM)
Aft cyclic flare: arrests descent, decelerates, raises RRPM momentarily
Collective raise (cushion): converts remaining RRPM into thrust
Collective must not be raised too early — RRPM will decay before touchdown
Touchdown: aircraft level, minimum vertical and horizontal speed

syllabus_desc: Describe the purpose of the Height/Velocity diagram (avoid curve).
concept: The Height/Velocity (H/V) diagram, also called the avoid curve, shows combinations of height AGL and airspeed from which a safe autorotation and landing cannot be made following an engine failure. The two unsafe areas are: the 'low, slow' region (insufficient altitude and low airspeed to enter a stable autorotation and flare before hitting the ground) and the 'low, fast' region (too close to the ground when flying fast — no time to flare). Operations within the H/V curve boundaries should be avoided except when necessary.
exam_tip: The H/V diagram shows combinations of height and speed where an engine failure is NOT survivable by autorotation. Avoid the curve.
key_facts: H/V diagram: avoid curve defines unsafe height-airspeed combinations
Lower left: low altitude and low airspeed — most dangerous region
Lower right knee: low altitude at high speed — no time to flare
Operations inside the curve: engine failure likely unrecoverable
Chart is specific to aircraft, AUW, and DA — check AFM

syllabus_desc: Explain the hazards involved in operations within the avoid curve.
concept: Operations within the avoid curve are hazardous because: in the low-slow region, there is insufficient altitude to establish autorotation and complete a flare before ground impact; in the low-fast region, high speed means the ground arrives before a flare can be completed. The risk is highest during takeoff (transition from hover to forward flight through the low-slow region) and landing (returning through the low-slow region on approach). These are the phases of flight where most autorotation accidents occur.
exam_tip: Inside the avoid curve = insufficient altitude or time to complete autorotation landing after engine failure. Most engine failure fatalities occur during takeoff and landing — inside the curve.
key_facts: Low-slow region: no height to establish autorotation and flare
Low-fast region: high speed plus low altitude = ground impact before flare
Takeoff transition passes through the avoid curve — plan carefully
Approach transition also passes through — go-around is not always possible inside the curve
Training operations inside the curve (practice autorotations) are conducted from sufficient height

syllabus_desc: Identify, on a graph of the avoid curve, boundaries of safe operation.
concept: On the H/V diagram, the safe zone is outside and above the avoid curve. The curve typically has two lobes: a large lower region (the main avoid area at low altitude and low airspeed) and a small upper-right region at very high altitude (rarely entered in normal operations). The boundary lines show the exact height-airspeed combinations tested by the manufacturer. During takeoff, transit through the curve quickly by accelerating to the recommended transit airspeed.
exam_tip: Safe zone = outside (above/right of) the avoid curve. The curve has a main low-altitude lobe and sometimes an upper lobe. Know how to read the boundaries.
key_facts: Safe zone: above and to the right of the avoid curve
Main lobe: low altitude, low speed region
Upper lobe: very high altitude (less commonly encountered)
Transit boundary: specific height and airspeed for departure transit
AFM specifies the exact H/V diagram for the aircraft type and AUW

syllabus_desc: Explain the meaning of the following types of power required: ancillary power.
concept: Ancillary power is the power required to drive aircraft systems other than the main rotor — including the hydraulic pump, generator, air conditioning, fuel pumps, and avionics. It is a relatively fixed overhead power draw that exists regardless of flight regime. Ancillary power consumption is typically small compared to main rotor power but must be included in the total engine power budget, particularly at high altitude or high AUW.
exam_tip: Ancillary power: fixed overhead for systems (hydraulics, electrics, etc.) — small but real. Always included in the total power budget.
key_facts: Ancillary power: drives aircraft systems (hydraulics, generator, AC, pumps)
Relatively constant power drain regardless of flight regime
Small percentage of total engine power (few percent)
Must be accounted for in total power budget
High ancillary load (e.g., air conditioning) reduces power available for flight

syllabus_desc: Explain the meaning of the following types of power required: profile power.
concept: Profile power is the power required to overcome the aerodynamic drag on the rotating rotor blades themselves — specifically the form drag and skin friction of the blade sections. It is sometimes called 'blade profile drag power.' It is relatively constant across airspeed ranges but increases at very high speeds (where compressibility and retreating blade issues increase blade drag). Profile power depends on rotor RPM, blade area, and blade profile drag coefficient.
exam_tip: Profile power = power to overcome blade drag (form drag + friction of the rotating blades). Relatively constant across speeds, rises at high speed.
key_facts: Profile power: aerodynamic drag on rotating blades (form drag + skin friction)
Relatively constant across most airspeeds
Increases at high airspeeds (compressibility, retreating blade drag)
Depends on RRPM, blade area, and drag coefficient
Not significantly controllable by the pilot in normal operations

syllabus_desc: Explain the meaning of the following types of power required: induced power.
concept: Induced power is the power required to generate the rotor thrust — it is the energy cost of accelerating air downward through the disc to produce lift. Induced power is highest in hover (rotor working in its own disturbed downwash) and decreases as forward airspeed provides cleaner inflow (translational lift). Induced power = rotor thrust × induced velocity, so it decreases with airspeed as induced velocity requirements reduce.
exam_tip: Induced power: the cost of pushing air down to make lift. Highest in hover, decreases with airspeed as inflow improves. This is why hovering uses more power than cruise.
key_facts: Induced power: energy to accelerate air downward for rotor lift
Highest in hover — rotor works in recirculating downwash
Decreases as forward airspeed increases (translational lift improves inflow)
Induced power = thrust × induced velocity
Minimum at mid-range airspeed (best endurance speed)

syllabus_desc: Explain the meaning of the following types of power required: parasite power.
concept: Parasite power is the power required to overcome non-lifting drag — the drag of the airframe, rotor hub, landing gear, pylons, and any external loads. It increases with the cube of airspeed: doubling the airspeed multiplies parasite power by eight. At low speeds it is negligible; at high speeds it is the dominant term and limits maximum airspeed. Streamlining (retractable gear, fairings) reduces parasite drag and improves high-speed performance.
exam_tip: Parasite power ∝ airspeed³. Negligible at low speed, dominant at high speed. This is why helicopter maximum speed is power-limited.
key_facts: Parasite power: overcome airframe, hub, gear, and external load drag
Increases with the cube of airspeed
Doubling speed = 8× the parasite power
Negligible at low speed; dominant at high speed
Streamlined airframes and retractable gear reduce parasite power

syllabus_desc: Describe the factors that affect the amount of: profile power required.
concept: Induced power is the power required to push air downward through the rotor disc to generate lift. In hover, induced power is highest because the rotor must work in its own downwash without the benefit of translational lift. As forward airspeed increases, translational lift reduces the induced velocity required for a given lift, and induced power decreases. Induced power ∝ 1/airspeed (roughly) at medium speeds.
exam_tip: Induced power is highest in hover and decreases as airspeed increases — this is why helicopters use more power to hover than to fly forward.
key_facts: Induced power highest in hover
Decreases as forward airspeed increases
Translational lift reduces induced power requirement
Induced power = rotor thrust × induced velocity
Minimum induced power at best endurance airspeed

syllabus_desc: Describe the factors that affect the amount of: induced power required.
concept: Profile power is the power required to overcome the aerodynamic drag on the rotating rotor blades themselves (form drag and friction). It is relatively constant across forward flight airspeeds but increases slightly at high speeds as retreating blade drag increases. Profile power represents the baseline power cost of simply spinning the rotor regardless of lift being generated.
exam_tip: Profile power is the background power cost of spinning the rotor — relatively constant, but rises at high speed as retreating blade issues increase blade drag.
key_facts: Profile power: drag of rotating blades (form drag + skin friction)
Relatively constant across most of the airspeed range
Increases slightly at high airspeeds
Depends on rotor RPM, blade area, and blade drag coefficient
Cannot be reduced without changing blade design or RPM

syllabus_desc: Describe the factors that affect the amount of: parasite power required.
concept: Parasite power is the power required to overcome non-lifting drag — airframe drag, hub drag, landing gear drag, and any external loads. It increases with the cube of airspeed (doubling airspeed multiplies parasite power by 8). At low airspeeds, parasite power is negligible; at high speeds it becomes the dominant factor limiting top speed.
exam_tip: Parasite power increases with the cube of airspeed — it's negligible at low speed but dominates at high speed. This is why top speed is power-limited.
key_facts: Parasite power ∝ airspeed³
Doubling speed = 8× the parasite power
Negligible at low speeds
Dominant at high speeds — limits maximum airspeed
Streamlined airframes reduce parasite drag coefficient

syllabus_desc: On a graph, identify: induced power required.
concept: The power required curve for a helicopter has a characteristic 'bucket' or bathtub shape: high at low airspeeds (dominated by induced power), dropping to a minimum at mid-range airspeeds (minimum drag speed — best endurance), and rising again at high airspeeds (dominated by parasite power). The minimum point on the curve represents the airspeed of maximum endurance — where power is smallest and fuel burn per hour is lowest.
exam_tip: The power curve is U-shaped: high at low speed (induced power), minimum at mid-speed (best endurance), rising steeply at high speed (parasite power).
key_facts: Low speed: induced power dominates — curve slopes steeply
Mid-range: minimum power required = best endurance airspeed
High speed: parasite power cubed relationship causes steep rise
Best endurance: minimum fuel flow per hour
Best range: slightly faster than best endurance (tangent from origin to power curve)

syllabus_desc: On a graph, identify: parasite power required.
concept: The effect of weight on the power required curve: increasing AUW shifts the entire power required curve upward. More lift is needed, so more induced power is required at all airspeeds. The minimum of the curve also shifts to a slightly higher airspeed. Heavier helicopters require more power to fly at any given airspeed and therefore have less excess power for climb, less range, and less endurance.
exam_tip: More weight = power required curve shifts upward at all airspeeds — the whole curve goes up, not just the low-speed portion.
key_facts: Increasing AUW raises power required at all airspeeds
Minimum power point shifts upward and slightly to higher airspeed
Less excess power for climb and other performance
Best endurance and best range speeds increase slightly with AUW
Maximum AUW must not be exceeded — performance margins disappear

syllabus_desc: On a graph, identify: profile power required.
concept: The effect of altitude (density altitude) on the power required curve: at higher density altitude, the rotor must develop more thrust for a given collective setting, and the engine produces less power. The power required curve itself shifts slightly — induced power increases (thinner air = higher induced velocity for same lift), while parasite power decreases (thinner air = less drag for same airspeed). The net effect is that minimum power required occurs at similar or slightly higher airspeed, but the available power margin shrinks dramatically.
exam_tip: At high DA: engine power available drops, induced power required rises, but parasite power reduces slightly. Net result: dramatically smaller excess power margin.
key_facts: Higher DA: engine power available decreases significantly
Induced power required increases at high DA (thinner air)
Parasite power decreases at high DA (less air resistance)
Overall excess power reduces — less performance margin
High DA operations require careful power management

syllabus_desc: On a graph, identify: total power required.
concept: Speed for minimum power required (best endurance speed): this is the bottom of the power required curve, where fuel flow (power × BSFC) is at its minimum. Flying at this speed gives the greatest time in the air per unit of fuel. It's typically slower than the best range speed. The exact speed is given in the AFM.
exam_tip: Minimum power speed = best endurance speed = bottom of the power curve = most time in the air per litre of fuel.
key_facts: Best endurance = minimum power required airspeed
Gives maximum time airborne per unit of fuel
Slower than best range speed
Located at minimum of power required curve
Useful for search and rescue or extended loiter operations

syllabus_desc: Explain the effect, on power required, of: altitude.
concept: At higher altitude (density altitude), induced power required increases because the rotor must spin faster and accelerate air harder to generate the same thrust in thinner air. Profile power remains similar. Parasite power decreases slightly (less air resistance for same TAS). The net effect: induced power increase outweighs parasite power decrease, and total power required typically increases at altitude.
