Simultaneously, engine power available decreases — the combination creates a narrowing power margin.
exam_tip: High altitude: induced power required rises, engine power available falls. The power margin shrinks from both sides — the classic high-altitude performance trap.
key_facts: Induced power required increases at altitude (thinner air)
Parasite power decreases slightly at altitude (less air resistance)
Engine power available decreases with altitude (reduced engine output)
Net: total power required up + power available down = reduced margin
High DA operations: carefully manage power to avoid exceeding limits

syllabus_desc: Explain the effect, on power required, of: weight.
concept: Increased weight raises power required at all airspeeds. More lift is needed, so induced power is higher across the board. Profile power is unaffected by weight. Parasite power is unaffected directly. The power required curve shifts upward, the minimum point rises, and the excess power margin shrinks. The aircraft's range and endurance also reduce because the engine must work harder for the same performance.
exam_tip: More weight → higher induced power required → entire power curve shifts up. Less excess power = degraded performance across all metrics.
key_facts: Higher weight: more lift needed → more induced power at all airspeeds
Power required curve shifts upward with weight
Profile and parasite power unaffected by weight directly
Excess power margin reduces — less climb, range, endurance
Best endurance and range speeds increase slightly with weight

syllabus_desc: Explain the effect, on power required, of: slingloads and parasite drag items.
concept: Slingloads and parasite drag items (floats, cameras, winches, wire cutters) add frontal area and drag to the helicopter. This increases parasite power required, particularly at higher speeds. The power required curve rises at medium and high airspeeds. Best range and endurance speeds may shift. The operator must use performance charts specific to the loaded configuration.
exam_tip: External parasite items raise the power required at cruise speeds — the parasite drag term increases. Check AFM for performance with specific external items fitted.
key_facts: Slingloads and external items add parasite drag
Parasite power required increases — especially at higher airspeeds
Power curve rises at medium/high speeds
Best range speed may decrease (more drag at high speed)
Always use configuration-specific performance data from the AFM

syllabus_desc: Explain the effect, on power required, of: the flare.
concept: The landing flare temporarily increases power required significantly. The aft cyclic input increases disc AoA, generating more thrust and drag on the rotor. RRPM rises momentarily as energy is extracted from airspeed and stored RRPM. Rotor drag is high during the flare as induced power and profile power both spike. The flare converts kinetic energy into rotor thrust — it is an energy exchange, not an additional power input from the engine.
exam_tip: The flare spikes power required momentarily — it's a high-drag, high-thrust event that converts kinetic energy into rotor thrust. No additional engine power needed (in autorotation); in powered flight, the engine may briefly be at or near limit.
key_facts: Flare: aft cyclic increases disc AoA and rotor thrust
Induced and profile drag both increase during flare
RRPM rises then may decrease as energy is used
High power demand — may approach engine limit in powered flare
In autorotation: energy comes from RRPM and airspeed kinetic energy, not engine

syllabus_desc: Explain the effect, on power required, of: acceleration.
concept: Acceleration requires additional power above and beyond level flight power because the rotor must provide both the power to maintain altitude and the power to accelerate the aircraft mass. Power required during acceleration is temporarily higher than for steady-state flight at the same airspeed. Once cruise speed is reached, power reverts to the steady-state value. Rapid acceleration from hover can briefly load the engine to near-maximum levels.
exam_tip: Acceleration requires extra power above the steady-state level flight value — the engine must do work to both maintain altitude and accelerate the mass.
key_facts: Acceleration requires additional power above level flight value
Extra power = force × distance = kinetic energy gained per second
Rapid acceleration can briefly reach engine limits
Once target speed is reached, power returns to steady-state cruise value
Deceleration (flare) returns this power — energy is recovered

syllabus_desc: Explain the effect, on power required, of: ground effect.
concept: Ground effect reduces power required for hover. Within approximately one rotor diameter of the surface, the rotor disc operates in a cushion of compressed air that increases effective disc efficiency — the induced velocity required to generate a given thrust is lower. This means less induced power is required in ground effect compared to out of ground effect. A helicopter that can't hover OGE may hover IGE — but power chart entries for IGE and OGE hovering are different and must be used correctly.
exam_tip: Ground effect reduces induced power required — rotor is more efficient near the surface. Always use OGE hover charts when planning OGE operations.
key_facts: Ground effect: induced velocity decreases within ~1 rotor diameter of surface
Less induced power required IGE vs OGE
Power savings of 10–15% typical in ground effect
Hover ceiling IGE is always higher than OGE
Never plan OGE operations based on IGE power checks

syllabus_desc: Explain the effect, on power required, of: rotor RPM.
concept: Rotor RPM directly affects power required. Higher RRPM increases profile power (blade drag scales with velocity squared). Lower RRPM reduces profile power but may reduce rotor efficiency and thrust for a given collective setting — requiring more collective, which increases induced power. The design RRPM is optimised for minimum total power required. Deviating from design RPM in either direction increases total power required.
exam_tip: Design RRPM is optimised for minimum total power. Too high: profile power rises. Too low: induced power rises (more collective needed). Stay in the green arc.
key_facts: Higher RRPM: profile power increases (∝ RPM²)
Lower RRPM: less thrust per blade pass → more collective needed → higher induced power
Design RPM is optimised for minimum total power
RRPM must remain in the green arc (normal operating range)
Avoid low RRPM situations — they're inefficient and reduce control effectiveness

syllabus_desc: Explain the effect, on power required, of: increased temperature.
concept: Increased temperature reduces air density (density altitude increases). The rotor must work harder for the same thrust, increasing induced power required. The engine also produces less power at higher temperatures. Net effect: power required rises, power available falls — the margin shrinks. Hot days are particularly problematic when combined with high-altitude operations. Preflight power checks are essential in hot conditions.
exam_tip: Hot temperatures increase density altitude — induced power required rises, engine output drops. The classic 'hot and high' trap where performance falls off a cliff.
key_facts: Higher temperature reduces air density (increases density altitude)
Induced power required increases (rotor less efficient in thinner air)
Engine power available decreases at higher temperature
Margin between power required and power available shrinks
Hot summer days at elevated aerodromes require thorough performance planning

syllabus_desc: Explain the effect, on power required, of: wind.
concept: Wind affects power required primarily through its effect on translational lift and retreating blade loading. A headwind component provides translational lift at lower forward airspeeds — reducing induced power required at lower speeds compared to calm conditions. A tailwind requires the helicopter to fly faster (relative to the air) to maintain the same groundspeed, which may increase parasite power. In hover, a headwind reduces power required by providing translational lift in what would otherwise be a pure hover condition.
exam_tip: Headwind reduces power required in hover and at low speed by providing translational lift. Calm days are the hardest — no wind assistance at all.
key_facts: Headwind provides translational lift at lower airspeeds
Reduces induced power required in windy conditions vs calm
Hover in a headwind uses less power than a no-wind hover
Tailwind at cruise: must fly faster IAS to maintain groundspeed → more parasite power
Wind planning should include its effect on power required

syllabus_desc: On a graph, draw a power available curve for a typical light piston engine helicopter.
concept: The power available curve for a typical light piston engine helicopter is relatively flat across the airspeed range — the engine produces a fairly constant shaft power output (limited by manifold pressure, RPM, and mixture) regardless of airspeed. It may decline slightly at very high airspeeds due to transmission or rotor limitations. On a power versus airspeed graph, it appears as a nearly horizontal line well above the power required curve at low airspeeds, with the two curves converging at maximum airspeed (where VNE is limited by power or retreating blade stall).
exam_tip: Power available curve for a piston helicopter = nearly horizontal line. The gap between PA and PR curves = excess power for climb. The two curves meet at maximum airspeed.
key_facts: Power available curve: approximately flat (constant power) across airspeeds
Determined by: manifold pressure, RPM, mixture, temperature, altitude
Decreases with altitude (less dense air reduces engine output)
Gap between PA and PR = excess power (available for climb and performance)
Curves converge at maximum airspeed — VNE region

syllabus_desc: Describe the effect of the following on the power available curve: changes in collective setting.
concept: Collective setting does not change the power available — it changes the power required. The power available curve is set by the engine output (throttle, RPM, mixture, density altitude). Collective pitch determines how that available power is distributed between induced, profile, and parasite components. Raising collective increases power required; if power required exceeds power available, the helicopter cannot maintain level flight at that speed.
exam_tip: Collective changes power required, not power available. Power available is set by the engine — collective decides how much of it you're using.
key_facts: Collective changes power required, not power available
Power available is set by engine (throttle/RPM/mixture/DA)
Raising collective increases blade pitch and power required
If power required > power available: aircraft cannot maintain altitude
Power available curve remains unchanged with collective setting

syllabus_desc: Describe the effect of the following on the power available curve: altitude.
concept: As altitude increases, air density decreases. A normally aspirated piston engine's power output falls with altitude because the engine ingests less air mass per cycle. The power available curve drops downward (less power at all airspeeds). Simultaneously, the power required curve rises slightly (induced power increases in thinner air). The combined effect is a rapid reduction in excess power — the gap between PA and PR shrinks significantly with altitude.
exam_tip: Altitude lowers the PA curve while raising the PR curve — excess power is squeezed from both sides. The higher you go, the less performance you have.
key_facts: Higher altitude: PA curve moves downward (less engine power)
PR curve moves slightly upward (induced power increases)
Excess power (gap between curves) shrinks rapidly with altitude
Service ceiling: PA and PR curves nearly touch (ROC = 100 fpm)
Turbocharged engines maintain PA curve to higher altitudes

syllabus_desc: Describe the effect of the following on the power available curve: density altitude.
concept: Density altitude is the altitude in the International Standard Atmosphere corresponding to the actual air density. High density altitude (hot, high, humid) has the same effect on power available as increased altitude — the PA curve drops. For performance planning, density altitude is used to find power available in the AFM, rather than just pressure altitude. On a warm day at a high aerodrome, the density altitude can be many thousands of feet above the actual aerodrome elevation.
exam_tip: Density altitude determines PA — not just pressure altitude. A hot day at a high aerodrome may have a density altitude of 4,000–5,000 ft above actual elevation.
key_facts: Density altitude = pressure altitude + temperature correction
High DA: PA curve drops (as if at higher altitude)
PA determined using density altitude in AFM performance charts
Hot, humid days increase DA even at low aerodromes
Always compute DA before operations — don't use field elevation alone

syllabus_desc: Describe the effect of the following on the power available curve: leaning of the mixture.
concept: Mixture leaning increases engine power at altitude. At sea level, full rich mixture provides the correct fuel/air ratio, but as altitude increases, air density drops and the mixture becomes too rich (excess fuel, insufficient air), reducing power. Leaning (reducing fuel flow) restores the correct ratio and recovers power. At altitude, a properly leaned engine can recover significant power compared to a rich setting — the PA curve rises when mixture is optimally adjusted.
exam_tip: Lean the mixture at altitude to recover power — too rich means wasted fuel and reduced engine output. Leaning raises the PA curve at altitude.
key_facts: Rich mixture at altitude: excess fuel, less air mass → reduced power
Leaning restores optimal fuel/air ratio → power increases
Correctly leaned mixture can recover 10–15% power at altitude
PA curve rises with optimum leaning at altitude
Never lean in the pattern or at low altitude — follow AFM procedures

syllabus_desc: Explain the factors affecting power excess.
concept: Power excess is the difference between power available and power required. It determines climb performance, ceiling, and manoeuvring capability. Factors that reduce power excess include: increased AUW (raises PR), increased altitude (lowers PA and raises PR), high density altitude (lowers PA), rich mixture at altitude (lowers PA), external loads (raises PR), and bank angles in turns (raises PR). Factors that increase power excess: lower AUW, lower altitude, optimal mixture, clean configuration, and tailwind (reduces induced power requirement at low speeds).
exam_tip: Power excess = PA minus PR. Everything that raises PR or lowers PA reduces your power excess — and your safety margins.
key_facts: Power excess = PA − PR
Reduces with: high AUW, high altitude, high DA, external loads, steep turns
Increases with: low AUW, low altitude, correct leaning, clean configuration
Zero power excess = level flight only — no climb capability
Negative power excess = aircraft descending even at max power

syllabus_desc: Explain the effect of lowering the power available curve.
concept: Lowering the power available curve (through altitude increase, engine deterioration, high DA, or rich mixture) reduces the excess power gap at all airspeeds. The immediate effect is a reduced rate of climb. If the PA curve is lowered enough, it may intersect the PR curve — at that point, level flight is impossible above certain airspeeds. VNE effectively reduces because the high-speed end of the PA and PR curves converge lower and sooner. Performance reductions are proportional to how much the PA curve has dropped.
exam_tip: Lowering the PA curve reduces excess power proportionally — climb degrades first, then maximum level flight speed, and finally the aircraft cannot maintain altitude at any speed.
key_facts: PA curve drop → reduced excess power at all airspeeds
Rate of climb decreases proportionally
If PA curve drops below PR minimum: aircraft cannot maintain level flight
Maximum airspeed (where PA and PR intersect) decreases
Engine deterioration has the same effect as altitude on PA

syllabus_desc: Explain the requirement for a power check.
concept: A power check is required to confirm that the engine is producing power consistent with the reference values in the performance charts for the actual conditions. Before any operation with marginal power margins — confined area, high AUW, OGE hover, high DA — the pilot must verify actual power output equals expected power output. An engine performing below specification will have a PA curve lower than charted — all performance figures are then invalid. The power check is the go/no-go decision point for demanding tasks.
exam_tip: The power check verifies that the actual PA curve matches the charted PA curve. Without it, you don't know if your performance planning is valid.
key_facts: Power check confirms engine output matches performance chart reference
Essential before OGE hover, confined area, and high-load operations
Method: set reference power → compare to AFM values for conditions
Engine below specification = PA curve lower than charted = reduced margins
Go/no-go decision before committing to demanding operation

syllabus_desc: With respect to piston engine helicopters, explain the requirement for range flying.
concept: Range flying maximises the distance covered per unit of fuel — measured in nautical miles per litre or similar. This is important for cross-country operations, reaching an alternate aerodrome, fuel planning in remote areas, and avoiding fuel exhaustion. For a piston helicopter, range is maximised at a specific airspeed and power setting where the ratio of fuel flow to airspeed is minimised. Flying too slow or too fast both reduce range.
exam_tip: Range flying = maximum distance per litre of fuel. Critical for cross-country, remote operations, and fuel planning. There's one best speed — fly it.
key_facts: Range: distance per unit of fuel (nm per litre or nm per kg)
Maximised at best range airspeed and power setting
Too slow or too fast: both increase fuel per nm
Critical for cross-country planning, remote area ops
Piston engine: specific best range airspeed in the AFM

syllabus_desc: On a power required graph, identify the speed for best range.
concept: On a power required versus airspeed graph, the best range speed is found by drawing a tangent line from the origin (zero speed, zero power) to the power required curve. The point where the tangent touches the curve is the best range airspeed. At this point, the ratio of power-to-airspeed is minimised, meaning the least power (and fuel) is used per knot of airspeed. This is slightly faster than the best endurance speed (which is at the minimum of the PR curve).
exam_tip: Best range: draw a tangent from the origin to the PR curve — the touch-point is the best range airspeed. Faster than best endurance speed.
key_facts: Best range speed: tangent from origin to PR curve
Minimises power-per-knot-of-airspeed ratio
Faster than best endurance speed (bottom of PR curve)
Can be identified graphically on PR versus airspeed chart
AFM specifies the actual best range airspeed for the aircraft

syllabus_desc: Explain the effect, on helicopter range, of: airspeed.
concept: Flying at the correct best range airspeed maximises distance per fuel burn. Flying slower than best range increases induced power required (worse fuel burn per nm). Flying faster than best range increases parasite power dramatically (fuel burn increases faster than speed gains). The best range speed is a specific optimum — deviating in either direction degrades range. In headwind conditions, best range speed should be flown faster than in still air.
exam_tip: Best range speed = specific optimum. Slower: induced power dominates. Faster: parasite power dominates. In headwind: fly faster than calm best range.
key_facts: Best range airspeed is a specific optimum — not 'faster is better'
Slower than best range: induced power increases fuel burn per nm
Faster than best range: parasite power cubed increase wastes fuel
Headwind: fly faster than best range to compensate for reduced groundspeed
Tailwind: fly slower than best range (groundspeed already higher)

syllabus_desc: Explain the effect, on helicopter range, of: wind.
concept: Wind significantly affects range over the ground. A headwind reduces groundspeed, so more fuel is burned per nautical mile — range decreases. A tailwind increases groundspeed, improving nm per litre — range increases. To compensate for a headwind, fly slightly faster than the still-air best range speed (trades some efficiency for groundspeed recovery). In a strong headwind, there may be a speed that maximises range over the ground despite the headwind.
exam_tip: Headwind reduces range; tailwind increases it. In a headwind, fly slightly faster than still-air best range to partially compensate.
key_facts: Headwind reduces range (more fuel per nm due to reduced groundspeed)
Tailwind increases range (better nm per litre due to increased groundspeed)
In headwind: fly faster than still-air best range speed
In tailwind: fly at or slightly slower than still-air best range
Strong headwind may make alternative routing more fuel-efficient

syllabus_desc: Explain the effect, on helicopter range, of: altitude.
concept: Altitude affects range through its impact on TAS and power required. At higher altitude, TAS is greater for the same IAS — covering more distance for the same airspeed. Also, piston engines can maintain power efficiency better at altitude with proper leaning. However, the engine power available decreases without turbocharging. For a normally aspirated piston helicopter, there is usually an optimum altitude for best range that balances these effects. In NZ operations, terrain often limits the ability to fly at optimum range altitude.
exam_tip: Altitude can improve range by increasing TAS and enabling mixture leaning — but only to the point where engine power loss doesn't negate the gain.
key_facts: Higher altitude: TAS greater than IAS → more nm per hour for same power
Proper mixture leaning at altitude recovers engine efficiency
Normally aspirated engines lose power at high altitude (limits optimum range altitude)
There is an optimum range altitude — above it, range decreases
In NZ, terrain often constrains altitude choice

syllabus_desc: Explain the effect, on helicopter range, of: weight.
concept: Higher weight increases power required at all airspeeds (more induced power needed for more lift). This means more fuel is burned per hour at the best range airspeed, reducing nm per litre. Heavier aircraft have a shorter range for the same fuel load. The best range airspeed shifts slightly higher with weight. Fuel load is part of AUW, so as fuel burns off, AUW decreases and range efficiency slowly improves during the flight.
exam_tip: Higher weight = more power required = more fuel burned = less range. As fuel burns off, weight reduces and efficiency improves through the flight.
key_facts: Higher weight raises PR curve — more fuel burned at all airspeeds
Range decreases with increased AUW
Best range airspeed increases slightly with weight
AUW decreases during flight as fuel burns — efficiency improves
Passengers and freight directly affect range — compute carefully

syllabus_desc: Explain the effect, on helicopter range, of: slingloads and parasite drag items.
concept: Slingloads and external drag items (floats, camera mounts, wire cutters) increase parasite drag. This raises the power required at cruise airspeeds, increasing fuel burn and reducing range. The best range airspeed also shifts lower (less benefit from flying fast with high drag). Operators should always compute range using performance data that accounts for the specific external load fitted — not the clean helicopter figures.
exam_tip: External drag items worsen range by increasing parasite power required at cruise speeds. Use specific configuration performance data.
key_facts: External loads add parasite drag → higher PR at cruise speeds
More fuel burned per nm → reduced range
Best range airspeed shifts lower with high drag items
Significant range penalty — can be 15–30% reduction for large slingloads
Always use load-specific performance data for range planning

syllabus_desc: Explain the effect, on helicopter range, of: engine RPM.
concept: Engine RPM affects both power available and fuel consumption. For a piston helicopter, lower RPM combined with higher manifold pressure (long range cruise technique) can sometimes improve fuel economy — the engine produces the same power but with fewer power strokes, reducing fuel burn per cycle. However, this must be done within manufacturer limits. Reducing RPM below recommended limits can cause rough running, excess heat, and detonation. Always follow AFM RPM and power setting recommendations.
exam_tip: Engine RPM affects fuel efficiency — the AFM specifies the RPM and MAP combination for best range. Never deviate from manufacturer limits to 'improve' economy.
key_facts: RPM affects both power available and specific fuel consumption
Lower RPM + higher MAP can improve economy (within limits)
Must remain within manufacturer-approved RPM range
Rough running, detonation, or overheating indicate incorrect power settings
Best range RPM and MAP specified in AFM — follow it

syllabus_desc: Describe the range configuration for piston and turbine helicopters.
concept: Range configuration for a piston helicopter: fly at the best range airspeed as specified in the AFM, at the recommended power setting (RPM and MAP), with optimal mixture (leaned per the EGT or AFM procedure), at the most efficient altitude, and in the cleanest aerodynamic configuration. For a turbine helicopter, range configuration involves flying at maximum range TAS and adjusting fuel control to minimum fuel flow for the required torque. Turbine engines are more efficient at high altitude, so range configuration often involves flying higher.
exam_tip: Range configuration: best range airspeed + optimal RPM/MAP + leaned mixture (piston) or minimum fuel flow (turbine) + best altitude. It's a package of settings, not just one thing.
key_facts: Piston range config: AFM best range airspeed + optimum RPM/MAP + leaned mixture
Turbine range config: best range TAS + high altitude + minimum fuel flow
Clean aerodynamic configuration (landing gear up if applicable)
Optimal altitude: balances TAS gain against engine power loss (normally aspirated)
Monitor fuel flow against AFM reference during cruise

syllabus_desc: With respect to piston engine helicopters, explain the requirement for endurance flying.
concept: Endurance flying maximises the time airborne per unit of fuel — measured in hours per litre. This matters for SAR operations, waiting for weather to clear, holding before descent, or any situation where time in the air (not distance) is the priority. Best endurance is achieved at the minimum power required airspeed — where fuel flow is lowest. Flying slower or faster than this speed both increase fuel consumption and reduce endurance.
exam_tip: Endurance flying = maximum time per litre of fuel. Minimum power = minimum fuel flow = maximum time. The opposite goal from range.
key_facts: Endurance: time airborne per unit of fuel
Maximised at minimum power required airspeed
Critical for SAR, holding, weather waiting
Too slow or too fast: both increase fuel burn per hour
Piston: minimum fuel flow at specific airspeed and power setting

syllabus_desc: On a power required graph, identify the speed for best endurance.
concept: On a power required versus airspeed graph, the best endurance speed is at the minimum of the power required curve — the lowest point. At this speed, total power required (and therefore fuel flow) is at its minimum. The helicopter is using the least fuel per unit time. This is slower than the best range speed and is found at the bottom of the 'bathtub' shaped power required curve.
exam_tip: Best endurance = bottom of the PR curve (minimum power required = minimum fuel flow). Slower than best range. Find the lowest point on the PR graph.
key_facts: Best endurance: minimum of the power required curve
Slower than best range airspeed
Minimum fuel flow per hour at this speed
Located at the lowest point of the PR vs airspeed graph
AFM specifies exact best endurance airspeed

syllabus_desc: Explain the effect, on helicopter endurance, of: airspeed.
concept: Flying at the best endurance airspeed minimises fuel flow and maximises time airborne. Flying slower than best endurance: induced power increases and fuel flow rises, reducing endurance. Flying faster: parasite power increases rapidly and fuel flow rises, also reducing endurance. The optimum is precisely at the minimum of the PR curve. In practice, the curve is fairly flat near the minimum, so small speed variations have relatively small endurance penalties.
exam_tip: Fly at best endurance speed for maximum time. Deviate in either direction and fuel flow rises. The curve is fairly flat near the minimum — don't obsess over 2 knots.
key_facts: Best endurance airspeed = minimum fuel flow = maximum time
Slower: induced power increases → less endurance
Faster: parasite power increases → less endurance
PR curve is relatively flat near the minimum (small speed errors tolerable)
Wind does not change the best endurance airspeed (only range speed shifts)

syllabus_desc: Explain the effect, on helicopter endurance, of: wind.
concept: Wind has no direct effect on endurance — endurance is a measure of time, not distance. Fuel flow at the best endurance airspeed is the same regardless of wind (assuming same TAS). However, if you're trying to maintain a position over a point on the ground (e.g., holding or SAR), a headwind requires higher TAS to stay over the point, increasing fuel flow and reducing endurance. For pure time-in-the-air with no position requirement, wind is irrelevant to endurance.
exam_tip: Wind doesn't change endurance for free flight — same TAS = same fuel flow regardless of wind. But if you're holding position, a headwind forces higher TAS and hurts endurance.
key_facts: Endurance = time airborne per fuel unit — wind doesn't change this for free flight
Same TAS = same fuel flow regardless of wind direction
Holding position over a point: headwind requires higher TAS → reduces endurance
SAR: if drifting with wind is acceptable, endurance is unaffected
Endurance planning uses TAS-based fuel flow, not groundspeed

syllabus_desc: Explain the effect, on helicopter endurance, of: altitude.
concept: At higher altitude, TAS is greater for the same IAS. For the same fuel flow, you travel further — so endurance in terms of fuel per hour is similar (as fuel flow depends on engine power, which is altitude-dependent). For normally aspirated piston engines, fuel flow may be slightly higher at altitude if power is maintained. However, with mixture leaning, fuel flow can be reduced at altitude, improving endurance. The relationship is complex — use the AFM.
exam_tip: Altitude effects on endurance are subtle for piston helicopters — leaning the mixture at altitude can improve endurance. Use the AFM performance tables.
key_facts: Altitude: TAS higher but fuel flow may change with altitude
Normally aspirated: power decreases at altitude → potential fuel flow reduction
Properly leaned mixture at altitude can reduce fuel flow → better endurance
Turbine: more fuel-efficient at higher altitude → improved endurance
Use AFM tables for endurance at specific altitude

syllabus_desc: Explain the effect, on helicopter endurance, of: weight.
concept: Higher weight increases induced power required, raising power needed at the best endurance airspeed. This means more fuel is burned per hour, reducing endurance. The best endurance airspeed shifts slightly higher with weight. As fuel burns off during the flight, weight decreases and endurance slowly improves. Heavy helicopters (high AUW) have shorter endurance for the same fuel load.
exam_tip: Higher weight = more induced power required = more fuel burned per hour = less endurance. As fuel burns, AUW drops and endurance improves gradually.
key_facts: Higher AUW increases power required at minimum power speed
More fuel burned per hour → less endurance
Best endurance speed shifts slightly higher with weight
AUW decreases as fuel burns — endurance efficiency improves during flight
Weight management critical for extended SAR or holding operations

syllabus_desc: Explain the effect, on helicopter endurance, of: slingloads and parasite drag items.
concept: Slingloads and external drag items increase both weight (more induced power) and parasite drag (more drag power at all speeds). This raises the minimum point of the power required curve upward — more fuel is burned at the best endurance speed. Additionally, the best endurance speed may shift to a different value with the load fitted. Significant endurance penalties can occur with large external loads.
exam_tip: External loads raise the minimum of the PR curve — more fuel burned per hour even at the best endurance speed. Plan for reduced endurance with any load fitted.
key_facts: External loads: add weight (induced power up) and drag (parasite power up)
Minimum of PR curve rises → more fuel at best endurance speed
Best endurance speed may shift with load fitted
Endurance can be significantly reduced with large slingloads
Use load-specific performance data for endurance planning

syllabus_desc: Describe the endurance configuration for piston and turbine helicopters.
concept: Endurance configuration for a piston helicopter: fly at the best endurance airspeed (AFM specified), at the power setting that gives minimum fuel flow, with mixture correctly leaned, and at an altitude that allows optimal leaning without excessive power loss. For a turbine helicopter, endurance is achieved at the minimum fuel flow power setting for the current conditions — typically at a lower altitude where fuel flow is managed by the fuel controller. Always consult the AFM for exact settings.
exam_tip: Endurance configuration: minimum power airspeed + minimum fuel flow power setting + leaned mixture (piston) + appropriate altitude. Package of settings, check the AFM.
key_facts: Piston endurance config: best endurance IAS + minimum fuel flow power setting + leaned mixture
Turbine endurance config: minimum fuel flow setting at appropriate altitude
Monitor fuel flow — confirm it matches AFM reference value
Lower altitude sometimes better for turbine endurance (fuel controller optimization)
Always use AFM procedures — there's no universal rule

syllabus_desc: Define retreating blade stall.
concept: Retreating blade stall (RBS) occurs when the retreating rotor blade (the blade moving opposite to the direction of flight) reaches a critical angle of attack and stalls aerodynamically. As forward airspeed increases, the retreating blade's relative airspeed is reduced (rotational speed minus forward TAS). To compensate for the reduced velocity and maintain lift symmetry, the blade's pitch (angle of attack) is increased — at high forward speed, this pitch increase causes the blade to stall in the retreating sector.
exam_tip: Retreating blade stall: at high forward speed, the retreating blade slows down aerodynamically and stalls when its AoA is increased to compensate for the reduced relative velocity.
key_facts: Retreating blade stall occurs at high forward airspeed
Retreating blade: rotational speed minus forward TAS = lower relative velocity
To compensate for lower velocity, blade pitch is increased
Pitch eventually reaches critical angle of attack → blade stalls
Limits the maximum forward airspeed (VNE) of helicopters

syllabus_desc: Explain the aerodynamic factors likely to lead to retreating blade stall.
concept: Factors leading to retreating blade stall: (1) high forward airspeed — the primary cause (reduces retreating blade relative velocity); (2) high density altitude — rotor must generate more lift, requiring higher blade pitch (reducing stall margin); (3) high AUW — more lift needed, higher collective, less stall margin; (4) high collective setting — directly increases blade pitch; (5) turbulence or abrupt control inputs — rapid pitch changes can trigger stall; (6) steep banked turns — load factor increases blade pitch demand.
exam_tip: Causes of RBS: high speed + high AUW + high DA + high collective + turbulence. Any combination that increases blade pitch while retreating blade relative velocity is low.
key_facts: High forward airspeed: primary cause — reduces retreating blade velocity
High AUW: more lift required → higher collective → less stall margin
High density altitude: more collective needed in thinner air
High collective: directly increases AoA on retreating blade
Turbulence or abrupt inputs: can momentarily exceed critical AoA
Steep banked turns: load factor increases collective and AoA demand

syllabus_desc: Describe and explain the symptoms of retreating blade stall.
concept: Symptoms of retreating blade stall include: (1) abnormal vibration — the stalled blade produces asymmetric aerodynamic forces causing cyclic vibration through the airframe; (2) pitching up — the loss of lift on the retreating side (typically the left side for CCW rotors) causes the helicopter to pitch up and roll toward the retreating side; (3) roll toward the retreating blade — loss of lift on that side; (4) possible yaw — as the torque balance changes; (5) cyclic control becoming ineffective. Onset can be rapid.
exam_tip: RBS symptoms: airframe vibration, pitch-up, roll toward retreating side. Onset is rapid and control becomes ineffective — immediate action required.
key_facts: Abnormal (cyclic) vibration — stalled blade creating asymmetric forces
Pitch-up as retreating side loses lift
Roll toward the retreating side (loss of lift)
Possible yaw as torque balance changes
Cyclic control may become ineffective — early recognition is critical

syllabus_desc: Explain the relationship between retreating blade stall and Vne.
concept: VNE (never exceed speed) is set in part to prevent retreating blade stall. As forward airspeed increases, the retreating blade velocity decreases and the blade must operate at higher angles of attack to generate sufficient lift. VNE is the speed at which the retreating blade stall margin is exhausted (accounting for turbulence, high AUW, and normal manoeuvring). VNE is lower at high density altitude, high AUW, and high power settings — because the retreating blade is already at a higher AoA in those conditions.
exam_tip: VNE is set to prevent RBS — it's not arbitrary. VNE is lower at high DA, high AUW, and high power — when the blade is already at a higher AoA and stall margin is reduced.
key_facts: VNE set to prevent retreating blade stall
At VNE: retreating blade stall margin is at minimum acceptable level
VNE decreases with high density altitude
VNE decreases with high AUW
High collective (high power setting) also reduces the speed margin to RBS
Never exceed VNE — especially in turbulence

syllabus_desc: Describe the recovery technique for retreating blade stall.
concept: Recovery from retreating blade stall: (1) immediately reduce collective — this directly reduces blade pitch and angle of attack, removing the stall condition; (2) reduce forward airspeed — lower the cyclic to reduce speed if possible; (3) level the aircraft — stabilise the roll; (4) avoid aggressive cyclic inputs — forward cyclic may worsen the stall on the retreating blade. Recovery must be prompt and deliberate. Once the blade un-stalls, normal flight can be resumed cautiously.
exam_tip: Retreating blade stall recovery: LOWER COLLECTIVE immediately (removes the AoA that caused the stall). Then reduce airspeed. Do not make aggressive cyclic inputs.
key_facts: Immediately lower collective — reduces blade AoA and un-stalls the blade
Reduce forward airspeed — lower cyclic
Level the aircraft once stall is broken
Avoid aggressive forward cyclic — can worsen retreating blade condition
Prompt, deliberate action — don't freeze; act immediately

syllabus_desc: Explain the hazards of inappropriate control input during recovery.
concept: Inappropriate control inputs during RBS recovery can worsen the situation. Applying aggressive forward cyclic (attempting to push the nose down) increases the pitch of the retreating blade further — deepening the stall. Applying aft cyclic to arrest the nose-up pitching can increase the load on the stalled side and worsen the roll. Any input that increases collective (to stop descent) worsens the RBS condition. The only correct initial response is to lower collective — overcome the instinct to add collective when the helicopter pitches and rolls unexpectedly.
exam_tip: Wrong inputs in RBS: adding collective, applying aggressive cyclic. These worsen the stall. The only right first move is lower collective.
key_facts: Aggressive forward cyclic: worsens retreating blade AoA — deepens stall
Aft cyclic to arrest pitch-up: increases load on stalled side
Raising collective: deepens the stall — the opposite of recovery
Instinct is wrong — the aircraft pitches and rolls unexpectedly
Overcome instinct: lower collective is the only correct first input

syllabus_desc: Define vortex ring state.
concept: Vortex ring state (VRS), also called settling with power, occurs when the helicopter descends into its own rotor downwash. In this condition, the rotor is simultaneously generating downwash (air being pushed down through the disc) while descending through that same air — the rotor operates within a recirculating vortex that severely degrades its efficiency. Lift fluctuates and collective inputs have little effect on the sink rate. This is a powered-flight phenomenon — the engine is running and power is applied.
exam_tip: VRS: helicopter descends into its own downwash — rotor trapped in a recirculating vortex. Collective loses effectiveness. Power is ON but the helicopter still sinks.
key_facts: VRS: rotor descends into its own rotor downwash
Rotor operates within recirculating vortex — efficiency severely degraded
Engine running and power applied — but lift is erratic and insufficient
Collective inputs have little effect — sinking continues
Also called settling with power

syllabus_desc: Describe the conditions that can lead to vortex ring state.
concept: Conditions leading to VRS: (1) high rate of descent — approximately 300 fpm or more; (2) low airspeed — typically below 30 knots forward airspeed (the helicopter isn't moving fast enough for clean inflow); (3) high power applied — the rotor is generating significant downwash; (4) combination of all three — this is the classic VRS entry condition. Steep powered approaches at slow speed are the most common scenario. Hovering OGE in a descending spot is another.
exam_tip: VRS conditions: rate of descent >300 fpm + low forward airspeed (<30 knots) + power applied. All three together. Steep slow powered approaches are the danger zone.
key_facts: High rate of descent: typically >300 fpm
Low forward airspeed: <30 knots (rotor in its own downwash)
Power applied: significant downwash being generated
All three conditions together = VRS entry
Steep powered approaches at low speed — most common entry scenario

syllabus_desc: With respect to vortex ring state, describe: the development.
concept: VRS development: as the helicopter descends into its own downwash, the inflow through the rotor disc becomes disrupted. The rotor, instead of drawing in clean air from above, starts circulating air in a toroidal (donut-shaped) vortex at the disc edge. This recirculating flow reduces the effective thrust of the rotor. As the rotor loses efficiency, the pilot applies more collective — but this increases the downwash, making the vortex worse. A vicious cycle develops where more power deepens the VRS rather than arresting it.
exam_tip: VRS develops gradually: recirculating vortex at disc edge reduces thrust → pilot adds collective → more downwash → worse vortex → more sink. Adding power worsens it.
key_facts: VRS begins as rotor disc enters its own downwash vortex
Toroidal recirculating vortex forms at disc edge
Rotor efficiency drops — less lift for same collective
Pilot adds collective → more downwash → worse vortex
Vicious cycle: more power = worse VRS

syllabus_desc: With respect to vortex ring state, describe: the symptoms.
concept: Symptoms of VRS: (1) high and increasing sink rate — despite high collective; (2) collective becoming ineffective — pulling up collective doesn't arrest the sink; (3) abnormal vibration — the disrupted airflow causes irregular aerodynamic loads; (4) reduced cyclic effectiveness; (5) a characteristic 'mushing' or 'settling' sensation. The helicopter may appear to be flying normally for a moment, then sink rapidly. At altitude, there may be time to recover; at low level, it can be fatal.
exam_tip: VRS symptoms: high sink rate + collective ineffective + vibration + mushing. All while power is applied. Any unexplained heavy sink in powered descent at low speed = suspect VRS.
key_facts: High sink rate despite high collective setting
Collective input becomes ineffective at arresting descent
Abnormal vibration from disrupted airflow
Mushing or settling sensation
Reduced cyclic and pedal effectiveness

syllabus_desc: With respect to vortex ring state, describe: the methods of recovery.
concept: VRS recovery methods: (1) accelerate forward — apply forward cyclic to gain airspeed; moving forward draws clean air into the rotor from the front, breaking the recirculating vortex; (2) reduce power — lowering collective reduces downwash, which can help break the vortex (but this is counterintuitive and only suitable at sufficient altitude); (3) autorotate — in extreme cases, entering autorotation breaks the power-on vortex condition. The primary recovery is forward acceleration to escape the downwash. Sufficient altitude is required.
exam_tip: VRS recovery: accelerate forward to break the vortex (primary method). Can also reduce collective (at altitude). Don't add collective — it worsens VRS.
key_facts: Primary recovery: forward cyclic to accelerate (breaks vortex by drawing clean air)
Alternative: reduce collective (only with sufficient altitude)
Emergency: enter autorotation (no power = no downwash)
Avoid: raising collective — worsens vortex
Recovery requires altitude — at low level, there may be no time

syllabus_desc: State the effect of power, weight and density altitude on vortex ring state.
concept: Power increases VRS risk because more power creates more downwash — if the helicopter is already descending into its downwash, more power increases the intensity of the vortex. Higher weight requires more power to maintain any given descent rate, increasing the downwash. High density altitude also demands more collective for the same lift, increasing downwash and VRS risk. The combination of high weight, high DA, and any powered descent at low airspeed is the most dangerous scenario.
exam_tip: Higher power, weight, and DA all increase VRS risk — they all require more collective, which creates more downwash to descend into. High DA + high AUW + slow descent = maximum VRS risk.
key_facts: More power: more downwash → increases VRS intensity
Higher weight: requires more power → more downwash
High density altitude: requires more collective → more downwash
Combination of high AUW + high DA + slow powered descent = very high VRS risk
Power cannot arrest VRS — it worsens it

syllabus_desc: Explain tail rotor vortex ring state.
concept: Tail rotor vortex ring state (TRVRS) occurs when the tail rotor descends into its own wake, similar to main rotor VRS. It typically occurs in a right sideward flight (for helicopters with a CCW main rotor) or slow right pedal turn, when the tail rotor is moving into its own downwash. The result is loss of tail rotor thrust and yaw control, potentially causing an uncontrolled yaw. It can also develop in left sideward flight in some configurations.
exam_tip: Tail rotor VRS: tail rotor descends into its own wake, losing yaw control. Occurs in specific sideward flight conditions — different from main rotor VRS.
key_facts: TRVRS: tail rotor enters its own downwash
Results in loss of tail rotor thrust and yaw control
Typically occurs in right sideward flight or right pedal turns (CCW main rotor)
Can cause uncommanded yaw
Different from main rotor VRS — affects directional control not lift

syllabus_desc: List the conditions that can lead to tail rotor vortex ring state.
concept: Conditions leading to tail rotor VRS: (1) sideward flight at slow speed with the tail rotor moving into its own downwash; (2) right sideward flight in strong left crosswinds (for CCW rotor helicopters — tail rotor into the wind); (3) high power demand (more tail rotor downwash); (4) tail rotor turbulence from the main rotor — in some flight configurations, the main rotor wake can disturb the tail rotor inflow and cause TRVRS-like conditions. The pilot experiences loss of pedal authority suddenly.
exam_tip: TRVRS conditions: slow sideward flight (tail rotor into its own downwash) + high power + specific wind conditions. Results in sudden loss of pedal authority.
key_facts: Slow right sideward flight (CCW rotor): tail rotor descends into own wake
Left crosswind conditions: tail rotor working harder into the wind
High power demand: more tail rotor downwash to enter vortex
Main rotor wake turbulence can also disturb tail rotor inflow
Sudden loss of right pedal authority is the key symptom

syllabus_desc: State the indications that differentiate between vortex ring state and a rotor stall.
concept: Differentiating VRS from rotor stall: VRS involves a powered rotor that has lost efficiency due to vortex recirculation — the rotor is still turning and spinning normally, collective loses effectiveness. Rotor stall (low RRPM condition) involves the rotor slowing down below safe limits — the blades lose AoA and centrifugal force, reducing thrust. The key differentiator is RRPM: in VRS, RRPM is normal but collective is ineffective; in a rotor stall, RRPM is low (below green arc) and the rotor itself is the problem.
exam_tip: VRS vs rotor stall: both involve loss of rotor effectiveness, but VRS has NORMAL RRPM while rotor stall has LOW RRPM. Check the RPM gauge.
key_facts: VRS: RRPM normal (green arc), collective ineffective — rotor has clean aerodynamic stall by vortex
Rotor stall: RRPM below green arc — rotor slowing, blades losing centrifugal force
RRPM gauge is the differentiating instrument
VRS: engine running, power applied, RPM OK
Rotor stall: may occur after engine failure or excessive collective application

syllabus_desc: Describe ground resonance.
concept: Ground resonance is a mechanical vibration phenomenon that can occur when a helicopter with an articulated rotor is in contact with the ground. It happens when the rotor blades become unevenly spaced (phase lag resonance) in their lead-lag plane, creating an imbalance in the rotor system. This imbalance sets up a self-exciting oscillation through the landing gear and fuselage at a frequency that can match the natural resonant frequency of the undercarriage, leading to rapid and potentially destructive vibration.
exam_tip: Ground resonance: blade lead-lag imbalance + landing gear resonant frequency = self-exciting oscillation. It can destroy the helicopter in seconds — act immediately.
key_facts: Ground resonance: blades lose equal spacing in lead-lag plane
Creates rotating imbalance in rotor system
Oscillation couples with landing gear natural frequency
Self-reinforcing — vibration increases rapidly without correction
Can be catastrophically destructive in seconds

syllabus_desc: Describe the conditions likely to cause ground resonance.
concept: Conditions likely to cause ground resonance include: (1) uneven or soft ground that allows differential gear compression; (2) partially flat tyre or oleos at unequal pressure — promotes asymmetric gear response; (3) incorrect landing technique — firm or off-centre touchdowns; (4) low rotor RPM during power-on contact with the ground — at certain RPM, blade lag frequency can match gear resonant frequency; (5) operation with worn or out-of-rig dampers (lead-lag dampers maintain blade spacing).
exam_tip: Ground resonance triggers: uneven ground, flat tyre, hard landing, low RRPM, and worn lag dampers. The common thread is blade spacing becoming disturbed while on the ground.
key_facts: Uneven or soft ground causing differential undercarriage compression
Flat tyre or unequal oleo pressure
Hard, off-centre, or sloped landing technique
Low RRPM — lag frequency may match gear resonant frequency
Worn or inoperative blade lag dampers — fail to maintain blade spacing

syllabus_desc: Describe the symptoms of ground resonance.
concept: Symptoms of ground resonance: (1) progressively increasing rocking or oscillation of the fuselage — starts mildly and escalates rapidly; (2) audible 'thudding' rhythm as the imbalanced rotor hits resonant frequency; (3) the oscillation frequency matches the wobbling of the airframe on the gear; (4) rapid escalation once resonance is established — from mild rocking to violent shaking in seconds. The pilot will feel an unmistakeable rhythmic thrashing motion.
exam_tip: Ground resonance starts as mild rhythmic rocking and escalates to violent shaking very quickly. Don't wait — act immediately when you feel abnormal oscillation on the ground.
key_facts: Initial symptom: mild rhythmic rocking of the airframe
Progressive escalation — does not dampen itself
Audible thudding rhythm
Violent shaking rapidly follows if uncorrected
Can destroy the helicopter in seconds once fully established

syllabus_desc: Describe the recovery technique for ground resonance.
concept: Recovery from ground resonance: the correct response depends on how severe the oscillation is. (1) If at sufficient rotor RPM to fly: immediately apply full collective and take off — getting airborne removes the ground contact and ends the resonance. (2) If at insufficient RPM to fly: immediately lower collective to flat pitch, close the throttle, and use rotor brake to stop the rotor as quickly as possible. Do NOT attempt a partial measure — decide and act immediately. Hesitation allows the oscillation to become destructive.
exam_tip: Ground resonance recovery: EITHER take off immediately (if power/RRPM allows) OR lower collective, close throttle, stop the rotor (if not). Choose and act instantly — no half-measures.
key_facts: Can fly: full collective, apply power, take off immediately
Cannot fly: lower collective to flat pitch, close throttle, apply rotor brake
Do NOT delay — resonance is self-reinforcing and escalates rapidly
Do NOT attempt to hover-taxi or reposition — airborne or shut down
After shutdown, inspect dampers, blades, and gear before further flight

syllabus_desc: Describe blade sailing.
concept: Blade sailing occurs when the rotor blades droop or flex downward excessively during startup or shutdown — when rotor RPM is low and aerodynamic stiffness is minimal. At low RPM, centrifugal force (which normally holds the blades out flat) is insufficient to keep the blades rigid. Wind gusts can cause individual blades to rise and fall unpredictably — a condition called sailing. Blade sailing creates a risk of blade strike on the tail boom or fuselage.
exam_tip: Blade sailing: low RRPM + wind = blades droop, flex, and can strike the fuselage or tail boom. Most dangerous during startup and shutdown.
key_facts: Blade sailing: excessive blade droop and flexing at low RRPM
Caused by insufficient centrifugal stiffening at low RPM
Wind gusts cause individual blades to rise and fall unpredictably
Risk of blade strike on tail boom or fuselage
Most dangerous during startup and shutdown phases

syllabus_desc: Describe the conditions likely to lead to blade sailing.
concept: Conditions leading to blade sailing: (1) low rotor RPM — the primary condition (centrifugal force is insufficient to maintain blade rigidity); (2) strong or gusty wind — especially crosswinds or downwash from aircraft; (3) flexible blades — semi-rigid or composite blades are more susceptible than fully rigid designs; (4) long blade span — longer blades are more susceptible to droop; (5) parking on slopes or in turbulent rotor wash from other aircraft. Blade sailing is most likely during engine start and shutdown.
exam_tip: Blade sailing conditions: low RRPM + wind gusts. Startup and shutdown in high or gusty winds — the most common scenario.
key_facts: Low RRPM: primary condition (insufficient centrifugal stiffening)
Strong or gusty wind: lifts and drops individual blades irregularly
Flexible blade design: semi-rigid blades more susceptible
Long rotor span: more susceptible to droop
Operating on slopes or near rotor wash from other aircraft

syllabus_desc: Describe the recovery technique for blade sailing.
concept: Recovery and avoidance of blade sailing: (1) if sailing is observed during startup, increase RPM quickly to reach the speed where centrifugal force stiffens the blades — don't linger at low RPM; (2) during shutdown, perform a rapid but controlled shutdown to minimise time at low RPM; (3) head the aircraft into the wind during start and shutdown — reduces the asymmetric wind loading on individual blades; (4) use rotor brake to reduce shutdown time. In strong winds, consider delay or relocation to a sheltered position.
exam_tip: Blade sailing prevention/recovery: accelerate through low RPM quickly on startup; shut down rapidly; head into wind; use rotor brake on shutdown in windy conditions.
key_facts: Startup: accelerate quickly through low RPM to normal idle
Shutdown: reduce RPM quickly to minimise time in sailing-prone zone
Head aircraft into wind during start and shutdown
Use rotor brake to shorten shutdown time in windy conditions
In high winds: delay start/shutdown or reposition to sheltered area

syllabus_desc: Describe dynamic rollover.
concept: Dynamic rollover occurs when a helicopter pivots about one skid (or wheel) while partially in contact with the ground, and the roll moment exceeds the pilot's ability to correct with lateral cyclic. The rotor thrust vector tips with the fuselage, and the rolling moment becomes self-reinforcing — each additional degree of roll increases the rolling moment. Beyond the critical rollover angle, recovery is impossible and the helicopter will roll onto its side.
exam_tip: Dynamic rollover: helicopter pivots about one skid — roll moment becomes self-reinforcing beyond the critical angle. Past that point, recovery is impossible.
key_facts: Dynamic rollover: pivot about one skid with increasing roll moment
Rotor thrust tips with fuselage — rolling moment becomes self-reinforcing
Critical angle: beyond this, recovery is impossible
Self-reinforcing: each degree of roll increases the rolling moment
Can occur very quickly — especially on slopes or during taxiing

syllabus_desc: Describe the conditions likely to lead to dynamic rollover.
concept: Conditions likely to lead to dynamic rollover: (1) one skid or wheel caught on the ground while attempting to lift off — the stuck skid becomes the pivot point; (2) lateral drift during takeoff or landing; (3) operations on slopes — particularly lateral slopes; (4) tail wheel lock in-flight, then landing with skid low side; (5) slung loads caught on an obstacle; (6) excessive lateral cyclic application near the ground; (7) rotor downwash on slope creating asymmetric loads; (8) hovering in crosswind over sloped terrain.
exam_tip: Dynamic rollover triggers: caught skid, lateral drift, sloped ground, stuck tail wheel, caught slingload. Any condition that puts you on one skid with a roll moment.
key_facts: One skid or wheel caught during liftoff — creates a pivot point
Lateral drift during takeoff or landing
Slope operations — especially lateral slopes
Locked tail wheel during landing (wheel helicopters)
Slung load caught on obstacle during liftoff
Excessive lateral cyclic near the ground

syllabus_desc: Explain the factors influencing the critical angle at which dynamic rollover will occur.
concept: Factors influencing the critical rollover angle include: (1) main rotor thrust — higher thrust increases the rolling moment for a given tilt; (2) centre of gravity — a high or lateral CG reduces the critical angle (makes rollover easier to achieve); (3) wind — a crosswind adds a horizontal moment, reducing the effective critical angle on one side; (4) slope angle — steeper slopes reduce the critical angle toward the low side; (5) landing gear width — narrower gear reduces stability; (6) RRPM — higher RRPM and thrust increases rollover tendency.
exam_tip: Critical rollover angle is reduced by: high thrust, lateral CG, crosswind, steep slope, narrow gear. All these shift the tipping point closer to the current attitude.
key_facts: High rotor thrust: increases rolling moment for given tilt
High or lateral CG: lowers the critical angle
Crosswind: adds lateral force — reduces critical angle on downwind side
Steep slope: reduces critical angle toward the low side
Narrow landing gear width: less stability
High RRPM/thrust: increases the available rolling moment

syllabus_desc: Describe the recovery technique for dynamic rollover.
concept: Recovery from dynamic rollover: (1) immediately reduce collective to minimum — this removes rotor thrust and the associated rolling moment; (2) simultaneously apply lateral cyclic away from the roll direction; (3) the pilot MUST lower collective before the critical angle is reached — after that, control inputs cannot stop the rollover. If the helicopter starts to roll uncommandedly, the first and most important action is always to lower collective. This is counterintuitive but essential.
exam_tip: Dynamic rollover recovery: LOWER COLLECTIVE immediately — removes rotor thrust and rolling moment. Then cyclic. Collective must be the first action, before the critical angle is reached.
key_facts: Immediately lower collective to minimum — removes rolling moment
Apply lateral cyclic away from the roll direction
Both inputs must happen before the critical angle
After critical angle: recovery is impossible regardless of input
Lowering collective is counterintuitive but is the only correct first action

syllabus_desc: Explain the factors that limit the available cyclic in the air and on the ground.
concept: Cyclic control authority in the air is limited by the geometry of the rotor system, flapping hinge offset, and the angle of attack of the rotor disc. At high forward speeds, the advancing blade flaps up and the retreating blade flaps down — this limits the available aft cyclic travel before retreating blade stall. On the ground, cyclic limits are set by the risk of blade strike (low RRPM + full cyclic can allow blades to droop into the fuselage or tail boom), and by the risk of inducing ground resonance through excessive lateral inputs.
exam_tip: Cyclic limits: in the air, retreating blade stall and rotor geometry set the boundaries. On the ground, blade strike and ground resonance risk limit cyclic authority.
key_facts: In the air: retreating blade stall limits high-speed aft cyclic
In the air: rotor geometry (flapping hinge geometry) limits total cyclic travel
On the ground: full cyclic at low RRPM risks blade strike on fuselage
On the ground: large lateral cyclic inputs can contribute to dynamic rollover
Low G pushover can lead to mast bumping in teetering rotor helicopters

syllabus_desc: Describe mast bumping.
concept: Mast bumping is a condition unique to two-bladed teetering rotor systems (like the Robinson R22/R44). It occurs when the rotor disc tilts so far that the rotor hub contacts (bumps) the main rotor mast — the central shaft. This contact can cause catastrophic structural failure, blade separation, or mast failure. Mast bumping typically results from low-G flight (pushovers, abrupt nose-down inputs) or excessive cyclic deflection during manoeuvres that reduce teetering disc load.
exam_tip: Mast bumping: teetering rotor disc tilts so far it contacts the mast — catastrophic structural failure possible. Low-G manoeuvres are the primary cause.
key_facts: Mast bumping: rotor hub contacts the main rotor mast
Unique to two-bladed teetering rotor systems (R22, R44, etc.)
Can cause catastrophic structural failure or blade separation
Primary cause: low-G flight — rotor unloads and tilts excessively
Result: rotor separation or mast failure

syllabus_desc: Describe the conditions likely to lead to mast bumping.
concept: Conditions likely to lead to mast bumping: (1) low-G flight — pushovers, bunts, rapid nose-down inputs, or turbulence-induced G reduction; (2) abrupt lateral cyclic inputs in low-G conditions — can tilt the disc unexpectedly; (3) high density altitude — less rotor efficiency may cause low G inadvertently; (4) operation in turbulence at high speed — sudden G reduction possible; (5) inappropriate manoeuvring at low level. Low-G is the key — when rotor thrust is reduced, the gyroscopic stiffness that keeps the disc stable is also reduced.
exam_tip: Mast bumping is caused by low-G (unloaded rotor) — pushovers, turbulence, or abrupt cyclic. The rotor unstiffens and can tilt into the mast.
key_facts: Low-G flight: pushover, bunt, abrupt nose-down cyclic
Turbulence at high speed: sudden G reduction
Abrupt lateral cyclic in low-G: disc tilts further than normal
High DA: reduced rotor efficiency may contribute to low G development
Any manoeuvre that rapidly unloads the rotor

syllabus_desc: Describe the forces involved during mast bumping.
concept: Forces during mast bumping: during low-G flight, rotor thrust reduces. The disc can tilt more easily than normal because the gyroscopic precession that normally resists tipping is reduced. When the disc tilts far enough, the rotor hub strikes the mast. The impact force is extremely high — the rotor system's mass and rotational inertia combined with the metal-on-metal contact creates an impact that can exceed the structural limit of the mast in a single contact. The teetering hinge stop is the last design protection before mast contact.
exam_tip: Mast bumping forces: low-G unloads the gyroscopic stiffness → disc tilts excessively → hub-to-mast impact force can immediately exceed structural limit.
key_facts: Low-G reduces gyroscopic resistance to disc tilt
Disc tilts further than normal limit
Hub contacts mast with extreme impact force
Single contact can exceed structural limit of the mast
Teetering hinge stop is the last mechanical protection

syllabus_desc: Describe the means of avoiding mast bumping.
concept: Avoiding mast bumping: (1) never allow G to go below +0.5G in a teetering rotor helicopter — maintain positive G at all times; (2) avoid abrupt nose-down cyclic inputs, especially at high airspeed; (3) avoid turbulence at high speed where G reduction may be beyond pilot control; (4) if in turbulence, reduce airspeed and maintain positive loading; (5) understand the flight envelope and H/V considerations — do not allow low-G conditions near the ground. Training in the Robinson series specifically addresses this hazard.
exam_tip: Avoid mast bumping: NEVER allow G below +0.5G in a teetering rotor helicopter. No abrupt nose-down inputs. Reduce speed in turbulence.
key_facts: Never allow G below +0.5G in teetering rotor helicopters
No abrupt nose-down cyclic inputs especially at speed
Reduce airspeed in turbulence to minimise G excursion risk
Understand and respect the flight envelope
Robinson Safety Courses emphasise low-G as the primary fatal accident cause

syllabus_desc: Describe the recovery technique for mast bumping.
concept: Recovery from developing mast bumping: if low-G is felt (weightless sensation, items floating in the cockpit), the ONLY correct response is to immediately apply aft cyclic to reload the rotor — this increases G and restores disc stability. Do NOT apply lateral cyclic — this can cause the disc to tilt into the mast rather than away. The recovery must be immediate because mast contact may occur within a fraction of a second of disc tilt.
exam_tip: Recovery from mast bump onset: immediately apply RAFT CYCLIC to reload the rotor (increase G). Do NOT apply lateral cyclic — that can tilt the disc into the mast.
key_facts: Feel low-G or weightlessness: immediately apply aft cyclic
Aft cyclic increases G and reloads the rotor — restores disc stability
Do NOT apply lateral cyclic — can tilt disc into mast
Recovery must be immediate — time to contact is fractions of a second
If mast has already bumped: land immediately, do not continue flight

syllabus_desc: Explain the reasons for high RPM limits.
concept: High RPM limits exist to prevent: (1) blade tip overspeed — at very high RPM, blade tip speeds may approach compressibility (transonic) conditions, producing noise, vibration, and loss of aerodynamic efficiency; (2) structural exceedance — rotor blades, hub, and mast experience centrifugal stress proportional to RPM squared; (3) engine overspeed — the engine itself has an RPM limit beyond which internal components may fail; (4) resonance frequencies — some structures have natural frequencies that coincide with high RPM harmonics.
exam_tip: High RPM limits: prevent blade tip compressibility, structural overstress (centrifugal load ∝ RPM²), engine overspeed, and resonance exceedance.
key_facts: Blade tip speed approaches compressibility (transonic) at high RPM
Structural loads: centrifugal stress ∝ RPM² — exponentially increasing
Engine components have RPM limits — failure risk at overspeed
Resonance frequencies: some RPM values excite structural resonances
Exceeding high RPM limit requires mandatory maintenance inspection

syllabus_desc: Explain the reasons for low RPM limits.
concept: Low RPM limits exist to prevent: (1) loss of centrifugal blade stiffening — at low RPM, blades can flex or droop (blade sailing risk on the ground, blade strike in flight); (2) reduced rotor efficiency — less centrifugal stiffening reduces blade twist and effective AoA range; (3) loss of gyroscopic inertia — at very low RPM, the rotor's ability to resist disc tilt is severely reduced (increases mast bumping risk in teetering rotors); (4) control ineffectiveness — tail rotor and cyclic authority reduce with RRPM; (5) autorotative risk — low RRPM means insufficient energy for the landing flare.
exam_tip: Low RPM limits: prevent blade flex/droop, loss of rotor efficiency, loss of gyroscopic inertia (mast bump risk), control loss, and inadequate autorotation energy.
key_facts: Insufficient centrifugal stiffening → blade flex, droop, possible blade strike
Reduced rotor efficiency at low RPM
Reduced gyroscopic inertia → mast bumping risk (teetering rotors)
Reduced control authority — tail rotor and cyclic less effective
Insufficient energy in rotor for safe autorotative flare

syllabus_desc: Describe rotor stalling.
concept: Rotor stalling occurs when rotor RPM decays to the point where the rotor blades can no longer generate sufficient lift — either because blade speed is too low (centrifugal force and aerodynamic efficiency both decrease) or because blade pitch is too high relative to the available RPM (overpitch condition). Unlike a fixed-wing stall, a rotor stall is a speed-dependent event — the blades themselves are moving too slowly to produce lift at the required pitch angles. This can occur following engine failure if collective is not reduced promptly.
exam_tip: Rotor stall: RRPM too low to generate lift (blades moving too slowly for required pitch). Different from VRS — the rotor itself is the problem here, not just the inflow.
key_facts: Rotor stall: RRPM too low for blade pitch to generate sufficient lift
Caused by excessive collective with insufficient RRPM
Or: engine failure with delayed collective reduction
Blades too slow → insufficient centrifugal force and aerodynamic efficiency
Progressive stall as RPM droops below minimum

syllabus_desc: Describe the conditions likely to lead to a rotor stall.
concept: Conditions likely to lead to rotor stall: (1) engine failure with delayed reduction of collective — the engine no longer drives the rotor and collective drag slows the rotor rapidly; (2) excessive collective at low RRPM — pulling too much pitch before RRPM has recovered (e.g. during a normal power reduction); (3) overpitching — applying excessive collective in demanding conditions (high AUW, high DA) until RRPM droops; (4) low rotor RPM from other causes (governor failure, accidental throttle reduction).
exam_tip: Rotor stall causes: engine failure + delayed collective reduction (most critical); excessive collective at low RPM; overpitching in demanding conditions; governor/throttle failure.
key_facts: Engine failure: delayed collective reduction → RRPM decays → rotor stalls
Excessive collective during power reduction
Overpitching at high AUW or high DA
Governor malfunction causing RRPM to droop
Throttle accidentally closed during flight

syllabus_desc: Describe the symptoms of a rotor stall.
concept: Symptoms of a rotor stall: (1) low or decreasing RRPM — RRPM gauge below green arc and falling; (2) loss of collective effectiveness — the helicopter sinks despite collective being raised; (3) possible vibration — as different sections of the blade stall; (4) the nose may pitch — as cyclic control authority reduces with RRPM; (5) high sink rate with no rotor noise from normal blade passage. The RRPM gauge is the primary indicator — low and decreasing RRPM confirms this is a rotor stall, not VRS.
exam_tip: Rotor stall symptoms: RRPM LOW and FALLING (gauge below green arc). Collective ineffective, high sink rate. RRPM gauge is the key differentiator from VRS.
key_facts: RRPM below green arc and decreasing — primary indicator
Loss of collective effectiveness
High sink rate despite collective application
Reduced or absent rotor noise from normal blade passage
Reduced control authority on all axes

syllabus_desc: Describe the recovery technique for a rotor stall.
concept: Recovery from a rotor stall: (1) immediately lower collective to flat (minimum) pitch — this removes the drag on the rotor and allows RRPM to recover; (2) the autorotative airflow through the descending rotor will help restore RRPM if the aircraft is descending; (3) once RRPM recovers to within the green arc, collective can be carefully raised; (4) avoid raising collective again before RRPM is recovered — it will re-stall the rotor. If the engine is still running, ensure it is delivering power. Recovery from near ground is extremely difficult.
exam_tip: Rotor stall recovery: LOWER COLLECTIVE immediately to allow RRPM to recover. Only raise collective once RRPM is back in the green arc.
key_facts: Immediately lower collective to flat pitch
Reduced blade drag allows RRPM to recover
Descending aircraft: autorotative airflow helps restore RRPM
Wait for RRPM green arc recovery before raising collective
Do not re-raise collective prematurely — will re-stall the rotor

syllabus_desc: State the indications that differentiate between a rotor stall and vortex ring state.
concept: Differentiating rotor stall from VRS: the key is RRPM. In a rotor stall, RRPM is low or falling — below the green arc. The rotor itself is the problem. In VRS, RRPM is normal (in the green arc) but collective is ineffective due to recirculating vortex disrupting inflow. In VRS, the rotor is spinning normally but not generating effective thrust because of the disturbed inflow. Check the RRPM gauge — normal RPM = suspect VRS; low RPM = rotor stall.
exam_tip: Rotor stall vs VRS: check the RRPM gauge. LOW RRPM = rotor stall. NORMAL RRPM but collective ineffective = VRS.
key_facts: Rotor stall: RRPM below green arc
VRS: RRPM normal (in green arc)
Rotor stall: rotor itself is too slow to generate lift
VRS: rotor spinning normally but inflow disrupted by vortex
RRPM gauge is the definitive differentiating instrument

syllabus_desc: Define stability.
concept: Stability is the tendency of an aircraft to return to its original equilibrium flight condition after being disturbed by a force (e.g., turbulence or a control input). A stable helicopter will tend to return to trimmed flight. An unstable helicopter will diverge further from equilibrium if disturbed. Stability in helicopters is more complex than in fixed-wing aircraft because the rotor provides both control and stability — and because helicopters are inherently less stable aerodynamically than most fixed-wing designs.
exam_tip: Stability: tendency to return to equilibrium after a disturbance. Helicopters are generally less inherently stable than fixed-wing — many are mildly unstable longitudinally.
key_facts: Stability: natural tendency to return to trimmed condition after disturbance
Positive stability: returns to equilibrium (desirable)
Neutral stability: stays at new disturbed state
Negative stability: diverges from equilibrium (dangerous)
Helicopters generally less stable than fixed-wing — may require active pilot input

syllabus_desc: Describe what is meant by: static stability.
concept: Static stability is the initial tendency of the aircraft immediately after being disturbed — before any oscillations develop. Positive static stability means the initial response is toward equilibrium (e.g., a nose-up disturbance causes a nose-down pitching moment). Negative static stability means the initial tendency is to diverge further from equilibrium. Neutral static stability means no initial restoring or diverging moment. Static stability is necessary (but not sufficient) for good aircraft handling.
exam_tip: Static stability = the INITIAL response to a disturbance. Positive = initial tendency back toward equilibrium. Negative = initial tendency to diverge.
key_facts: Static stability: initial response immediately after a disturbance
Positive static: initial tendency toward equilibrium (restoring force)
Negative static: initial tendency away from equilibrium (diverging)
Neutral static: no initial response either way
Static stability is a necessary but not sufficient condition for overall stability

syllabus_desc: Describe what is meant by: dynamic stability.
concept: Dynamic stability describes the long-term behaviour of the aircraft after a disturbance — whether the oscillations that follow the initial displacement die out, remain constant, or grow over time. An aircraft can be statically stable (initial restoring tendency) but dynamically unstable (oscillations grow over time). Positive dynamic stability means oscillations dampen and die out. Negative dynamic stability means oscillations grow — the aircraft never returns to equilibrium.
exam_tip: Dynamic stability = long-term behaviour after disturbance. Oscillations dampen (positive), stay constant (neutral), or grow (negative). An aircraft can be statically stable but dynamically unstable.
key_facts: Dynamic stability: long-term behaviour following a disturbance
Positive dynamic: oscillations dampen and die out over time
Neutral dynamic: oscillations continue at constant amplitude
Negative dynamic: oscillations grow over time
Statically stable + dynamically unstable = oscillations grow despite initial return

syllabus_desc: Describe what is meant by: neutral stability.
concept: Neutral stability describes a condition where the aircraft, after being displaced, remains in the new attitude without either returning to or diverging further from equilibrium. In static neutral stability, there is no restoring or diverging moment — the aircraft just stays at the disturbed attitude.
