In dynamic neutral stability, oscillations following a displacement continue indefinitely at a constant amplitude rather than dampening.
exam_tip: Neutral stability: no restoring or diverging tendency — aircraft stays where it is put. Neither positive nor negative.
key_facts: Neutral static stability: no restoring moment after displacement
Aircraft remains at the disturbed attitude
Neutral dynamic: oscillations continue at constant amplitude
Not particularly desirable — offers no assistance to the pilot
Some helicopters have neutral longitudinal stability — require continuous attention

syllabus_desc: Explain the relationship between: static stability and dynamic stability.
concept: Relationship between static and dynamic stability: positive static stability is a prerequisite for positive dynamic stability, but it does not guarantee it. An aircraft that is statically stable will have an initial tendency to return to equilibrium — but depending on how the oscillation is damped, it may still be dynamically unstable (oscillations grow) or dynamically neutral. Positive static stability combined with sufficient damping produces positive dynamic stability.
exam_tip: Static stability is necessary but not sufficient for dynamic stability. Positive static + adequate damping = positive dynamic. Positive static alone doesn't guarantee stability over time.
key_facts: Positive static stability: initial restoring tendency
Positive static does not guarantee positive dynamic stability
Dynamic stability also requires adequate damping of oscillations
Positive static + adequate damping = positive dynamic (oscillations die out)
Most helicopters: positive static but may be weakly positive or neutral dynamic

syllabus_desc: Explain the relationship between: static stability and dynamic instability.
concept: An aircraft can be statically stable but dynamically unstable. This means: after a disturbance, the initial tendency is toward equilibrium (positive static), but the restoring force overshoots and the oscillation that develops grows progressively in amplitude (negative dynamic). The helicopter returns past equilibrium, overshoots in the other direction, comes back further, and so on. The pilot must intervene to damp the oscillation — without input, it will diverge. This is a characteristic of some helicopter phugoid oscillations.
exam_tip: Statically stable but dynamically unstable: initial tendency is correct but oscillations grow. The pilot must actively damp the oscillation — it doesn't self-correct.
key_facts: Positive static: initial tendency toward equilibrium
Negative dynamic: oscillation grows with each cycle
Each overshoot exceeds the last — divergent oscillation
Pilot must actively damp or the aircraft will diverge
Phugoid mode in some helicopters is dynamically unstable

syllabus_desc: Define convergent and divergent phugoid (oscillation).
concept: A phugoid is a long-period oscillation in pitch and airspeed, where the aircraft trades altitude for speed in a gentle, slow oscillation. A convergent phugoid is one where the oscillation damped out over time — the aircraft naturally returns to trimmed airspeed and altitude (positive dynamic stability). A divergent phugoid grows with each cycle — the airspeed and altitude excursions get larger — the aircraft will eventually exceed limits if not corrected. Many helicopters exhibit a mildly divergent phugoid that requires periodic control input.
exam_tip: Phugoid: long-period pitch/speed oscillation. Convergent = damps out (stable). Divergent = grows with time (dynamically unstable). Many helicopters have a divergent phugoid.
key_facts: Phugoid: long-period pitch and airspeed oscillation (typically 30–90 seconds)
Convergent phugoid: oscillation damps out — positive dynamic stability
Divergent phugoid: oscillation grows — negative dynamic stability
Most helicopters have a weakly divergent phugoid in forward flight
Pilot must apply small inputs to damp a divergent phugoid

syllabus_desc: Describe the meaning of: stick-held.
concept: Stick-held stability refers to the stability characteristics of the helicopter when the pilot holds the cyclic fixed (does not make any control corrections). It represents the aircraft's inherent aerodynamic stability with controls locked. Most stability analysis and certification testing is done stick-held, as it isolates the aerodynamic stability from pilot input effects. A helicopter that is stick-held stable will naturally return to equilibrium without any pilot input.
exam_tip: Stick-held stability: stability with controls held fixed — no pilot corrections. This is the aircraft's inherent aerodynamic stability.
key_facts: Stick-held: cyclic (and other controls) held fixed during disturbance
Represents inherent aerodynamic stability
Certification testing often performed stick-held
Positive stick-held stability: returns to trim without pilot input
Negative stick-held: diverges even without pilot input

syllabus_desc: Describe the meaning of: stick-free.
concept: Stick-free stability refers to stability when the controls are released — free to move as the aerodynamic forces dictate. In mechanical control systems, stick-free stability may differ from stick-held because control surfaces (or in a helicopter, the cyclic) can float to positions determined by aerodynamic balance. Helicopters with power-assisted or fully powered controls effectively always operate stick-held (the cyclic position is set by hydraulics, not aerodynamic balance), so stick-free characteristics are less relevant in these aircraft.
exam_tip: Stick-free stability: controls released, free to float to aerodynamically balanced position. Differs from stick-held in mechanical systems; less relevant in hydraulic systems.
key_facts: Stick-free: controls released, free to float aerodynamically
In mechanical systems: control position may differ from stick-held
Stick-free stability often less favourable than stick-held
In powered hydraulic systems: stick-free same as stick-held (no aerodynamic float)
Most modern helicopters: full or partial hydraulic assist — stick-free less relevant

syllabus_desc: Explain: longitudinal stability in the hover and in forward flight.
concept: Longitudinal stability (in the hover): in a hover, most helicopters are longitudinally unstable — a pitch disturbance creates a velocity that, without correction, leads to further divergence. The rotor tends to tilt with the fuselage, amplifying any pitch change. In forward flight, the situation improves — the horizontal tail surface (if fitted) and the rotor flapping response to airspeed provide some longitudinal damping. Most helicopters are mildly stable or neutral longitudinally in cruise flight, but require continuous pilot attention.
exam_tip: Longitudinal stability: poor in hover (unstable — demands continuous control), better in forward flight (some aerodynamic damping from tail and flapping response).
key_facts: Hover: longitudinally unstable — pitch disturbance diverges without correction
Forward flight: improved stability from tail surfaces and rotor flapping
Most helicopters: neutral to mildly unstable longitudinally at cruise
Pilot must continuously correct longitudinal displacement
Stability augmentation systems improve longitudinal stability significantly

syllabus_desc: Explain: lateral stability in forward flight.
concept: Lateral stability in forward flight: a banking disturbance in forward flight causes a sideslip, which generates aerodynamic forces that partly correct the bank (dihedral effect of the main rotor and fuselage). In most helicopters, lateral stability is neutral to weakly positive in forward flight. The tail rotor contributes to yaw stability indirectly. Lateral oscillations (Dutch roll tendency) may occur in some designs and are addressed by yaw dampers in more complex aircraft.
exam_tip: Lateral stability in forward flight: neutral to weakly positive in most helicopters. Sideslip from banking creates a restoring lateral force — but it's usually weak.
key_facts: Bank disturbance creates sideslip in forward flight
Sideslip generates aerodynamic restoring lateral force
Lateral stability: neutral to weakly positive in most light helicopters
Dutch roll tendency exists in some designs
Yaw dampers address lateral-directional oscillations in complex aircraft

syllabus_desc: Explain: directional stability in the hover and in forward flight.
concept: Directional stability (in the hover): in a hover, the tail rotor provides yaw stability — any yaw disturbance increases the side force on the tail rotor in a direction that counters the yaw (if the pedals are free). In forward flight, the tail boom and vertical fin provide weathercock stability — the aircraft tends to align into the relative wind, similar to a weathervane. Directional stability improves with forward speed due to the fin and boom contribution.
exam_tip: Directional stability: tail rotor provides yaw stability in hover; fin and boom add weathercock stability in forward flight. Improves with speed.
key_facts: Hover: tail rotor provides yaw stability
Forward flight: vertical fin and tail boom add weathercock stability
Weathercock effect: aircraft tends to align into relative wind in forward flight
Directional stability improves with increased forward airspeed
Crosswinds reduce tail rotor directional effectiveness in hover

syllabus_desc: Explain how stick-held and stick-free affect longitudinal and lateral stability.
concept: Stick-held stability is generally better than stick-free stability in mechanical control systems. When controls are held fixed, the rotor and airframe aerodynamics determine stability. When controls are free, the cyclic can float to positions that reduce effective restoring moments. In powered hydraulic systems, the distinction largely disappears. Stick-held longitudinal stability in helicopters may be slightly positive (some designs) or neutral; stick-free longitudinal stability tends toward neutral or slightly negative — the helicopter requires active pilot input to maintain trim.
exam_tip: Stick-held: generally better stability (controls fixed, aerodynamics determine response). Stick-free: may reduce stability (controls float, reducing restoring moments). In hydraulic systems, the difference is minimal.
key_facts: Stick-held: better stability — controls fixed, full aerodynamic response
Stick-free: reduced stability — cyclic floats, reduces restoring moments
In mechanical systems: stick-free significantly different from stick-held
In hydraulic systems: little difference — cyclic position determined by pilot input
Certification requires adequate stability stick-held

syllabus_desc: Explain the operation and effect of the following longitudinal stability aids: stabilisers.
concept: Horizontal stabilisers (tail planes) improve longitudinal stability by providing a pitch-restoring moment when the helicopter nose pitches up or down. A nose-up pitch increases the stabiliser's angle of attack, generating a tail-down force that counteracts the pitch. The stabiliser may be fixed or, in some designs, designed to follow the changing rotor wash direction as airspeed changes. Stabilisers are more effective at higher airspeeds (greater dynamic pressure). The Robinson R22/R44 use a fixed horizontal stabiliser.
exam_tip: Horizontal stabilisers provide a pitch-restoring moment — nose up → more down force on tail → restores trim. More effective at higher speeds.
key_facts: Stabiliser: provides pitch-restoring moment in forward flight
Nose-up pitch increases stabiliser AoA → down force on tail → restores nose down
More effective at higher airspeeds (higher dynamic pressure)
May be fixed or variable geometry
Robinson R22/R44: use a fixed horizontal stabiliser

syllabus_desc: Explain the operation and effect of the following longitudinal stability aids: synchronised elevators.
concept: Synchronised elevators are elevator surfaces that move in coordination with the cyclic or collectively to improve pitch stability. As the cyclic is moved aft (nose-up command), the elevators deflect to create an additional nose-up moment — but this must be carefully designed so that the overall result is net stability improvement without creating excessive pitch authority. Some designs use the elevator to modify the pitch moment of the tail in response to airspeed changes, improving longitudinal trim across the speed range.
exam_tip: Synchronised elevators move with the cyclic to supplement pitch control authority — improving stability and reducing cyclic input required across the speed range.
key_facts: Synchronised elevators: linked to cyclic movement
Provide additional pitch moment in coordination with rotor control
Improve pitch stability and reduce cyclic stick force
Designed to improve longitudinal trim across speed range
More complex than a fixed stabiliser but more effective

syllabus_desc: Explain the operation and effect of the following longitudinal stability aids: synthetic stabilisation.
concept: Synthetic stabilisation (stability augmentation systems — SAS) uses sensors and actuators to automatically apply small control inputs that damp undesired oscillations and improve stability. A SAS detects angular rates (rate damping) or attitude deviations and makes corrections through the flight control system — faster than a pilot can react. Autopilots are an advanced form of synthetic stabilisation. SAS significantly improves handling qualities in all axes and reduces pilot workload, particularly in IFR conditions.
exam_tip: Synthetic stabilisation (SAS): sensors detect unwanted motion and actuators correct it automatically — faster than a pilot. Greatly improves stability and reduces workload.
key_facts: SAS: detects angular rates and attitude deviations via sensors
Applies automatic corrective inputs through actuators
Rate damping: primary SAS function — damps angular oscillations
More advanced: attitude hold, heading hold (approaching autopilot)
Significantly reduces pilot workload, especially in IFR

syllabus_desc: Describe common methods whereby directional stability can be enhanced.
concept: Directional stability can be enhanced through: (1) larger vertical fin area — more surface for weathercock effect; (2) longer tail boom — increases the moment arm of the vertical fin; (3) strakes or ventral fins — add additional surface area for directional stability; (4) tail rotor design — a tail rotor with higher blade area and pitch authority provides more yaw stability; (5) fenestron (fan-in-fin) designs — provide both directional stability and quieter anti-torque.
exam_tip: Directional stability enhanced by: larger/longer fin, strakes, powerful tail rotor, fenestron design. All increase the weathercock moment arm or surface area.
key_facts: Larger vertical fin: more weathercock stability
Longer tail boom: greater moment arm for fin
Strakes or ventral fins: additional directional surface area
Tail rotor design: higher authority tail rotors improve yaw stability
Fenestron (fan-in-fin): effective anti-torque and quiet directional stability

syllabus_desc: Define control power.
concept: Control power is the ability of a control input to generate a moment (force × distance) that changes the aircraft's attitude or heading. In a helicopter, control power determines how responsive the aircraft is to cyclic, collective, and pedal inputs — and how much authority the pilot has to correct disturbances. Higher control power means more responsive handling but may require higher pilot skill. Control power depends on rotor type (teetering, articulated, rigid), hinge offset, blade inertia, and moment arm.
exam_tip: Control power = ability to generate attitude-changing moments. More control power = more responsive, more authority to correct disturbances. Depends on rotor design.
key_facts: Control power: moment generated per unit of control input
Higher control power: more responsive handling
Depends on: rotor type, flapping hinge offset, blade inertia, moment arm
More control power improves ability to correct CG extremes and gusts
Different rotor designs have dramatically different control power

syllabus_desc: Explain the differences in control power between helicopters fitted with a: teetering rotor.
concept: A teetering (semi-rigid) rotor — such as on the Robinson R22/R44 — has limited control power because the blades can only tilt as a unit about the teetering hinge. Control inputs tilt the disc, which tilts the thrust vector. There is no individual blade pitch control independent of the teetering motion. Control power is moderate — sufficient for normal flight but limited at extremes of CG and for high-G manoeuvring. Teetering rotors are also susceptible to mast bumping at low G.
exam_tip: Teetering rotor: moderate control power — tilts as a unit. No individual blade pitch. Lower control power than articulated or rigid. Mast bumping risk at low G.
key_facts: Teetering rotor: two blades, teetering hinge allows disc to tilt as a unit
Control power: moderate — limited by teetering hinge geometry
No individual blade pitch control from the hinge motion
CG limits are more restrictive due to limited control power
Susceptible to mast bumping at low G

syllabus_desc: Explain the differences in control power between helicopters fitted with a: articulated rotor.
concept: An articulated rotor (multiple blades, each with flapping, lead-lag, and feathering hinges) has higher control power than a teetering rotor because each blade can be individually phased for pitch, providing a more powerful and nuanced disc tilt. The blade flapping and lead-lag motions allow for greater rotor control authority and dampening of asymmetric loads. Most medium and large commercial helicopters use fully articulated rotors. Control power is good and CG limits are wider.
exam_tip: Articulated rotor: each blade has its own hinges — higher control power, wider CG limits, better at high loads. The standard for medium/large commercial helicopters.
key_facts: Articulated rotor: individual flapping, lead-lag, and feathering hinges per blade
Higher control power than teetering — disc tilt more powerful and precise
Better handling of asymmetric loads
Wider CG limits due to greater control authority
Standard design for medium and large commercial helicopters

syllabus_desc: Explain the differences in control power between helicopters fitted with a: rigid rotor.
concept: A rigid rotor has blades that are fixed to the hub without flapping or lead-lag hinges — all blade motions occur through flexing of the blade material itself (bearingless designs). Rigid rotors have the highest control power of all rotor types because the forces from the blade tips are transmitted directly to the hub without any hinge offset energy losses. The rotor reacts very quickly to control inputs. CG limits can be wider. The trade-off is higher vibration transmission to the fuselage and higher structural loads.
exam_tip: Rigid rotor: no hinges — highest control power. Very responsive. Wider CG limits. Trade-off: higher vibration and structural loads.
key_facts: Rigid rotor: no flapping or lead-lag hinges — blades flex structurally
Highest control power of all rotor types
Very fast, direct response to control inputs
Widest CG limits due to maximum control authority
Higher vibration and structural loads transmitted to fuselage

syllabus_desc: Explain how control power can be improved through: offset flapping hinges.
concept: Offset flapping hinges (or virtual offset in bearingless designs) increase control power by creating a moment arm between the blade root and the rotor hub. When a blade flaps, the centrifugal force acts outward through the blade — if the flapping hinge is offset from the hub centre, this creates a moment that tilts the whole hub and thus the aircraft. Greater offset = more control power. This is why helicopters with large flapping hinge offsets have more authority over the airframe than those with zero offset.
exam_tip: Offset flapping hinges: the offset creates a moment arm that multiplies control power. Larger offset = more control authority. This is how rotor design creates handling quality differences.
key_facts: Flapping hinge offset from rotor centre creates a moment arm
Centrifugal force × offset distance = moment on the hub/fuselage
Larger offset = greater control power
Zero offset (e.g., teetering at hub centre) = minimum control power
Most articulated rotors use deliberate hinge offset for control power

syllabus_desc: Explain how control power can be improved through: horizontal stabilisers.
concept: Horizontal stabilisers improve control power indirectly by enhancing longitudinal stability — reducing the control inputs required to maintain trim. A stable aircraft requires less continuous control input, which allows a smaller cyclic range to be effective for manoeuvring. Stabilisers also damp phugoid oscillations, meaning the pilot uses less control power to maintain steady flight. In this way, stability aids reduce the demand on cyclic control power.
exam_tip: Stabilisers reduce the demand on cyclic control power by improving stability — less continuous correction needed, so available control range can be used for manoeuvring.
key_facts: Horizontal stabilisers improve longitudinal stability
More stable helicopter: less cyclic needed for trim maintenance
Available cyclic control range can be used for manoeuvring
Stabilisers damp phugoid — less control input needed for corrections
Indirectly improves effective control power by reducing wasted input

syllabus_desc: Describe the advantages of control power on: CG limits.
concept: Higher control power allows wider CG limits. If the helicopter can generate enough moment to overcome an extreme CG position (forward or aft), the aircraft is controllable at those extremes. A teetering rotor with low control power has narrow CG limits — the pilot runs out of cyclic authority at less extreme CG positions. A rigid rotor with high control power can tolerate wider CG limits because there is always sufficient authority to counteract the CG moment.
exam_tip: Higher control power = wider CG limits. More authority to counteract extreme CG positions. Low control power designs (teetering rotors) have narrow CG limits.
key_facts: Control power determines how extreme a CG offset can be counteracted
High control power = wider CG limits
Low control power (teetering): narrow CG limits
High control power (rigid rotor): wide CG limits
CG must remain within limits — control power sets those limits

syllabus_desc: Describe the advantages of control power on: maximum forward speed.
concept: Higher control power extends maximum forward speed capability. At high forward speeds, the retreating blade stall margin decreases and the disc loads become asymmetric — requiring more lateral cyclic input to maintain trim. A rotor with high control power can apply larger corrective inputs and resist the rolling/pitching moments from retreating blade asymmetry. Low control power designs reach the cyclic limit at lower forward speeds, limiting VNE. This is why rigid rotor helicopters generally have higher VNE than teetering rotor designs.
exam_tip: Higher control power = higher VNE. At high speed, retreating blade asymmetry demands more lateral cyclic — only possible if control power is sufficient.
key_facts: High speed creates asymmetric rotor loads — retreating blade stall tendency
Lateral cyclic needed to counteract rolling moment from disc asymmetry
High control power: can provide sufficient lateral correction at high speed
Low control power: reaches cyclic limit at lower speed — limits VNE
Rigid rotor helicopters generally have higher VNE than teetering designs

syllabus_desc: Describe the factors involved during: crosswind take-off and landing.
concept: Crosswind take-off and landing: a crosswind creates a lateral force on the fuselage and tail, affecting yaw. For a helicopter with a CCW main rotor (like the Robinson R22), a left crosswind increases the left yaw tendency and requires more right pedal; a right crosswind reduces left yaw tendency and may allow some left pedal correction. During takeoff in a crosswind, the pilot must apply more cyclic into wind to prevent drift. Landing: the pilot should approach into wind where possible; if a crosswind is unavoidable, maintain a crab angle on approach and correct to wings-level (in fixed-wing terms: disc-level) just before touchdown. Lateral drift at touchdown increases dynamic rollover risk.
exam_tip: Crosswind: lateral drift on takeoff/landing increases dynamic rollover risk. Lean into the wind with cyclic. More into-wind pedal for CCW rotors with left crosswind.
key_facts: Left crosswind (CCW rotor): requires more right pedal to counter yaw
Right crosswind: right pedal demand reduces
Apply cyclic into wind on takeoff to prevent drift
Lateral drift at touchdown: dynamic rollover risk — avoid any sideways skid contact
Land into wind where possible — use running landing in strong crosswinds

syllabus_desc: Describe the factors involved during: downwind take-off and landing.
concept: Downwind take-off and landing: a downwind departure means the helicopter must accelerate against its own groundspeed — it takes longer to gain translational lift and the ground roll distance (for running takeoffs) is longer. The height-velocity diagram's avoid curve is more critical downwind — less effective translational lift margin exists. Downwind landings are more demanding: groundspeed at any approach airspeed is higher, making flare and touchdown more critical. Whenever possible, take off and land into wind.
exam_tip: Downwind operations: longer to gain translational lift on takeoff, higher groundspeed on approach, and more critical flare. Avoid where possible.
key_facts: Downwind takeoff: translational lift gained at higher groundspeed — more ground run
H/V diagram avoid curve still applies — can't ignore it downwind
Downwind landing: higher groundspeed at touch-down — more precise flare needed
Flare must control both airspeed and groundspeed — wind is now additive
Always take off and land into wind where operationally possible

syllabus_desc: Describe the factors involved during: running take-off and landing.
concept: Running take-off and landing: used when power is insufficient for a hover-in, hover-out landing. A running takeoff involves accelerating along the ground until translational lift allows liftoff without hovering. A running landing maintains forward speed through touchdown to keep translational lift assisting the rotor until contact. Both require a suitable firm, clear, and flat surface. Running landings are preferred at maximum AUW, high DA, or when power margins for hovering are marginal.
exam_tip: Running takeoff/landing: use when power is insufficient to hover. Maintains translational lift through the critical phase. Requires clear, firm surface.
key_facts: Running takeoff: accelerate on ground until translational lift allows flight
Running landing: maintain airspeed through touchdown — translational lift assists
Required at high AUW, high DA, or marginal power conditions
Requires a long, firm, clear, relatively flat surface
Not suitable for soft ground, rough terrain, or confined areas

syllabus_desc: Describe the factors involved during: cushion creep take-off.
concept: Cushion creep take-off: used when power is marginal for a clear OGE departure. The helicopter is hovered in ground effect (IGE), where rotor efficiency is higher, and then progressively moved forward (creeping) until translational lift allows climbing out of ground effect. The technique uses the ground cushion to reduce power demand while accelerating to a speed where translational lift supplements rotor efficiency. Requires careful area assessment — obstacles must not be encountered during the creep.
exam_tip: Cushion creep: hover IGE and creep forward to gain translational lift before climbing OGE. Used when power is marginal for a direct OGE departure.
key_facts: Cushion creep: hover IGE, creep forward to gain translational lift
Ground effect reduces power required during the creep phase
Once translational lift achieved, climbing power becomes available
Requires a clear path for the creep — no obstacles in the creep direction
Used at max AUW, high DA, or marginal power situations

syllabus_desc: Describe the factors involved during: confined area ("towering") take-off.
concept: Confined area (towering) take-off: used to depart from a confined area surrounded by obstacles. The helicopter climbs vertically (or near-vertically) to obstacle height, then transitions to forward flight. This requires sufficient power for a sustained vertical or near-vertical climb — OGE power must be available. The H/V diagram may be entered during this manoeuvre — the pilot must plan a safe abort point. Wind direction and confined area size dictate the optimal technique.
exam_tip: Confined area towering departure: climb vertically to obstacle height then convert to forward flight. Requires OGE power and a pre-planned abort point. You will pass through the H/V avoid curve.
key_facts: Vertical or near-vertical climb to obstacle height
Requires OGE hover power as a minimum
Passes through the H/V avoid curve — plan the abort point
Wind assessment critical — into-wind departure preferred
Committed point: beyond this, a safe abort is no longer possible

syllabus_desc: Describe the factors involved during: maximum performance take-off.
concept: Maximum performance take-off: uses the maximum available power to achieve the greatest obstacle clearance for the distance available. Combines the running or cushion creep technique with maximum power application to maximise the climb gradient. The pilot uses the best angle of climb airspeed (Vx) once translational lift is achieved. Requires pre-departure power check to confirm power available matches AFM performance chart values.
exam_tip: Maximum performance takeoff: max power + best angle airspeed (Vx) after translational lift = maximum obstacle clearance angle. Power check mandatory beforehand.
key_facts: Apply maximum available power from the start
Use cushion creep or running technique to gain translational lift
Transition to Vx (best angle of climb) once airborne
Maximises height gained per unit of horizontal distance
Power check mandatory — performance is only valid if engine meets AFM reference

syllabus_desc: Describe the factors involved during: zero speed landing.
concept: Zero speed landing: a landing made with zero groundspeed and zero airspeed at touchdown — a pure vertical descent to the surface. This is the standard confined area landing technique. It requires sufficient power to hover OGE at the landing point height and then descend vertically. The approach is flown to a hover above the landing spot and then the helicopter descends vertically. High power demand makes this technique most critical at maximum AUW and high DA.
exam_tip: Zero speed landing: arrive at a hover then descend vertically — requires OGE hover power at the landing site altitude.
key_facts: Land at zero groundspeed and zero airspeed
Arrive at a hover above the landing point
Descend vertically into the landing area
Requires OGE hover power at the landing site altitude
High DA + high AUW: may require a power check before committing

syllabus_desc: Describe the factors involved during: take-off and landing on sloping ground.
concept: Take-off and landing on sloping ground: the primary hazard is dynamic rollover. On a slope, the uphill skid contacts the ground first, creating a pivot point. As the helicopter is lifted, the downhill side must support the weight while the uphill side is on the slope — cyclic must be applied up-slope to counter the roll tendency. The critical technique is: apply cyclic into the slope before lifting, and lift slowly and smoothly. For landing on a slope, land on the uphill skid first, then lower the downhill skid. Apply rotor brake after full touchdown.
exam_tip: Sloped landing: land uphill skid first, apply cyclic into slope before lifting off. Dynamic rollover is the primary risk — never allow downslope drift.
key_facts: Land uphill skid first on sloped ground
Apply cyclic into the slope to counter roll tendency
Lift off: apply up-slope cyclic before collective — prevent rollover during liftoff
Maximum slope angle limit in AFM (typically 5–8°)
Dynamic rollover risk increases with slope angle and lateral CG offset

syllabus_desc: With respect to external (sling) load operations, describe: consequences of, and precautions for, cable/strop snag during take-off.
concept: Cable or strop snag during external load takeoff: if the load cable snags on an obstacle during liftoff, the helicopter is suddenly restricted at one corner — the same as catching a skid on the ground. Dynamic rollover can occur very rapidly. Precautions: pre-departure inspection of the area to ensure the cable path is clear; use a loadmaster to check cable as the helicopter lifts; always release the load immediately using the emergency cargo jettison if a snag is detected. The pilot must have a clear abort point and jettison authority.
exam_tip: Cable snag during liftoff = dynamic rollover risk. Pre-flight check the cable path; use loadmaster; have immediate cargo jettison available. Act before the angle becomes critical.
key_facts: Snag can cause dynamic rollover — same as catching a skid on the ground
Pre-flight: inspect the complete cable path to ensure it is clear of obstacles
Use a ground handler (loadmaster) to monitor the load during liftoff
Emergency cargo jettison: must be immediately accessible to the pilot
Jettison the load immediately if a snag is detected — don't hesitate

syllabus_desc: With respect to external (sling) load operations, describe: effect on Vne.
concept: VNE reduction with slingloads: external loads create additional parasite drag and can cause load oscillation, which may couple with the helicopter's natural frequencies. To prevent structural damage and maintain control, AFMs specify a reduced VNE for external load operations. The reduction depends on the type, size, and pendulum behaviour of the load. Some loads (particularly large or aerodynamically unstable loads) require very significant speed reduction. Always check the AFM for the applicable VNE with the specific load configuration.
exam_tip: VNE is reduced with slingloads — parasite drag and load oscillation limit safe airspeed. Check AFM for the specific VNE reduction for your load type.
key_facts: Slingloads increase parasite drag — raises power required at all speeds
Load oscillation can couple with helicopter natural frequencies
VNE reduced to prevent structural coupling and control issues
AFM specifies VNE for external load operations
Some loads require very significant VNE reduction

syllabus_desc: With respect to external (sling) load operations, describe: actions to take in case of helicopter oscillation.
concept: Helicopter oscillation with slingloads: if the helicopter begins oscillating (pilot-induced oscillation or load-induced resonance), the pilot should release the slingload immediately using the emergency cargo jettison if the oscillation is uncontrollable. For milder oscillation, the technique is to slow down (reduce speed reduces aerodynamic coupling), avoid control inputs that match the oscillation frequency, and fly at a speed below the resonant onset speed. External load oscillation is more common at higher speeds and with aerodynamically unstable loads.
exam_tip: If helicopter begins oscillating with a slingload: slow down and use smooth control inputs. If oscillation is uncontrollable: jettison the load immediately.
key_facts: Reduce speed: oscillation is usually speed-dependent
Avoid control inputs that match the oscillation frequency
Smooth, gentle control — avoid over-correcting
If uncontrollable: jettison the load using emergency release
Aerodynamically unstable loads (flat objects) are most susceptible

syllabus_desc: Explain the effect of altitude on a helicopter's performance.
concept: Altitude (density altitude) reduces helicopter performance by: reducing engine power output (less air mass per combustion cycle), reducing rotor efficiency (blades generate less lift per unit collective in thinner air), requiring more collective pitch for any given lift requirement, reducing the margin between power required and power available. All performance figures — hover ceiling, rate of climb, VNE, range, endurance — are affected. Performance planning must use density altitude, not field elevation alone.
exam_tip: Altitude reduces EVERYTHING: engine power down, rotor efficiency down, power margins shrink. Performance planning must use density altitude.
key_facts: Engine power output decreases with altitude (less air mass)
Rotor generates less lift per unit collective in thinner air
Power required increases at altitude (more collective needed)
All performance margins shrink with increasing altitude
Must use density altitude for performance planning — not field elevation

syllabus_desc: Given the elevation and QNH of a location, calculate the pressure altitude.
concept: Pressure altitude is calculated from QNH. If QNH is below 1013 hPa, the pressure altitude is above the elevation (altitude reads high on a standard set altimeter). If QNH is above 1013 hPa, pressure altitude is below the elevation. Calculation: Pressure Altitude = Elevation + (1013 - QNH) × 30 ft per hPa (approximately). For example, an aerodrome at 500 ft with QNH of 1003 hPa: pressure altitude = 500 + (1013 - 1003) × 30 = 500 + 300 = 800 ft.
exam_tip: Pressure altitude formula: PA = elevation + (1013 - QNH) × 30 ft. Low QNH = PA above elevation. Know this calculation — it's often in the exam.
key_facts: Pressure altitude: what the altimeter reads with 1013 set
Formula: PA = elevation + (1013 − QNH) × 30 ft
QNH below 1013: PA above field elevation
QNH above 1013: PA below field elevation
Example: 500 ft elevation, QNH 1003 → PA = 500 + (10 × 30) = 800 ft

syllabus_desc: Given a standard altimeter, determine the pressure altitude.
concept: To determine pressure altitude using a standard altimeter: set the sub-scale to 1013 hPa (or 29.92 in Hg). The altimeter reading then displays pressure altitude directly. This is the standard method for determining pressure altitude without a calculation. Aircraft performance charts are referenced to pressure altitude — so setting 1013 and reading the altimeter gives you the value to use in the chart. The altimeter must be serviceable and calibrated.
exam_tip: Set altimeter sub-scale to 1013 hPa → read pressure altitude directly. This is the practical method — no maths needed if you have a serviceable altimeter.
key_facts: Set altimeter sub-scale to 1013 hPa (standard pressure)
Altimeter reading = pressure altitude
Used for performance chart entry
Must have a serviceable, calibrated altimeter
Most accurate method for determining pressure altitude on the ground

syllabus_desc: Given the pressure altitude and air temperature of a location, calculate the density altitude.
concept: Density altitude is calculated from pressure altitude and OAT. Formula: Density Altitude ≈ Pressure Altitude + 120 × (OAT - ISA temperature at that pressure altitude). ISA temperature at any altitude = 15°C - (2°C × altitude in thousands of feet). For example: PA = 2,000 ft, OAT = 25°C. ISA temp at 2,000 ft = 15 - 4 = 11°C. Deviation = 25 - 11 = +14°C. DA ≈ 2,000 + (120 × 14) = 2,000 + 1,680 = 3,680 ft. Many flight computers have a DA calculation function.
exam_tip: Density altitude formula: DA ≈ PA + 120 × (OAT − ISA temp at PA). Know the formula and how to apply it — common exam calculation.
key_facts: DA formula: DA ≈ PA + 120 × (OAT − ISA temperature at that altitude)
ISA temp at altitude = 15°C − 2°C per 1,000 ft
Positive temperature deviation increases DA above PA
Negative temperature deviation decreases DA below PA
Example: PA 2,000 ft, OAT 25°C → ISA 11°C → DA ≈ 2,000 + 120×14 = 3,680 ft

syllabus_desc: Given the elevation of a location, air temperature deviation from ISA, and QNH, calculate the ambient temperature.
concept: To calculate ambient temperature given elevation, ISA deviation, and QNH: (1) calculate pressure altitude from elevation and QNH (PA = elevation + (1013 - QNH) × 30); (2) calculate ISA temperature at that pressure altitude (ISA temp = 15°C - 2°C per 1,000 ft of PA); (3) add the ISA deviation to ISA temperature to find OAT (OAT = ISA temp + ISA deviation). This allows density altitude to then be calculated. In practice, OAT is measured directly — but the calculation is required for exam purposes.
exam_tip: OAT calculation: find PA → find ISA temp at PA → add ISA deviation. Then you have OAT for density altitude calculation.
key_facts: Step 1: PA = elevation + (1013 − QNH) × 30 ft
Step 2: ISA temp at PA = 15°C − (2°C × PA in thousands)
Step 3: OAT = ISA temp + ISA deviation (e.g., ISA +15 means add 15°C)
ISA deviation is given as a temperature in degrees above or below ISA
Once OAT is known, DA can be calculated

syllabus_desc: Explain the effect of a change in air temperature on the power required to hover IGE.
concept: An increase in air temperature reduces air density (hot air is less dense). This increases density altitude, which means: the rotor must generate more induced velocity (spin faster or increase collective) to produce the same lift. Power required to hover IGE therefore increases with temperature. On a hot summer day, a helicopter that hovers comfortably in winter may be marginal or unable to hover at the same aerodrome at the same weight. IGE hover performance is directly and significantly affected by temperature.
exam_tip: Hotter air = higher DA = more power required to hover IGE. Same aircraft, same aerodrome, same weight: hotter day = less performance margin.
key_facts: Higher temperature reduces air density → increases density altitude
More power required to hover IGE as temperature rises
IGE hover ceiling decreases on hot days
The effect is significant — 30°C vs 15°C may require 10–15% more power
Performance must be re-evaluated when temperature is significantly above ISA

syllabus_desc: Explain why Vne varies with altitude.
concept: VNE varies with altitude because retreating blade stall determines the upper speed limit. At higher altitude, the rotor must spin faster (higher RRPM) or operate at higher collective to generate the same thrust in thinner air — either way, the retreating blade is already at a higher pitch. Less airspeed margin exists before retreating blade stall is reached. VNE therefore decreases with altitude. The AFM provides a VNE versus altitude or density altitude chart that must be consulted for high-altitude operations.
exam_tip: VNE decreases with altitude because the retreating blade is already working harder in thin air — less speed margin before retreating blade stall. Check the AFM VNE chart.
key_facts: Retreating blade stall limits maximum forward airspeed
At high altitude: rotor needs higher collective for same thrust
Retreating blade already at higher AoA at altitude → less margin to RBS
VNE decreases with altitude (DA)
AFM VNE vs altitude chart must be consulted for high-altitude operations

syllabus_desc: Explain the effect of humidity on a helicopter's performance.
concept: Humidity (water vapour content of the air) reduces air density because water vapour (molecular weight 18) is lighter than dry air (average molecular weight approximately 29). Moist air is less dense than dry air at the same temperature and pressure. This increases effective density altitude. The effect of humidity is relatively small — perhaps 200–300 ft of density altitude in very humid conditions — but it is additive to temperature and altitude effects. NZ coastal operations in summer can involve high humidity.
exam_tip: Humidity reduces air density (water vapour is lighter than dry air), increasing effective density altitude. The effect is small but additive — worth accounting for in marginal conditions.
key_facts: Water vapour lighter than dry air → humid air is less dense
Humidity increases effective density altitude
Effect: approximately 200–300 ft DA increase in very humid conditions
Additive to temperature and pressure altitude effects
Most relevant in hot, humid coastal operations

syllabus_desc: Describe the effect of a change in humidity on the density altitude of a location.
concept: Increased humidity raises the density altitude of a location because humid air is less dense than dry air at the same pressure and temperature. When humidity increases: the effective mass of air per cubic metre decreases; this lowers the density; density altitude rises. The calculation of density altitude from pressure altitude and temperature assumes dry air — in highly humid conditions, actual density altitude is slightly higher than the dry air formula suggests. For critical performance calculations in humid conditions, use the full psychrometric calculation or consult a humidity-corrected chart.
exam_tip: Higher humidity = higher effective density altitude (less dense air). It's additive — accounts for a couple of hundred feet in extreme humidity.
key_facts: Higher humidity reduces air density
Effective DA increases with humidity
Standard DA formula assumes dry air — humidity adds to the calculated value
Correction is small but relevant in marginal power situations
NZ coastal areas can have high relative humidity in summer

syllabus_desc: Explain the effect of the following on a helicopter's performance: aircraft gross weight.
concept: Aircraft gross weight directly affects performance in all regimes. Higher weight requires more rotor thrust, more collective, more power, higher induced velocity — the entire power required curve shifts upward. Hover ceiling (both IGE and OGE) decreases with weight. Rate of climb decreases. VNE may decrease (retreating blade stall margin reduces). Range and endurance both decrease. Gross weight is the most directly controllable performance factor — fuel load, passengers, and freight can all be managed.
exam_tip: Weight is the most controllable performance factor — every extra kilogram increases power required and reduces all performance margins. Control your weight carefully.
key_facts: Higher gross weight raises power required at all flight regimes
Hover ceiling (IGE and OGE) decreases with weight
Rate of climb and climb ceiling decrease
Range and endurance both reduce with higher AUW
Weight is directly controllable — fuel, pax, and freight management critical

syllabus_desc: Explain the effect of the following on a helicopter's performance: external stores.
concept: External stores (slingloads, camera mounts, floats, agricultural spray bars) add both weight and parasite drag. The weight component raises induced power required across all flight regimes. The drag component raises parasite power at cruise speeds. The combined effect reduces: hover ceiling, climb performance, VNE (oscillation and drag considerations), range, and endurance. Performance planning with external stores must use the AFM data for the specific equipment fitted, not the clean aircraft values.
exam_tip: External stores add weight AND drag — both hurt performance. Use the specific configuration AFM data, not clean aircraft figures.
key_facts: Weight addition: raises induced power required
Drag addition: raises parasite power at cruise speeds
Combined effect: lower hover ceiling, worse climb, lower VNE, less range
Can also cause load oscillation — pilot technique must account for this
AFM must provide performance data for the specific external equipment

syllabus_desc: Explain the effect of the following on a helicopter's performance: the wind.
concept: Wind effects on helicopter performance: a headwind reduces groundspeed — improving range and angle of climb over the ground. At the same airspeed, a headwind reduces hover fuel flow slightly by providing some translational lift. For hover and low-speed work, a headwind improves performance by providing some translational lift effect (power required reduces slightly above calm-air hover). A tailwind increases groundspeed, reducing range per litre and worsening the ground angle of climb. Strong crosswinds affect tail rotor authority.
exam_tip: Wind helps performance: headwind reduces power required for hover/slow flight (translational lift) and improves range and climb angle over ground. Tailwind hurts.
key_facts: Headwind: reduces power required for hover by providing translational lift
Headwind: improves range (nm per litre) and ground angle of climb
Tailwind: increases groundspeed — worsens range and ground climb angle
Strong crosswind: increases tail rotor power demand (CCW rotor with left crosswind)
Performance data in AFM is based on zero wind — apply wind corrections manually

syllabus_desc: Explain the effect of anti-torque application on rotor RPM.
concept: Anti-torque (tail rotor) application affects main rotor RPM through the power sharing with the engine. When more left pedal is applied (more tail rotor thrust — CCW main rotor), the tail rotor pitch increases, demanding more power from the engine. If this additional power demand cannot be met instantly by the governor, main rotor RPM may droop momentarily. The engine governor compensates, but there is a lag. In critical situations (already near max power), a sudden demand for increased anti-torque can cause a rotor RPM transient.
exam_tip: More tail rotor pitch = more engine power demanded = potential RRPM droop if governor lags or maximum power is nearly reached.
key_facts: Tail rotor pitch increase requires more power from the engine
Power extraction: engine power split between main rotor and tail rotor
Governor compensates, but with a small lag
At near-maximum power: anti-torque demand can cause RRPM droop
Monitor RRPM when making large pedal inputs in demanding conditions

syllabus_desc: Describe the effect on autorotation of ice accumulation on the rotor blades.
concept: Ice accumulation on rotor blades disrupts the blade aerofoil profile. Ice adds weight (increasing AUW and lowering hover ceiling), changes the blade's aerodynamic shape (disrupting smooth airflow and increasing drag), and may change the blade's mass distribution (affecting balance and vibration). In autorotation, iced blades have higher drag and reduced autorotative efficiency — RRPM may be harder to maintain and the landing flare will be less effective. Ice accumulation on rotor blades is an airworthiness emergency.
exam_tip: Rotor blade icing: adds weight, disrupts aerofoil shape (more drag, less lift), and degrades autorotative performance. Any icing of the rotor is an emergency — land immediately.
key_facts: Ice adds weight → increases effective AUW → lower hover ceiling
Ice disrupts aerofoil profile → reduced lift, increased drag
Autorotation: higher blade drag → RRPM harder to maintain
Landing flare effectiveness reduced — more energy lost to drag
Rotor blade icing is an airworthiness emergency — land immediately

syllabus_desc: Derive performance planning information from horsepower required and horsepower available curves, and from graphs published in piston engine and turbine engine helicopter Flight Manuals, with emphasis on: hover ceiling.
concept: Hover ceiling graphs in the AFM show the maximum altitude (pressure or density altitude) at which the helicopter can maintain a hover — either IGE or OGE — at a given AUW. To read the chart: enter from the AUW axis, move horizontally to the OAT curve, then read the hover ceiling from the altitude axis. If the field density altitude exceeds the hover ceiling at your AUW, the helicopter cannot hover OGE (or IGE). The OGE ceiling is always lower than the IGE ceiling.
exam_tip: Hover ceiling graph: enter with AUW → trace to OAT curve → read DA ceiling. If field DA > chart value, you can't hover at that weight. OGE ceiling < IGE ceiling always.
key_facts: Enter chart with AUW on weight axis
Trace horizontally to the appropriate OAT curve
Read the hover ceiling (density or pressure altitude)
If field DA exceeds chart value: hover is not possible at that AUW
Always use OGE ceiling for operations where ground effect is not available

syllabus_desc: Derive performance planning information from horsepower required and horsepower available curves, and from graphs published in piston engine and turbine engine helicopter Flight Manuals, with emphasis on: take-off distance required over a 50ft obstacle.
concept: Take-off distance to clear a 50 ft obstacle is found in the performance section of the AFM. Charts typically require inputs of: AUW, pressure altitude, and OAT (or density altitude). The result is the ground distance required from brake release (or initial lift) to clear a screen height of 50 ft AGL. At high DA or high AUW, this distance increases significantly. If the available runway or clear area is shorter than the required distance, the take-off is not permissible at that configuration.
exam_tip: Takeoff distance to clear 50 ft: enter AFM chart with AUW, pressure altitude, and OAT → read required distance. If available distance is less, don't go.
key_facts: Inputs: AUW, pressure altitude, and OAT (or density altitude)
Output: ground distance to clear 50 ft AGL screen height
Distance increases significantly at high DA or high AUW
Compare chart result to available clear area before departure
Exceeding the required distance: take-off is not permitted

syllabus_desc: Derive performance planning information from horsepower required and horsepower available curves, and from graphs published in piston engine and turbine engine helicopter Flight Manuals, with emphasis on: hover IGE and/or OGE, at various all-up weights.
concept: IGE and OGE hover charts show the maximum AUW for hovering at various density altitudes and temperatures. For OGE hover: find the intersection of density altitude and OAT on the chart — the reading gives maximum AUW. For IGE hover: same process but a different chart (IGE is always more favourable — higher AUW or higher ceiling). Pilots must use OGE charts when planning operations where they cannot maintain ground effect (e.g., slope operations, confined area OGE approach).
exam_tip: IGE/OGE charts: maximum AUW for hovering at the planned density altitude and OAT. Always use OGE values for OGE operations — don't use the easier IGE figures.
key_facts: IGE chart: maximum AUW for hovering within ground effect
OGE chart: maximum AUW for hovering outside ground effect
OGE limits are always more restrictive than IGE
Enter chart with density altitude and OAT → read maximum AUW
Use OGE for any operation where ground effect cannot be maintained

syllabus_desc: Derive performance planning information from horsepower required and horsepower available curves, and from graphs published in piston engine and turbine engine helicopter Flight Manuals, with emphasis on: gross weight for hovering.
concept: Maximum gross weight for hovering is read directly from the IGE or OGE hover charts. It gives the maximum AUW at which the helicopter can sustain a hover at the planned density altitude and temperature. If the intended AUW exceeds this figure, the helicopter cannot hover at that location. This value must be compared with the actual AUW (including passengers, fuel, and freight) before any operation where hovering is required.
exam_tip: Max gross weight for hovering: read from hover chart at your DA and OAT. Your actual AUW must not exceed this figure for the operation.
key_facts: Read maximum hover weight from IGE or OGE chart for planned conditions
Compare to actual AUW (weight including all fuel, occupants, cargo)
If actual AUW > maximum hover weight: operation not permitted at that location
Key pre-flight check for confined area, sling load, or OGE operations
Temperature and altitude both affect the chart values significantly

syllabus_desc: Derive performance planning information from horsepower required and horsepower available curves, and from graphs published in piston engine and turbine engine helicopter Flight Manuals, with emphasis on: turbine engine power.
concept: Turbine engine power charts typically express power as torque (percent or lb-ft), gas generator speed (Ng %), or shaft horsepower, plotted against outside air temperature and pressure altitude. The chart allows the pilot to determine the power available from the engine at the planned conditions, and compare it against the power required (from the PR chart) to confirm adequate margins. Turbine power available decreases with increasing OAT and increasing altitude (above the engine's flat-rated altitude).
exam_tip: Turbine power charts: read available torque/power from OAT and pressure altitude. Compare to power required. Any margin is for climb and performance.
key_facts: Turbine power expressed as torque (%) or shaft horsepower
Inputs: OAT and pressure altitude
Output: maximum available power in the planned conditions
Turbine power decreases with OAT above flat-rating temperature
Compare PA to PR to determine excess power and margins

syllabus_desc: Derive performance planning information from horsepower required and horsepower available curves, and from graphs published in piston engine and turbine engine helicopter Flight Manuals, with emphasis on: climb performance.
concept: Climb performance charts allow the pilot to determine rate of climb and/or angle of climb at a given AUW, density altitude, and airspeed. They show how climb performance degrades with altitude and weight. The chart may also specify the best rate or best angle airspeed for maximum performance. These charts are essential for obstacle clearance planning and for determining whether a departure from a high-altitude, high-temperature aerodrome is feasible.
exam_tip: Climb performance chart: enter with AUW, DA, and airspeed → read rate of climb or angle of climb. Essential for obstacle clearance and high-DA departures.
key_facts: Inputs: AUW, density altitude, and airspeed (usually at Vy)
Output: rate of climb in fpm or climb gradient
Climb performance degrades with weight and altitude
May specify best rate (Vy) and best angle (Vx) airspeeds
Used for obstacle clearance planning on departure

syllabus_desc: Derive performance planning information from horsepower required and horsepower available curves, and from graphs published in piston engine and turbine engine helicopter Flight Manuals, with emphasis on: range and endurance.
concept: Range and endurance charts provide the maximum range or endurance achievable from a given fuel load at a specified AUW, altitude, and power setting. They account for fuel burn at the best range or best endurance airspeed. These charts are essential for fuel planning — especially for remote area operations in NZ where refuelling options may be limited. The charts typically show still-air range; wind corrections must be applied separately.
exam_tip: Range/endurance charts: read from AUW and altitude → range or endurance from a given fuel load. Still-air values — apply wind correction manually.
key_facts: Inputs: AUW, fuel load, altitude, and power setting
Output: range (nm) or endurance (hours)
Still-air values only — apply headwind/tailwind corrections
Essential for remote area and cross-country fuel planning
Best range and best endurance settings should be clearly identified in AFM

syllabus_desc: Given a typical height/velocity diagram: derive, for a given airspeed, the band of heights from which a forced landing is not possible following an engine failure.
concept: From the H/V diagram, for any given airspeed on the X-axis, there is a band of heights on the Y-axis from which a safe autorotative landing cannot be made following an engine failure. This is the 'avoid band' — the unsafe height range at that airspeed. Below the lower boundary of the avoid band at that airspeed: safe (enough altitude to enter autorotation and flare). Above the upper boundary: safe (enough altitude and speed). Between the boundaries: unsafe — the diagram shows the specific heights to avoid for each airspeed.
exam_tip: For any airspeed on the H/V diagram, there is an unsafe height band. Above and below the band are safe; within the band is the avoid zone. Know how to read these boundaries.
key_facts: For each airspeed on X-axis: an unsafe height band is shown
Below the lower boundary: safe (sufficient altitude for autorotation)
Above the upper boundary: safe (altitude clears the high-speed knee)
Within the band: unsafe — engine failure not survivable by autorotation
Read the boundaries for any given airspeed directly from the diagram

syllabus_desc: Given a typical height/velocity diagram: describe the effect of a change in air temperature on the range of speeds for safe level flight.
concept: An increase in air temperature (or any increase in density altitude) shifts the H/V diagram's avoid curve to encompass a larger range of speeds — the unsafe region grows. At higher DA: the engine produces less power (failure is more critical), the rotor generates less thrust in thin air, and autorotation performance degrades. To compensate, higher airspeeds may be needed to have sufficient margin for successful autorotation. The avoid curve expands — the safe operating corridor narrows at high density altitude.
exam_tip: High temperature = higher DA = H/V avoid curve expands. More speeds and heights become unsafe. Assess your H/V diagram for the actual DA conditions.
key_facts: Higher temperature increases DA
DA increase shifts H/V avoid curve: unsafe region expands
More height needed for safe autorotation in hot/high conditions
Higher airspeed needed to have sufficient energy for flare in thin air
Always use a DA-corrected H/V diagram — not the sea-level standard chart

syllabus_desc: Explain the effects of the following on the size and shape of the avoid area of a height/velocity diagram: gross weight.
concept: Higher gross weight expands the H/V avoid area. A heavier helicopter descends faster in autorotation (higher disc loading) and needs more energy to flare. More altitude is required for a safe autorotation and landing. This means the lower boundary of the avoid area rises (more altitude needed) and the overall unsafe area grows. AFMs typically provide H/V diagrams at maximum AUW — at lower weights, the avoid area is smaller and performance is better.
exam_tip: Higher gross weight: larger H/V avoid area. Descends faster in autorotation, needs more altitude to flare. AFM H/V is usually at max AUW — lighter aircraft is safer.
key_facts: Higher AUW: higher autorotative descent rate
More altitude needed for safe autorotation and flare
Lower boundary of avoid area rises with AUW
Overall unsafe avoid area expands
AFM H/V at max AUW — lighter weights give more margin

syllabus_desc: Explain the effects of the following on the size and shape of the avoid area of a height/velocity diagram: altitude.
concept: Higher altitude (density altitude) expands the H/V avoid area. At altitude, the rotor is less efficient, autorotative descent rate may be slightly higher, and flare effectiveness is reduced. Engine failure at altitude is also more severe in its impact on rotor RPM decay timing. The safe operation corridor narrows — more height and airspeed are needed to guarantee a safe autorotation. Always apply altitude corrections to the H/V diagram for high-altitude operations.
exam_tip: High altitude: H/V avoid area expands. Less efficient rotor, weaker flare, faster RPM decay. Need more height and airspeed for safe autorotation.
key_facts: Higher DA reduces rotor efficiency
Autorotative flare effectiveness decreases at altitude
RRPM decay after engine failure may be faster at altitude (less air mass)
Lower boundary of avoid area rises — more altitude needed
Apply DA correction to H/V diagram for high-altitude operations

syllabus_desc: Explain the effects of the following on the size and shape of the avoid area of a height/velocity diagram: density altitude.
concept: Density altitude combines temperature, altitude, and humidity effects. High density altitude is the most critical combined factor affecting the H/V diagram. At high DA, the engine produces less power (RPM decay faster), rotor efficiency is lower (autorotation less efficient), and flare effectiveness is reduced. The H/V avoid area expands significantly. Some AFMs provide DA-corrected H/V diagrams; where not available, the pilot must conservatively expand the margins for high DA operations.
exam_tip: Density altitude is the combined killer for H/V performance — all three effects (engine, rotor, flare) worsen at high DA. Use DA-corrected H/V data or add conservative margins.
key_facts: High DA: engine power available reduces (faster RPM decay after failure)
High DA: rotor efficiency reduces (less thrust per collective)
High DA: flare effectiveness reduces (less lift per unit RRPM)
H/V avoid area expands significantly at high DA
Use DA-corrected data; if unavailable, add conservative safety margins

syllabus_desc: Explain the effects of the following on the size and shape of the avoid area of a height/velocity diagram: parasite drag.
concept: Increased parasite drag (from external equipment, slingloads, or non-streamlined airframe) increases the autorotative descent rate — more drag means faster descent. A faster descent in autorotation means more altitude is needed to complete a safe flare and landing. The H/V avoid area lower boundary rises (more height needed to compensate for faster descent). Additionally, high drag limits the maximum forward airspeed, shifting the high-speed knee of the H/V diagram to a lower speed.
exam_tip: Higher parasite drag: faster autorotative descent → lower boundary of H/V rises. Also reduces maximum safe speed (lower VNE for external loads).
key_facts: Parasite drag increases autorotative descent rate
Faster descent: more altitude needed for safe flare
Lower boundary of H/V avoid area rises
High speed knee shifts lower (VNE reduces with external loads)
External loads: use load-specific H/V diagram from AFM

syllabus_desc: Explain the effects of the following on the size and shape of the avoid area of a height/velocity diagram: carriage of a sling load.
concept: Carrying a slingload expands the H/V avoid area due to: increased AUW (heavier → faster descent, lower ceiling), increased parasite drag (more descent rate, lower VNE), possible load oscillation (reduces pilot ability to control precisely in autorotation), and load pendulum dynamics (during flare, the load may swing and affect the timing of the energy transfer). If the AFM provides a slingload H/V diagram, it must be used. Slingload H/V avoid areas are significantly larger than the clean helicopter diagram.
exam_tip: Slingload expands H/V avoid area from all angles: more weight, more drag, lower VNE, pendulum effects. Use the slingload-specific H/V from the AFM.
key_facts: Slingload adds AUW → heavier autorotation → faster descent
Slingload adds drag → further increases descent rate
VNE reduced → high-speed knee shifts to lower speed
Load pendulum during flare can affect energy exchange timing
Use slingload-specific H/V diagram from AFM — avoid area is larger

syllabus_desc: Derive performance planning information from a graph showing the relationship between airspeed, altitude and Vne at various RPM settings.
concept: A VNE versus altitude and RPM graph allows the pilot to determine the maximum allowable airspeed for a given combination of altitude (density altitude or pressure altitude) and RRPM. At higher altitudes, VNE decreases because retreating blade stall occurs at lower speeds (retreating blade already at higher AoA). At lower RRPM, VNE also decreases because the advancing/retreating blade velocity differential is reduced and stall margin shrinks. The pilot reads the graph: enter with altitude and RRPM → read VNE. Never exceed this value.
exam_tip: VNE vs altitude and RPM graph: enter with your altitude and RRPM → read VNE. VNE decreases with altitude (RBS closer) and with low RRPM (less stall margin). Never exceed.
key_facts: VNE decreases with altitude — retreating blade stall margin reduces
VNE decreases with low RRPM — less velocity differential between advancing and retreating blades
Enter chart with density altitude and RRPM
Read VNE from the chart — this is the hard limit for that combination
Exceeding VNE at high altitude with low RRPM is extremely hazardous
